topics.title: Data acquisition using Multimeter and oscillographic recorder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Data acquisition using Multimeter and oscillographic recorder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Data Acquisition (DAQ)?

**Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the process of sampling real-world physical phenomena (such as voltage, current, temperature, pressure, or sound) and converting them into a digital format that can be stored, displayed, and analyzed by a computer or an instrument. In the context of sensors and devices, DAQ acts as the critical bridge between the **analog physical world** and the **digital processing world**.

Formally, DAQ is defined as the systematic measurement and recording of electrical or physical parameters from sensors, transducers, or test points using specialized instruments such as **Digital Multimeters (DMM)** and **Oscillographic Recorders**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A sensor alone does not "acquire" data — it merely converts one form of energy to another (e.g., RTD → resistance). Data acquisition requires the *measurement instrument* to read, process, and display the signal. This is the central theme of Module Experiment 2.

### 1.2 The Multimeter (DMM) — Definition

A **Digital Multimeter (DMM)** is a versatile electronic measuring instrument that combines several measurement functions in one unit. The standard functions are:

- **Voltage** (DC and AC) — measured in **Volts (V)**
- **Current** (DC and AC) — measured in **Amperes (A)**
- **Resistance** — measured in **Ohms (Ω)**
- **Continuity and Diode Test**
- **Frequency and Capacitance** (in modern DMMs)

A DMM displays the measured value as a discrete numerical readout on an LCD/LED display, typically with a resolution of **3.5 to 6.5 digits** and a basic DC accuracy in the range of **±0.03% to ±0.5%** of the reading.

### 1.3 The Oscillographic Recorder — Definition

An **Oscillographic Recorder** is an instrument that graphically records or displays an electrical signal as a function of time on a screen or paper chart. The two principal types encountered in KTU laboratories are:

1. **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO/DSO)** — displays the waveform on a phosphor/LCD screen in real time.
2. **Strip Chart / X-Y Recorder** — records the waveform continuously on a moving strip of paper using a pen or thermal print head.

The oscillographic recorder excels at capturing **time-varying, transient, or periodic signals** (e.g., sine waves, PWM outputs, sensor response curves), whereas the multimeter excels at **steady-state DC and low-frequency AC measurements**.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Doctor's Toolkit:**
> 
> Imagine you are a doctor checking a patient:
> - The **thermometer** measures a single static value (body temperature = 37 °C). This is the **Multimeter's** role — it gives you one accurate number at a time.
> - The **ECG (Electrocardiogram) machine** draws the entire heartbeat waveform over time, showing how the heart's electrical activity rises and falls. This is the **Oscillographic Recorder's** role — it gives you the *shape* of the signal.
> 
> In a sensor lab, the DMM measures the steady output of a thermocouple or strain gauge, while the oscilloscope captures the dynamic response of a piezoelectric sensor or a PWM-driven actuator.

### 1.5 Why Both Instruments?

| Aspect | Multimeter (DMM) | Oscillographic Recorder |
|---|---|---|
| Best for | DC & low-freq AC steady values | Time-varying, fast, complex signals |
| Output | Numerical digits | Waveform graph (Voltage vs Time) |
| Bandwidth | DC to ~100 kHz typically | DC to several hundred MHz (DSO) |
| Resolution | High (µV, nA, mΩ) | Moderate (mV to V) |
| Sampling | Single sample, updated slowly | Continuous real-time sampling |

Together, they form a **complementary measurement pair** that handles virtually every lab measurement scenario.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of the Digital Multimeter (DMM)

A modern DMM is built around a single integrated circuit called an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, most commonly the **dual-slope integrating type** or the **sigma-delta type**. The signal flow inside a DMM is:

1. **Input Signal** (Voltage/Current/Resistance) → enters through the test leads and the rotary selector switch.
2. **Attenuator / Range Selector** → scales the input to a level the ADC can handle (typically ±2 V or ±200 mV full scale).
3. **Signal Conditioner** → amplifies, filters, and converts (e.g., current → voltage across a shunt resistor; resistance → voltage via a current source).
4. **ADC** → converts the conditioned analog DC voltage into a digital code.
5. **Display Driver** → drives the 7-segment LCD to show the measured value with proper units, decimal point, and polarity.

#### Why "Digital"?

Because the **ADC** outputs a discrete binary number corresponding to the analog input. A 3.5-digit DMM has a full-scale count of **1999**, while a 4.5-digit DMM has **19999**, and a 6.5-digit laboratory DMM reaches **1999999**. The "½ digit" represents the leading "1" that can only be displayed, not zero.

### 2.2 Operating Principle of the Oscilloscope (DSO)

A **Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)** — which is the modern form of the oscillographic recorder — operates on the principle of **digitization and memory storage**:

1. **Vertical Attenuator / Amplifier** → scales the input signal.
2. **ADC** → samples the signal at a high rate (sample rate, in **Samples per Second (S/s)**).
3. **Acquisition Memory** → stores the digital samples in a circular buffer.
4. **Time Base** → controls the horizontal sweep speed (s/div, ms/div, µs/div).
5. **Trigger Circuit** → synchronizes the display to a stable reference point on the waveform.
6. **Display Reconstruction** → reads the stored samples and rebuilds the waveform on the screen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist Criterion (Fundamental Law of DAQ):** To faithfully reconstruct a signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the sampling rate $f_s$ **must be at least twice** the highest frequency component.
> 
> $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
> 
> In practice, oscilloscopes use **5× to 10× oversampling** to obtain a clean visual display.

### 2.3 The Three Functional Blocks of a Data Acquisition System

Every DAQ system, whether a simple DMM or a sophisticated DSO, contains three universal blocks:

- **Sensor / Transducer** — converts physical quantity to electrical signal.
- **Signal Conditioner** — amplifies, filters, linearizes, and isolates the signal.
- **Digitizer / Display** — converts to digital form and presents to the user.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table compiles every key formula you need for viva, lab record, and end-semester questions on this experiment.

| Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Ohm's Law (DMM Resistance Mode) | $V = I \cdot R$ | Voltage across shunt × current = power dissipation |
| DC Voltage Resolution | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Full Scale}}{2^n - 1}$ | $n$ = number of ADC bits |
| DMM Count Resolution | $\text{Count} = 10^{d-0.5}$ for $d$ digits | For 3.5 digit → 2000 counts |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | $T_s$ = sampling interval in seconds |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate |
| Quantization Error (ADC) | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Step size of ADC in volts |
| Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | For ideal N-bit ADC |
| Vertical Sensitivity | $V_{pp} = \text{Volts/div} \times \text{number of divisions}$ | Peak-to-peak voltage on scope |
| Time Interval | $\Delta t = \text{Time/div} \times \text{number of divisions}$ | Period of waveform on scope |
| Frequency from Period | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | $T$ measured from one complete cycle |
| RMS Value of Sine Wave | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | For pure sinusoidal AC |
| Average Value of Sine | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2 V_{peak}}{\pi}$ | Full-wave rectified average |
| Crest Factor | $CF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ | $= \sqrt{2}$ for a sine wave |
| Shunt Current Measurement | $V_{shunt} = I_{load} \cdot R_{shunt}$ | Voltage across known low-value resistor |
| Accuracy Specification | $\text{Reading Accuracy} = \pm(\% \text{rdg} + n \text{ digits})$ | Typical DMM spec format |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the `\|` pitfall in your answer sheet:** When writing $|V_{in}|$ in a markdown table, always use the LaTeX commands $\vert V_{in} \vert$ or $\mid V_{in} \mid$ to prevent table-breaking issues. KTU examiners look for clean notation.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

This experiment's principles are the backbone of:

- **Industrial Process Control** — SCADA systems using DMMs as front-end sensors.
- **Medical Electronics** — Patient monitors combining ECG (oscillographic) and SpO₂ (multimeter-style numerical readouts).
- **Automotive Diagnostics** — OBD-II scanners using oscilloscope modes for ignition analysis.
- **IoT and Embedded Systems** — Microcontroller ADCs performing the same function as a DMM ADC, but at lower precision.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: ADC Resolution and Quantization Step

A Digital Multimeter and a Digital Storage Oscilloscope both rely on the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**. The fundamental parameter that defines the precision of the ADC is its **resolution** — the smallest voltage change it can detect.

**Step 1: Define the input range.** Suppose the ADC accepts an input range from $-V_{FS}$ to $+V_{FS}$ volts (full-scale bipolar range). The total span is:

$$V_{span} = V_{FS} - (-V_{FS}) = 2 \cdot V_{FS}$$

**Step 2: Number of discrete levels.** An $n$-bit ADC produces $2^n$ distinct output codes. Since one code is reserved for the zero-level, the *usable* levels are $2^n - 1$.

**Step 3: Quantization step size $Q$.** This is the voltage represented by one LSB (Least Significant Bit):

$$Q = \frac{V_{span}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{2 \cdot V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Step 4: Application to a typical 3.5-digit DMM.** A 3.5-digit DMM on the 2 V range has $V_{FS} = 2.000 \text{ V}$ and $n_{\text{effective}} \approx 11$ bits internally (since $2000 \approx 2^{11}$).

$$Q = \frac{2 \times 2.000}{2^{11} - 1} = \frac{4.000}{2047} \approx 1.954 \text{ mV}$$

This means the smallest voltage change the DMM can display on the 2 V range is approximately **1 mV** — which matches the "1 mV" displayed resolution of a typical 3.5-digit handheld DMM.

**Step 5: Application to a 12-bit DSO with 1 V full-scale.** A typical 12-bit ADC oscilloscope has:

$$Q = \frac{2 \times 1.000}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{2.000}{4095} \approx 0.488 \text{ mV}$$

This is the voltage corresponding to one LSB on the scope.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

When an ADC quantizes a continuous signal, it introduces an unavoidable error called **quantization noise**. For an ideal $n$-bit ADC digitizing a full-scale sine wave, the theoretical SQNR is derived as follows:

**Step 1: Quantization error power.** The quantization error $e$ lies uniformly in the range $\pm Q/2$, so its RMS value is:

$$e_{rms} = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{12}}$$

**Step 2: Full-scale sine wave RMS.** A sine wave of peak amplitude $V_{FS}$ has RMS:

$$V_{sine,rms} = \frac{V_{FS}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $Q = \dfrac{2 V_{FS}}{2^n}$.** Expressing the signal-to-noise ratio in dB:

$$SQNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{sine,rms}}{e_{rms}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{FS}/\sqrt{2}}{Q/\sqrt{12}}\right)$$

$$SQNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{FS} \cdot \sqrt{12}}{\sqrt{2} \cdot Q}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{FS} \cdot 2\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2} \cdot Q}\right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute $Q = \dfrac{2 V_{FS}}{2^n}$.**

$$SQNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{FS} \cdot 2\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \dfrac{2 V_{FS}}{2^n}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot 2^n}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the constants.**

$$SQNR = 20 \log_{10}(2^n) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

$$SQNR = 20 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}(2) + 20 \log_{10}(\sqrt{1.5})$$

$$SQNR \approx 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

This is the celebrated **6.02 dB per bit rule** — a 12-bit DSO therefore has a theoretical SQNR of approximately:

$$SQNR_{12-bit} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 \approx 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

A 16-bit audio ADC reaches ~98 dB, and an 8-bit microcontroller ADC reaches only ~50 dB.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Frequency and Amplitude Measurement from an Oscilloscope Display

A common KTU lab question is: *"Given a scope display of 4.2 divisions peak-to-peak at 1 V/div and 6.4 divisions per cycle at 50 µs/div, calculate the signal's amplitude and frequency."*

**Step 1: Peak-to-Peak Voltage.**

$$V_{pp} = (\text{Volts/div}) \times (\text{number of vertical divisions peak-to-peak})$$

$$V_{pp} = 1 \, \frac{V}{div} \times 4.2 \, div = 4.2 \, V$$

**Step 2: Peak Voltage.**

$$V_{peak} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2} = \frac{4.2}{2} = 2.1 \, V$$

**Step 3: RMS Voltage (assuming sine wave).**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2.1}{1.4142} \approx 1.485 \, V$$

**Step 4: Period of the Waveform.**

$$T = (\text{Time/div}) \times (\text{number of horizontal divisions per cycle})$$

$$T = 50 \, \frac{\mu s}{div} \times 6.4 \, div = 320 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5: Frequency.**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{320 \times 10^{-6}} = 3125 \, Hz \approx 3.125 \, kHz$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Current Measurement Using DMM and Shunt Resistor

To measure a current beyond the DMM's direct current range, a **shunt resistor** $R_{shunt}$ of known, precise value is inserted in series with the load. The DMM is set to the **mV DC range** to measure the voltage across the shunt.

**Step 1: Voltage measured by DMM.**

$$V_m = 47.3 \, mV$$

**Step 2: Known shunt resistance.**

$$R_{shunt} = 0.01 \, \Omega \text{ (10 mΩ, precision type)}$$

**Step 3: Apply Ohm's Law.**

$$I_{load} = \frac{V_m}{R_{shunt}} = \frac{47.3 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01} = 4.73 \, A$$

**Step 4: Verify power dissipation in shunt.**

$$P_{shunt} = I^2 \cdot R = (4.73)^2 \times 0.01 = 0.224 \, W$$

A 1 W or 2 W shunt would be safely chosen.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Instrument

| Signal Characteristic | Recommended Instrument | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| DC voltage from a thermocouple (1 mV, steady) | DMM on **mV DC range** | High resolution, low noise, high input impedance |
| 1 kHz sine wave from function generator | Oscilloscope | Shows waveform shape, peak, RMS, distortion |
| 50 Hz mains AC line voltage | DMM on **VAC range** (True RMS) | Numerical safety readout, autoranging |
| 10 MHz clock signal from microcontroller | Oscilloscope (≥100 MHz BW) | DMM bandwidth is too low (max ~100 kHz) |
| Resistance of a thermistor at 25 °C | DMM on **Ω range** | 4-wire ohms eliminates lead resistance |
| Capacitor charging transient (RC = 1 ms) | Oscilloscope | Captures time-domain exponential curve |
| Battery voltage check | DMM on **VDC range** | Quick, accurate, autoranging |
| Frequency measurement of audio tone | DMM with Hz mode or oscilloscope period | Both work; scope gives better waveform context |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Data Acquisition System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the universal signal flow of a DAQ system, showing how the DMM and the oscilloscope share the same three core stages (Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Digitizer) but diverge in the final display and post-processing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHYSICAL PHENOMENON\nTemperature Pressure Light Strain] --> B[SENSOR / TRANSDUCER\nThermocouple LVDT Photodiode Strain Gauge]
    B --> C[SIGNAL CONDITIONER\nAmplifier Filter Linearizer]
    C --> D[ANALOG SIGNAL\nMillivolts to Volts Range]
    D --> E[MODE SELECTOR SWITCH\nRotary Selector on DMM or DSO Channel]
    E --> F1[DMM ADC PATH\nDual Slope Sigma Delta Type]
    E --> F2[DSO ADC PATH\nFlash or Pipelined Type]
    F1 --> G1[DMM DISPLAY\n7 Segment LCD Numeric Readout]
    F2 --> G2[ACQUISITION MEMORY\nCircular Buffer in DSO]
    G2 --> H2[TRIGGER + TIME BASE\nSynchronization Circuit]
    H2 --> I2[DSO DISPLAY\nReconstructed Waveform on LCD]
    G1 --> J[FINAL OUTPUT TO USER]
    I2 --> J[FINAL OUTPUT TO USER]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Inside a Digital Multimeter (DMM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[INPUT TERMINALS\nCOM V A mA Ω] --> B1[RANGE SELECTOR\nManual or Autoranging Attenuator]
    B1 --> C1[MODE CONVERSION\nCurrent Shunt or Ohms Current Source]
    C1 --> D1[SIGNAL CONDITIONER\nAmplifier Anti Alias Filter]
    D1 --> E1[ADC\nDual Slope Integrating]
    E1 --> F1[MICROCONTROLLER\nAverages Scales Formats]
    F1 --> G1[LCD DRIVER AND DISPLAY\nNumerical Readout with Units]
    G1 --> H1[USER READS VALUE\nFor example 12.34 V DC]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Inside a Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[CH1 CH2 CH3 CH4 INPUT BNC] --> B2[VERTICAL ATTENUATOR AND AMPLIFIER]
    B2 --> C2[VERTICAL RESOLUTION ADC\n8 bit or 12 bit]
    C2 --> D2[ACQUISITION MEMORY\nCircular RAM Buffer]
    D2 --> E2[TRIGGER CIRCUIT\nEdge Pulse Video Logic]
    E2 --> F2[TIME BASE GENERATOR\nCrystal Controlled Sweep]
    F2 --> D2
    D2 --> G2[DISPLAY PROCESSOR\nReads Memory Rebuilds Waveform]
    G2 --> H2[LCD OR LED SCREEN\nVoltage vs Time Plot]
    H2 --> I2[USER MEASURES AMPLITUDE PERIOD FREQUENCY]
```

### 4.4 Operational Workflow — Performing a Lab Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Student Receives Experiment]) --> Step1[Step 1 Identify Quantity to Measure\nVoltage Current Resistance Time]
    Step1 --> Step2{Step 2 Choose Instrument}
    Step2 -->|Steady DC value| DMM[Use DMM]
    Step2 -->|Time varying signal| DSO[Use DSO]
    DMM --> Step3[Step 3 Select Correct Range and Function]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4 Connect Probes Observe Polarity]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5 Read Display and Record Value]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6 Calculate Uncertainty from DMM Accuracy Spec]
    DSO --> Step7[Step 3 Set Vertical Sensitivity Volts per div]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 4 Set Horizontal Time Base s per div]
    Step8 --> Step9[Step 5 Adjust Trigger for Stable Display]
    Step9 --> Step10[Step 6 Measure Divisions and Calculate]
    Step10 --> Step11[Step 7 Record Waveform via USB Stick or Camera]
    Step6 --> End([Lab Report Submission])
    Step11 --> End
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> 
> **Q1.** Define a Digital Multimeter (DMM). List its main measurement functions and state the typical accuracy specification of a general-purpose handheld DMM.
> 
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A Digital Multimeter is an electronic instrument that measures voltage, current, and resistance and displays the value as a numerical readout on an LCD. Its main functions are: (i) DC Voltage (ii) AC Voltage (iii) DC Current (iv) AC Current (v) Resistance (vi) Continuity/Diode test. A general-purpose 3.5-digit handheld DMM has a typical DC voltage accuracy of ±(0.5% of reading + 1 digit), with a full-scale count of 1999 on the lowest range. It uses a dual-slope integrating ADC internally, providing high noise rejection at power-line frequencies.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**
> 
> **Q2.** What is an oscillographic recorder? Differentiate between a real-time oscilloscope and a digital storage oscilloscope (DSO).
> 
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> An oscillographic recorder is an instrument that displays or records an electrical signal as a function of time, showing its waveform shape, amplitude, period, and frequency. A real-time (analog) oscilloscope uses a cathode ray tube and continuous electron beam deflection, displaying the signal only at the instant it occurs — no storage. A Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) first samples the signal using an ADC, stores the digital values in memory, and then reconstructs the waveform on an LCD. The DSO can capture single-shot transients, store waveforms for later analysis, and perform automated measurements.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> 
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> 
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO). Discuss the role of the trigger circuit, time base, and acquisition memory. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A 4.2 V peak-to-peak sinusoidal signal at 2.5 kHz is displayed on a DSO. The screen shows the waveform spanning exactly 4.2 vertical divisions and 5.0 horizontal divisions per complete cycle. If the vertical sensitivity is set to 1 V/div and the time base is set to 100 µs/div, verify the displayed values and calculate the RMS voltage and frequency of the signal. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Block Diagram and Functional Architecture of a DSO (7 Marks):**

A DSO consists of the following functional blocks arranged in a signal flow:

1. **Input Channels and Attenuator** — The signal enters through BNC connectors (CH1, CH2, etc.). A compensated attenuator network scales high-voltage signals down to a level the ADC can handle. **(1 Mark)**

2. **Vertical Amplifier** — A variable-gain amplifier boosts the attenuated signal to fill the ADC input range. **(0.5 Mark)**

3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Typically 8-bit or 12-bit, samples the conditioned signal at a rate determined by the time base. For a 1 GSa/s scope, the sample interval is **1 ns**. **(1 Mark)**

4. **Acquisition Memory** — A high-speed circular RAM buffer stores the digital samples. The trigger event freezes a portion of the memory, capturing the waveform before and after the trigger point (pre-trigger and post-trigger). **(1 Mark)**

5. **Time Base Generator** — A crystal-controlled clock produces the sampling clock. The time/div setting determines how the buffer is filled relative to the trigger event. **(1 Mark)**

6. **Trigger Circuit** — Detects a user-specified event (rising edge, falling edge, pulse width, video sync) on the signal and synchronizes the display so that the waveform appears stable rather than scrolling. Without a trigger, the display would be a meaningless jumble. **(1.5 Marks)**

7. **Display Processor and LCD** — Reads the stored samples and reconstructs the waveform visually on the LCD, with graticule markings, channel labels, and on-screen measurement readouts. **(1 Mark)**

**(b) Verification of Displayed Values, RMS Voltage, and Frequency (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Verify Vertical Display.** The waveform spans 4.2 divisions at 1 V/div sensitivity:

$$V_{pp} = 4.2 \, div \times 1 \, \frac{V}{div} = 4.2 \, V \quad \checkmark$$

This matches the given peak-to-peak voltage of 4.2 V, confirming the correct vertical calibration. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2: Verify Horizontal Display.** The waveform spans 5.0 divisions per cycle at 100 µs/div:

$$T = 5.0 \, div \times 100 \, \frac{\mu s}{div} = 500 \, \mu s$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{-6}} = 2000 \, Hz = 2 \, kHz$$

The calculated frequency is **2.0 kHz**, which differs from the given 2.5 kHz — there is a calibration error. Either the time base should be 80 µs/div, or the source frequency is actually 2 kHz. The student should record this discrepancy in the lab report. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 3: Calculate Peak Voltage.**

$$V_{peak} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2} = \frac{4.2}{2} = 2.1 \, V \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 4: Calculate RMS Voltage (sine wave assumption).**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2.1}{1.4142} = 1.485 \, V \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 5: Verification with the given source.** If the actual source is 4.2 V pp at 2.5 kHz, then on a perfectly calibrated scope the waveform should span 4.2 V vertically and 5.0 divisions of 80 µs/div each (for a period of 400 µs) horizontally. The student must check the time base setting. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 6: Percentage discrepancy.**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{2.5 - 2.0}{2.5} \times 100\% = 20\% \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

This large a discrepancy is unacceptable — the student must re-check the time base and the signal generator output.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> 
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a dual-slope integrating ADC used inside a Digital Multimeter. Derive an expression for the output count in terms of the input voltage. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A 3.5-digit DMM is used on the 20 V DC range to measure the output of a sensor. The display reads 12.34 V. The DMM's accuracy specification is ±(0.5% of reading + 2 digits). Calculate the absolute and relative measurement uncertainty. If the same DMM were used on the 200 mV range, what is the maximum resolution it could resolve? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Dual-Slope Integrating ADC — Principle and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Principle of Operation:** The dual-slope ADC integrates the unknown input voltage for a fixed time $T_1$, then "de-integrates" by integrating a known reference voltage of opposite polarity until the integrator output returns to zero. The time $T_2$ taken for de-integration is proportional to the input voltage. **(2 Marks)**

**Phase 1 — Run-Up (Signal Integration):** The input voltage $V_{in}$ is connected to the integrator for a fixed period $T_1$ (determined by counting a fixed number of clock pulses, e.g., 1000 pulses). The integrator output ramps from zero to:

$$V_{A} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_0^{T_1} V_{in} \, dt = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$$

Assuming $V_{in}$ is constant during integration: **(2 Marks)**

$$V_{A} = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$$

**Phase 2 — Run-Down (Reference Integration):** The reference voltage $-V_{ref}$ (opposite polarity) is now applied. The integrator output ramps back to zero. The time $T_2$ required is:

$$0 = V_{A} - \frac{(-V_{ref}) \cdot T_2}{RC} = V_{A} + \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

$$\Rightarrow T_2 = \frac{-V_{A} \cdot RC}{V_{ref}} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{V_{ref}} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

**Count Output:** A counter measures $T_2$ by counting clock pulses of period $T_{clk}$:

$$N = \frac{T_2}{T_{clk}} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{V_{ref} \cdot T_{clk}}$$

Since $T_1$ is also generated by a fixed count $N_1$, we have $T_1 = N_1 \cdot T_{clk}$, hence:

$$\boxed{N = N_1 \cdot \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}}$$

The output count $N$ is **directly proportional** to the input voltage, independent of the RC time constant. This self-calibration property is the key advantage of dual-slope ADCs in DMMs, providing excellent linearity and 50/60 Hz noise rejection. **(2 Marks)**

**(b) DMM Measurement Uncertainty and Resolution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Absolute Uncertainty from the Accuracy Specification.** The DMM specification is ±(0.5% of reading + 2 digits). On the 20 V range, the least significant digit represents 10 mV. **(1 Mark)**

Reading = 12.34 V, so:

$$\text{Reading term} = 0.5\% \times 12.34 = 0.0617 \, V = 61.7 \, mV$$

$$\text{Digit term} = 2 \times 10 \, mV = 20 \, mV$$

$$\text{Total absolute uncertainty} = 61.7 + 20 = 81.7 \, mV \approx 82 \, mV \quad \textbf{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**Step 2: Relative Uncertainty.**

$$\text{Relative Uncertainty} = \frac{0.0817}{12.34} \times 100\% \approx 0.662\% \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 3: Reported Value with Uncertainty.**

$$V_{measured} = (12.34 \pm 0.08) \, V$$

**Step 4: Maximum Resolution on 200 mV Range.** A 3.5-digit DMM displays 1999 counts maximum. On the 200 mV range, the least significant digit represents:

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{200 \, mV}{1999} \approx 0.100 \, mV = 100 \, \mu V \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

This is also the **smallest resolvable voltage change** the DMM can show on the 200 mV range. Note that the resolution is determined by the digit count, but the *accuracy* (uncertainty) is determined by the manufacturer's specification, which is always larger than the resolution. **(1.5 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Resolution with Accuracy** — Resolution is the smallest change the instrument *can* display; accuracy is how close the reading is to the *true* value. Many students write "the DMM is accurate to 1 mV" — this is the resolution, not the accuracy. **(–2 marks)**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the √2 Factor in RMS Calculations** — When a DMM displays AC voltage, it shows the RMS value directly, but if the student is calculating RMS from scope-measured peak voltage, they must use $V_{rms} = V_{peak}/\sqrt{2}$. Missing the √2 loses 1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the "Half Digit"** — A 3.5-digit DMM has a count of 1999, not 9999. Students who write "3.5 digit means 3500 counts" lose a mark.
> 
> 4. **Trigger Confusion** — On the DSO, students often describe the trigger as "starting the display" rather than "synchronizing the sweep to a stable reference point on the waveform." The latter is the textbook definition. **(–1 mark)**
> 
> 5. **Not Stating Units in Numerical Answers** — A value of "12.34" without "V" is incomplete. Always include the unit.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the Nyquist Criterion Discussion** — When asked about DSO sample rate, students often state "1 GSa/s" without explaining that this is to satisfy the Nyquist criterion for the bandwidth of interest.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Data Acquisition using Multimeter and Oscillographic Recorder:**
> 
> - **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the process of converting physical signals into a measurable electrical form and then into a readable display.
> - **DMM** measures DC/AC voltage, current, resistance, and shows a **numerical readout**.
> - **Oscilloscope/DSO** measures **time-varying signals** and shows a **waveform plot** of voltage vs. time.
> - A **3.5-digit DMM** has a full-scale count of **1999**; a **4.5-digit** has **19999**; a **6.5-digit laboratory DMM** has **1999999**.
> - The **dual-slope ADC** is the heart of most DMMs — it provides excellent noise rejection at 50/60 Hz line frequencies.
> - The **DSO** uses a high-speed ADC (8-bit or 12-bit) and stores samples in an **acquisition memory** for later display.
> - The **Nyquist criterion** states that the sample rate $f_s$ must be **at least 2× the highest signal frequency** to avoid aliasing.
> - The **trigger circuit** synchronizes the scope's sweep to a stable point on the waveform for a non-flickering display.
> - **Time/div × divisions = period $T$**, and **frequency $f = 1/T$**.
> - **Volts/div × vertical divisions = peak-to-peak voltage $V_{pp}$**, and $V_{peak} = V_{pp}/2$.
> - For a sine wave, **$V_{rms} = V_{peak}/\sqrt{2}$** and **$V_{avg} = 2V_{peak}/\pi$**.
> - **Quantization step** $Q = V_{FS}/2^{n-1}$; **SQNR** $= 6.02n + 1.76$ dB for an ideal N-bit ADC.
> - A **shunt resistor** is used to extend the DMM's current range by measuring the voltage across a known low resistance.
> - **DMM accuracy** is specified as **±(% of reading + n digits)**, where "digits" refers to the LSD (least significant digit) of the selected range.
> - **Resolution** ≠ **Accuracy**. Resolution is the smallest *displayable* increment; accuracy is the closeness to the *true* value.
> - The DMM has a typical input impedance of **10 MΩ** on voltage ranges — important for high-impedance sensor circuits.
> - The DSO has a much wider **bandwidth** (often 100 MHz to 1 GHz) compared to a DMM (typically DC to 100 kHz), making it the only choice for high-frequency signals.
> - In a lab record, always include: instrument model, range, accuracy spec, calibration date, and any environmental conditions affecting the reading.
> - For full KTU marks, sketch the **block diagram** of the DMM and DSO before writing the procedure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connect an LED to GPIO pin 25 and control it through the command line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Connect an LED to GPIO pin 25 and control it through the command line # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** refers to the generic, uncommitted digital signal pins on a microcontroller or single-board computer (SBC) whose behaviour (input or output) and logic level (HIGH/LOW) can be dynamically configured by the user at runtime through software. In the context of the **Raspberry Pi**, **GPIO pin 25** is one such programmable digital pin on the Broadcom BCM2837/BCM2711 SoC, which is physically accessible on the **40-pin J8 header**. The pin operates at a logic level of **3.3 V DC** and can safely source or sink a maximum DC current of approximately **16 mA per pin** (with a total chip limit of **50 mA** across all GPIOs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Experiment 2 of the Sensors and Devices Lab (PCCIL607), the student is expected to demonstrate the ability to (a) identify GPIO pins using board pinout diagrams, (b) safely interface an external actuator (LED) with proper current-limiting, and (c) manipulate GPIO state purely through **Linux command-line interfaces (CLI)** — without writing a high-level program. > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Command-Line Control (CLI):** A text-based method of interacting with the Linux kernel and hardware through terminal commands, as opposed to a Graphical User Interface (GUI) or compiled program. For GPIO, the three principal CLI methods are: (1) the sysfs interface at `/sys/class/gpio/`, (2) the `raspi-gpio` utility, and (3) the legacy `gpio` command from the WiringPi library. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the **Raspberry Pi** as a **large office building with 40 numbered mailboxes (the GPIO pins)** nailed to its front desk. Each mailbox has a small flag: - **Flag UP = HIGH = 3.3 V** (e.g., a tiny "ON" notification is being broadcast) - **Flag DOWN = LOW = 0 V** (the default neutral state, like ground) **GPIO 25** is just one specific mailbox. By attaching a small electric **lamp (LED)** with the right **fuse (resistor)** to mailbox 25, we can make the lamp glow simply by "raising the flag" with a one-line command typed in the terminal — no need to write a full program, no need to click any button. > [!TIP] > Think of the **resistor** as a *safety fuse* on the lamp. Without it, the lamp would draw too much current from the GPIO pin and burn out — both the LED and the Pi's pin can be permanently destroyed. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Raspberry Pi GPIO Logic HIGH Voltage | $V_{OH}$ | **3.3** | V | | Raspberry Pi GPIO Logic LOW Voltage | $V_{OL}$ | **0.0** | V | | Maximum source current per pin | $I_{max}$ | **16** | mA | | Red LED typical forward voltage | $V_F$ | **1.8 – 2.2** | V | | Green LED typical forward voltage | $V_F$ | **2.0 – 3.0** | V | | Standard current-limiting resistor (practical) | $R$ | **220 – 330** | Ω | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Theory — Breaking Down the Concept The complete experiment can be decomposed into **three independent functional blocks**: ### Block A — Hardware Interface Layer 1. **Pin Identification:** Locate **GPIO 25** on the 40-pin J8 header. By Broadcom (BCM) numbering convention, GPIO 25 is exposed on **physical pin 22** of the header. The numbering is *not* sequential — physical pin 22 = BCM GPIO 25. 2. **Current-Limiting Resistor Selection:** The Raspberry Pi GPIO outputs at 3.3 V, but a standard red LED drops about 2 V across itself when conducting. The remaining 1.3 V must be dissipated as a voltage drop across a series resistor to limit the current to a safe value (typically 8 – 15 mA). 3. **Circuit Topology:** The LED is driven in *current-sinking* or *current-sourcing* mode. In our standard configuration, the GPIO pin acts as the **source** (3.3 V), the current flows through the resistor → LED → GND. ### Block B — Linux Kernel / sysfs Layer 1. The Linux kernel exposes GPIO pins as **virtual files** under `/sys/class/gpio/`. Each pin is represented by a directory `gpioN/` where `N` is the BCM number. 2. To control a pin, the user must *export* it (make it visible to user space), set its *direction* (`in` or `out`), and then write a *value* (`0` or `1`). 3. This file-based approach is *agnostic* to programming language — any tool that can write to a file (echo, Python, C, shell script) can control the pin. ### Block C — High-Level Utility Layer 1. The `raspi-gpio` utility (official, bundled with Raspberry Pi OS) provides a simplified wrapper. 2. The `gpio` utility from **WiringPi** (third-party, now deprecated but widely used) provides yet another wrapper. 3. Both ultimately translate to sysfs or `/dev/gpiochip` (libgpiod) calls under the hood. --- ## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' of Each Step > **Why a resistor?** Without it, the LED's low dynamic resistance would cause it to draw excessive current, exceeding the 16 mA per-pin rating and possibly destroying both the LED and the Pi's SoC. > **Why export the pin first?** By default, the kernel does not allow user-space access to GPIO pins to prevent accidental damage. The `export` step is a deliberate "unlock" that the user must perform. > **Why set direction before writing value?** A pin configured as input will *not* drive current when you set value = 1; it merely enables the internal pull-up. Always set direction = `out` first if you want to power an LED. > **Why `sudo`?** GPIO files in `/sys/class/gpio/` are owned by the `root` user. Without elevated privileges, write operations will return "Permission denied". --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Description | Typical Values | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $V = I \cdot R$ | Ohm's Law — fundamental V-I-R relationship | — | V, A, Ω | | $R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_{LED}}$ | Current-limiting resistor calculation | $V_{CC} = 3.3$ V, $V_F = 2.0$ V, $I_{LED} = 10$ mA → $R = 130\ \Omega$ | Ω | | $I_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R}$ | Verification — actual current through LED | For $R = 220\ \Omega$, $V_F = 2$ V → $I_{LED} \approx 5.9$ mA | mA | | $P_{R} = I^2 \cdot R$ | Power dissipated by resistor | For $I = 10$ mA, $R = 220\ \Omega$ → $P_R = 22$ mW | mW | | $P_{LED} = V_F \cdot I_{LED}$ | Power consumed by LED | For $V_F = 2$ V, $I = 10$ mA → $P_{LED} = 20$ mW | mW | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Pipe Rule (KTU Formula Sheet):** In all KTU formula tables, the absolute-value or division-bar symbol is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. Notice the use of `\dfrac{}{}` and `\approx` in the table above. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The skill of "command-line GPIO manipulation" is foundational in: - **Industrial Automation:** Headless Raspberry Pi controllers running in server racks with no monitor, controlled remotely via SSH. - **IoT Edge Devices:** Quickly prototyping sensor/actuator wiring before committing to a Python/C firmware implementation. - **Embedded Linux Development:** Many production systems use sysfs or `libgpiod` directly for deterministic, low-latency control. - **Robotics:** Rapid bench testing of motor drivers, relays, and indicator LEDs during hardware bring-up. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Resistor Value for a Safe LED Interface **Problem Statement:** Design a current-limiting resistor for a red LED ($V_F = 2.0$ V, $I_{safe} = 10$ mA) connected between **GPIO 25** (3.3 V logic) and **GND** on a Raspberry Pi. **Step 1 — Identify the knowns.** The Raspberry Pi GPIO 25 outputs a HIGH voltage of: $$V_{CC} = 3.3\ \text{V}$$ The red LED has a forward voltage drop of: $$V_F = 2.0\ \text{V}$$ The target forward current through the LED is: $$I_{LED} = 10\ \text{mA} = 0.010\ \text{A}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).** The sum of voltage drops around the series loop (GPIO → Resistor → LED → GND) must equal the supply voltage: $$V_{CC} = V_R + V_F$$ The voltage that must drop across the resistor is therefore: $$V_R = V_{CC} - V_F$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$V_R = 3.3\ \text{V} - 2.0\ \text{V} = 1.3\ \text{V}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Ohm's Law to the resistor.** $$R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_{LED}}$$ $$R = \dfrac{1.3\ \text{V}}{0.010\ \text{A}} = 130\ \Omega$$ **Step 5 — Choose the nearest standard E12 resistor value.** Standard E12 values near 130 Ω are 120 Ω and 150 Ω. The next value **above** the calculated minimum (to limit current conservatively) is **150 Ω**, but a more commonly stocked and slightly safer value is: $$R_{chosen} = 220\ \Omega$$ **Step 6 — Verify the actual LED current with the chosen resistor.** $$I_{LED,actual} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R_{chosen}} = \dfrac{1.3\ \text{V}}{220\ \Omega}$$ $$I_{LED,actual} \approx 5.9\ \text{mA}$$ **Step 7 — Verify power dissipation in the resistor.** $$P_R = I_{LED,actual}^{\ 2} \cdot R_{chosen}$$ $$P_R = (0.0059)^2 \cdot 220 \approx 7.7\ \text{mW}$$ A standard ¼ W (250 mW) resistor is more than adequate. ✅ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** A **220 Ω resistor** in series with the red LED provides safe operation at approximately **5.9 mA**, well below the 16 mA per-pin limit of the Raspberry Pi. The LED will glow with comfortable brightness, and both the LED and the Pi's SoC will remain protected. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Walk-Through: Three Command-Line Methods to Control GPIO 25 ### Method 1 — The Legacy sysfs Interface (Universal, Kernel-Level) This method uses Linux's virtual filesystem to manipulate GPIO 25 directly. It works on all Raspberry Pi OS versions prior to Bookworm's libgpiod default. **Step 1 — Navigate to the GPIO sysfs root.** ``` cd /sys/class/gpio/ ``` This directory is a *pseudo-filesystem* maintained by the kernel. Each GPIO pin is represented by a numbered subdirectory. **Step 2 — Export GPIO 25 to user space.** ``` echo 25 > /sys/class/gpio/export ``` This command writes the string `"25"` to the `export` file, causing the kernel to dynamically create the directory `/sys/class/gpio/gpio25/`. **Step 3 — Set the direction to output.** ``` echo out > /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/direction ``` The `direction` file accepts the strings `"in"` or `"out"`. Setting it to `"out"` configures the pin as a push-pull output driver. **Step 4 — Turn the LED ON.** ``` echo 1 > /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/value ``` Writing `"1"` drives the pin to 3.3 V. Current flows through the resistor and LED to ground; the LED illuminates. **Step 5 — Turn the LED OFF.** ``` echo 0 > /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/value ``` Writing `"0"` drives the pin to 0 V. No potential difference exists, no current flows, the LED turns dark. **Step 6 — Unexport the pin (clean-up).** ``` echo 25 > /sys/class/gpio/unexport ``` This removes the `gpio25/` directory and releases the pin back to the kernel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **All of the above commands require `sudo` privileges** because the sysfs files are owned by the `root` user. Forgetting `sudo` will result in a `Permission denied` error — a common KTU lab mistake. --- ### Method 2 — The `raspi-gpio` Utility (Official, Modern, Recommended) The `raspi-gpio` tool is bundled with Raspberry Pi OS and provides a single-line interface to the kernel's GPIO character device. **Step 1 — Read the current state of GPIO 25 (optional sanity check).** ``` raspi-gpio get 25 ``` Output: `GPIO 25: level=0 func=OUTPUT pull=UP` **Step 2 — Turn ON the LED.** ``` raspi-gpio set 25 op dh ``` Here: - `op` → set pin as **o**ut**p**ut - `dh` → drive level **h**igh (3.3 V) **Step 3 — Turn OFF the LED.** ``` raspi-gpio set 25 op dl ``` Here: - `dl` → drive level **l**ow (0 V) **Step 4 — Read state again to verify.** ``` raspi-gpio get 25 ``` Output: `GPIO 25: level=1 func=OUTPUT pull=UP` --- ### Method 3 — The `gpio` Utility (WiringPi, Legacy but Iconic) WiringPi was the *de facto* GPIO library for over a decade. It is deprecated as of 2019, but the `gpio` command still works on older Raspberry Pi OS images. **Step 1 — Set pin 25 as output (WiringPi uses its own pin-numbering scheme; for physical header pin numbering, use `-g` flag for BCM).** ``` gpio -g mode 25 out ``` **Step 2 — Turn ON the LED.** ``` gpio -g write 25 1 ``` **Step 3 — Turn OFF the LED.** ``` gpio -g write 25 0 ``` **Step 4 — Read the state.** ``` gpio -g read 25 ``` Output: `1` (or `0`) --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three CLI Methods | Feature | sysfs (`/sys/class/gpio/`) | `raspi-gpio` | WiringPi `gpio` | |---|---|---|---| | **Maintainer** | Linux Kernel | Raspberry Pi Foundation | Gordon Henderson (deprecated) | | **Raspberry Pi OS Support** | Buster and earlier; legacy in Bookworm | All recent versions | Legacy only | | **Root Required?** | **Yes** (`sudo`) | **No** (uses gpiochip) | **No** | | **Pin Numbering** | BCM only | BCM only | WiringPi / BCM (with `-g`) | | **Granularity** | Maximum (raw access) | High (with extras like pull config) | Medium | | **Industry Use Today** | Educational / scripts | Production-grade | Almost nil | | **KTU 2024 Recommendation** | ✅ Teach the fundamentals | ✅ **Preferred for lab** | ⚠ Mention historically | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hardware Circuit Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **physical wiring topology** of the LED + Resistor + GPIO 25 + GND circuit. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Raspberry Pi
3.3V Pin
Physical Pin 1"]:::power P2["GPIO 25
Physical Pin 22
BCM Pin 25"]:::gpio R1["Current-Limiting
Resistor
R = 220 ohm"]:::resistor LED1["LED
Anode to Resistor
Cathode to GND"]:::led GND1["GND Pin
Physical Pin 6"]:::ground P2 -- "Wire" --> R1 R1 -- "Anode Leg" --> LED1 LED1 -- "Cathode Leg" --> GND1 P1 -. "3.3V Rail" .- P2 classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gpio fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef resistor fill:#D2691E,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef led fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef ground fill:#2F2F2F,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The signal path is **GPIO 25 → 220 Ω Resistor → LED Anode → LED Cathode → GND**. The LED must be connected with correct polarity — the longer leg (anode) goes toward the resistor, the shorter leg (cathode, with a flat edge on the LED body) goes to GND. **Reverse-biased LEDs will not light up** — a common KTU lab observation. --- ## 4.2 Software Control Flow — Command Sequence Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["User Opens
Terminal via SSH"]:::step B["Check Current
GPIO State
raspi-gpio get 25"]:::step C{"User Decision
ON or OFF?"}:::decision D["Configure as Output
raspi-gpio set 25 op"]:::step E["Drive Pin HIGH
raspi-gpio set 25 op dh"]:::step F["LED Glows ON
3.3V across circuit"]:::result G["Drive Pin LOW
raspi-gpio set 25 op dl"]:::step H["LED Goes OFF
0V across circuit"]:::result I["Verify State
raspi-gpio get 25"]:::step J["Clean Up
Unexport if needed"]:::step A --> B B --> C C -->|"ON"| D D --> E E --> F C -->|"OFF"| G G --> H F --> I H --> I I --> J classDef step fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 GPIO 25 Pin Location Reference (40-Pin J8 Header) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P1["J8 Header Left Side (Odd Pins)"] direction TB pin1["Pin 1: 3.3V"] pin3["Pin 3: GPIO 2"] pin5["Pin 5: GPIO 3"] pin7["Pin 7: GPIO 4"] pin9["Pin 9: GND"] pin11["Pin 11: GPIO 17"] pin13["Pin 13: GPIO 27"] pin15["Pin 15: GPIO 22"] pin17["Pin 17: 3.3V"] pin19["Pin 19: GPIO 10"] pin21["Pin 21: GPIO 9"] pin23["Pin 23: GPIO 11"] pin25["Pin 25: GND"] pin27["Pin 27: GPIO 0"] pin29["Pin 29: GPIO 5"] pin31["Pin 31: GPIO 6"] pin33["Pin 33: GPIO 13"] pin35["Pin 35: GPIO 19"] pin37["Pin 37: GPIO 26"] pin39["Pin 39: GND"] end subgraph P2["J8 Header Right Side (Even Pins)"] direction TB pin2["Pin 2: 5V"] pin4["Pin 4: 5V"] pin6["Pin 6: GND"] pin8["Pin 8: GPIO 14"] pin10["Pin 10: GPIO 15"] pin12["Pin 12: GPIO 18"] pin14["Pin 14: GND"] pin16["Pin 16: GPIO 23"] pin18["Pin 18: GPIO 24"] pin20["Pin 20: GND"] pin22["Pin 22: GPIO 25 TARGET"] pin24["Pin 24: GPIO 8"] pin26["Pin 26: GPIO 7"] pin28["Pin 28: GPIO 1"] pin30["Pin 30: GND"] pin32["Pin 32: GPIO 12"] pin34["Pin 34: GND"] pin36["Pin 36: GPIO 16"] pin38["Pin 38: GPIO 20"] pin40["Pin 40: GPIO 21"] end pin22:::target classDef target fill:#FF1493,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff ``` > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** The 40-pin J8 header has **odd-numbered pins on the left** and **even-numbered pins on the right** when the Pi is oriented with the USB ports facing you. **GPIO 25 is on physical pin 22** — the 22nd pin from the top-right corner of the header. --- ## 4.4 Voltage and Current State Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR OFF["State: OFF
GPIO 25 = 0V
No current
LED Dark"]:::off ON["State: ON
GPIO 25 = 3.3V
I approx 5.9 mA
LED Bright"]:::on CMDOFF["Command:
echo 0 > value
or
raspi-gpio set 25 op dl"]:::cmd CMDON["Command:
echo 1 > value
or
raspi-gpio set 25 op dh"]:::cmd CMDON --> ON CMDOFF --> OFF classDef off fill:#696969,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef on fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cmd fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Sensors Lab]` **Define GPIO. What is the significance of the `export` step in the Linux sysfs interface for GPIO control?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)** are uncommitted digital pins on a microcontroller/SBC whose direction (input/output) and logic level (HIGH/LOW) can be controlled by software at runtime. - **(1 mark)** - The **Raspberry Pi's GPIO 25** operates at **3.3 V logic** and is physically located on **pin 22** of the 40-pin J8 header. - **(1 mark)** - The `export` step (`echo 25 > /sys/class/gpio/export`) dynamically creates a user-space directory (`gpio25/`) and **unlocks the pin for application-level access** — without it, the pin remains hidden and protected by the kernel. - **(1 mark)** --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Sensors Lab]` **Why is a current-limiting resistor necessary when interfacing an LED to a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin? Calculate its value for a 2 V LED at 10 mA driven from a 3.3 V GPIO.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - An LED has very low dynamic resistance once forward-biased. Without a series resistor, it would draw current exceeding the **16 mA per-pin limit** of the Raspberry Pi, potentially destroying both the LED and the SoC. - **(1 mark)** - Using $R = (V_{CC} - V_F) / I_{LED}$: - **(1 mark)** $$R = \dfrac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.010} = \dfrac{1.3}{0.010} = 130\ \Omega$$ - The nearest standard value is **220 Ω**, which limits actual current to ≈ 5.9 mA — safe and adequately bright. - **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14-Mark Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE Pattern Note:]** KTU End Semester Examinations for lab-based courses (PCCIL607) typically follow a 14-mark written component that may accompany a viva-voce and record evaluation. The questions below are constructed to mirror the depth of a typical KTU Part B long-answer. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]` **(a)** Explain the physical pin layout of the Raspberry Pi 40-pin GPIO header. How is **GPIO 25** identified in terms of BCM numbering versus physical pin number? With a neat circuit description, explain how an LED is safely interfaced to GPIO 25. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe, with exact command syntax, the **sysfs method** to control the LED connected to GPIO 25. List the sequence of commands to turn the LED ON, turn it OFF, and verify its state. Also state the role of the `direction` and `value` files. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Pin Layout & LED Interfacing (7 marks):** - The Raspberry Pi exposes a **40-pin dual-row header** known as the **J8 header** on the board. The pins are numbered **1 to 40**, with **odd pins on one side** and **even pins on the other**. - **(1 mark)** - The Raspberry Pi supports **two numbering conventions**: - **Physical (Board) Numbering:** Counts the header pin position (1, 2, 3, …, 40). - **BCM (Broadcom) Numbering:** Refers to the pin's identity on the Broadcom SoC. - **GPIO 25** (BCM) corresponds to **physical pin 22** of the J8 header. - **(1 mark)** - **Required components:** One red LED, one 220 Ω resistor (¼ W), two jumper wires, one breadboard. - **(1 mark)** - **Circuit description:** The LED's **anode** (longer leg) is connected through the **220 Ω current-limiting resistor** to **physical pin 22 (GPIO 25)**. The LED's **cathode** (shorter leg, identified by the flat edge on the LED dome) is connected to a **GND pin** (e.g., physical pin 6). - **(2 marks)** - **Why the resistor?** To limit the forward current of the LED to a safe value (≈ 5.9 mA with a 220 Ω resistor), protecting both the LED and the Pi's GPIO from exceeding the **16 mA per-pin** and **50 mA bank** limits. - **(1 mark)** - **Why 3.3 V and not 5 V?** The Raspberry Pi's GPIO pins are **not 5 V tolerant**. Applying 5 V to a GPIO pin will permanently damage the SoC. The 3.3 V rail is the safe high level. - **(1 mark)** --- **(b) sysfs Command-Line Control of GPIO 25 (7 marks):** - The Linux kernel exposes GPIO pins through the **sysfs pseudo-filesystem** at `/sys/class/gpio/`. This is the lowest-level, language-agnostic interface to GPIO. - **(1 mark)** - **Step 1 — Export the pin:** ``` sudo echo 25 > /sys/class/gpio/export ``` This makes the kernel create `/sys/class/gpio/gpio25/` dynamically. If the directory already exists, the command throws a "Device or resource busy" error, which is harmless. - **(1 mark)** - **Step 2 — Set direction to output:** ``` sudo echo out > /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/direction ``` The `direction` file accepts the strings `"in"` or `"out"`. For our LED, the pin must be an **output**. - **(1 mark)** - **Step 3 — Turn the LED ON:** ``` sudo echo 1 > /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/value ``` Writing `"1"` drives the pin HIGH (3.3 V), completing the circuit and illuminating the LED. - **(1 mark)** - **Step 4 — Turn the LED OFF:** ``` sudo echo 0 > /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/value ``` Writing `"0"` drives the pin LOW (0 V), opening the circuit and switching the LED off. - **(1 mark)** - **Step 5 — Verify the state:** ``` sudo cat /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/value ``` The output will be `1` (ON) or `0` (OFF), confirming the actual logic level of the pin. - **(1 mark)** - **Step 6 — Unexport (clean-up):** ``` sudo echo 25 > /sys/class/gpio/unexport ``` This removes the user-space handle and returns the pin to kernel control. - **(1 mark)** --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]` **(a)** With relevant formulae, derive the value of the current-limiting resistor required to safely drive a red LED ($V_F = 2.0$ V, $I_{LED} = 10$ mA) from a 3.3 V Raspberry Pi GPIO pin. Verify the power dissipation and explain the significance of the 16 mA per-pin current limit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the three command-line methods — `sysfs`, `raspi-gpio`, and WiringPi `gpio` — for controlling GPIO 25. For each, provide **one complete command sequence** to turn the LED ON, and list **one practical advantage** and **one disadvantage**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Resistor Derivation (7 marks):** - The system forms a series circuit: **GPIO (3.3 V) → R → LED → GND**. - **(1 mark)** - **KVL equation:** $$V_{CC} = V_R + V_F$$ $$V_R = V_{CC} - V_F = 3.3 - 2.0 = 1.3\ \text{V}$$ - **(1 mark)** - **Ohm's Law application:** $$R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_{LED}} = \dfrac{1.3\ \text{V}}{0.010\ \text{A}} = 130\ \Omega$$ - **(1 mark)** - **Chosen standard value:** $R = 220\ \Omega$ (next standard value above 130 Ω, ensuring current remains below the safe target). - **(1 mark)** - **Actual current verification:** $$I_{actual} = \dfrac{1.3}{220} \approx 5.91\ \text{mA}$$ - **(1 mark)** - **Power dissipation in resistor:** $$P_R = I^2 \cdot R = (0.00591)^2 \cdot 220 \approx 7.7\ \text{mW}$$ A ¼ W (250 mW) resistor comfortably handles this. ✅ - **(1 mark)** - **Significance of the 16 mA limit:** The Broadcom SoC's GPIO pin driver transistors have a maximum rated DC current of **16 mA**. Exceeding this risks permanent junction failure. Additionally, the **total current sunk by all GPIO pins combined must not exceed 50 mA**. Hence conservative design (e.g., 5–10 mA per LED) is mandatory. - **(1 mark)** --- **(b) Comparison of the Three CLI Methods (7 marks):** **Method 1 — sysfs Interface** - **Command sequence to turn LED ON:** ``` sudo echo 25 > /sys/class/gpio/export sudo echo out > /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/direction sudo echo 1 > /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/value ``` - **Advantage:** Universally supported; works on any Linux system; no extra packages required; language-agnostic. - **Disadvantage:** Verbose; requires `sudo`; deprecated in newer kernels (Bookworm onwards). - **(2 marks)** **Method 2 — `raspi-gpio` Utility** - **Command sequence to turn LED ON:** ``` raspi-gpio set 25 op dh ``` - **Advantage:** Concise, single-line operation; official and maintained by Raspberry Pi Foundation; supports extra features like pull-up/pull-down configuration. - **Disadvantage:** Raspberry Pi-specific (not portable to other SBCs); slightly less granular than raw sysfs. - **(2 marks)** **Method 3 — WiringPi `gpio` Utility** - **Command sequence to turn LED ON:** ``` gpio -g mode 25 out gpio -g write 25 1 ``` - **Advantage:** Iconic and widely documented; integrates well with the broader WiringPi C library; simple syntax. - **Disadvantage:** Officially **deprecated** as of 2019; not available on newer Raspberry Pi OS images; limited future support. - **(2 marks)** **Conclusion:** For new KTU 2024 lab work, `raspi-gpio` is the **recommended default**; sysfs is the **didactic choice** for understanding kernel-level GPIO; WiringPi is mentioned for **historical context**. - **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing BCM and Physical Pin Numbers:** Writing *"Connect to pin 25"* is wrong — the student must specify *"physical pin 22 (BCM GPIO 25)"*. KTU examiners specifically look for this distinction. **(−1 to −2 marks)** > 2. **Forgetting `sudo`:** Many students write commands without `sudo` and claim "permission denied" is a bug. Examiners deduct marks for not knowing the privilege model. **(−1 mark)** > 3. **Omitting the Resistor:** Connecting an LED directly to a GPIO pin *will* light it up briefly but **permanently damages** the pin. Examiners expect the **220 Ω resistor with proper derivation** using $R = (V_{CC} - V_F) / I$. **(−2 to −3 marks)** > 4. **Wrong LED Polarity:** Reversing the LED's anode and cathode gives a dark LED. Examiners expect the student to **identify the longer leg as the anode**. **(−1 mark)** > 5. **Confusing `direction = in` and `direction = out`:** A pin set as input will NOT drive the LED regardless of the value written. **(−1 mark)** > 6. **Using 5 V instead of 3.3 V:** The Raspberry Pi's GPIO is **3.3 V only** and is **NOT 5 V tolerant**. Connecting a 5 V source to a GPIO pin destroys the SoC. **(−2 marks, plus a mandatory lab safety comment)** > 7. **Forgetting the `export` step:** Beginners often try to write directly to `/sys/class/gpio/gpio25/value` without first exporting, leading to "No such file or directory". **(−1 mark)** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - ✅ **GPIO 25 = Physical Pin 22** on the Raspberry Pi 40-pin J8 header (BCM numbering). - ✅ Raspberry Pi GPIO logic HIGH = **3.3 V**; logic LOW = **0 V**. **Not 5 V tolerant.** - ✅ Maximum per-pin current = **16 mA**; maximum bank current = **50 mA**. - ✅ A **current-limiting resistor** (typically **220 Ω**) is **mandatory** between GPIO and LED to prevent damage. - ✅ **LED anode (longer leg) → Resistor → GPIO pin**; **LED cathode (shorter leg / flat edge) → GND**. - ✅ **Always use `sudo`** with the sysfs interface because GPIO files are root-owned. - ✅ The **sysfs** method requires three steps: **export → set direction → write value**. - ✅ The **`raspi-gpio`** method is the modern, single-line, official alternative: `raspi-gpio set 25 op dh` (ON) / `dl` (OFF). - ✅ The **WiringPi `gpio`** method is deprecated but historically significant. - ✅ The sysfs path: `/sys/class/gpio/gpio25/{direction, value}`. - ✅ The KTU-preferred method for lab work in 2024 scheme: **`raspi-gpio`** (concise + official). - ✅ Always **unexport** pins after use to release kernel resources cleanly. - ✅ Verify state with `raspi-gpio get 25` or `cat /sys/class/gpio/gpio25/value`.

topics.title: Connect an LED to GPIO pin 24 and a Switch to GPIO 25 and control the LED with the switch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Connect an LED to GPIO pin 24 and a Switch to GPIO 25 and control the LED with the switch # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** refers to the uncommitted digital signal pins on a microcontroller or single-board computer (such as the Raspberry Pi) that can be programmatically configured at runtime to act either as an **input** (to read the state of an external device like a switch) or as an **output** (to drive an external device like an LED). In this experiment, **GPIO 24** (physical pin 18, BCM numbering) is configured as an **OUTPUT** to drive a Light Emitting Diode, while **GPIO 25** (physical pin 22, BCM numbering) is configured as an **INPUT** to read the state of a tactile push-button switch. The software continuously polls the switch and mirrors its logic state onto the LED, implementing the foundational concept of **digital input-output mapping**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment falls under the core competency of *Microcontroller/SBC peripheral interfacing*, a key skill assessed in PCCIL607. Mastery of GPIO configuration, current-limiting resistor calculation, and switch debouncing is mandatory. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a hotel room. The **wall switch** near the door is the **INPUT (GPIO 25)** — it tells the hotel's electrical system what *you* want. The **ceiling light bulb** is the **OUTPUT (GPIO 24)** — it physically responds to the command. The **wiring hidden inside the walls** is the **software program** (Python script) that connects the switch to the bulb. The Raspberry Pi acts as the hotel's "central nervous system" that electrically and logically binds the two together. ``` Switch (INPUT) ---> Raspberry Pi Brain ---> LED (OUTPUT) GPIO 25 (Python program) GPIO 24 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Decoded:** > - **BCM Numbering** → Broadcom SOC channel numbers (e.g., GPIO 24, GPIO 25) > - **BOARD Numbering** → Physical pin position on the 40-pin header (e.g., Pin 18, Pin 22) > - **HIGH / True / 1** → 3.3 V logic level > - **LOW / False / 0** → 0 V (Ground) logic level ## 1.3 Hardware Components Required | Sl. No | Component | Specification | Quantity | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Raspberry Pi (3B+/4B/Zero) | 40-pin GPIO header | 1 | Host controller | | 2 | LED (Light Emitting Diode) | 5 mm, V_f ≈ 2.0 V (red) | 1 | Visual output indicator | | 3 | Tactile Push-Button Switch | SPST, momentary type | 1 | Digital input source | | 4 | Resistor (current limiting) | 220 Ω, ¼ W, ±5 % | 1 | Protects LED from overcurrent | | 5 | Resistor (pull-down) | 10 kΩ, ¼ W, ±5 % | 1 | Defines stable LOW when switch is open | | 6 | Breadboard & Jumper Wires | Male-to-Female, Male-to-Male | As needed | Prototyping wiring | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations ### 2.1.1 GPIO Pin Electrical Characteristics (Raspberry Pi) Every GPIO pin on the Raspberry Pi operates strictly at **3.3 V CMOS logic levels**. Drawing more than **16 mA** from a single pin risks permanent damage to the Broadcom SoC. - **V_OL (Logic LOW output voltage)** ≈ **0 V** - **V_OH (Logic HIGH output voltage)** ≈ **3.3 V** - **Maximum source/sink current per pin** = **16 mA** - **Maximum total current across all pins** = **50 mA** - **Input logic LOW threshold (V_IL)** < **0.8 V** - **Input logic HIGH threshold (V_IH)** > **2.0 V** > [!WARNING] > **Fatal Mistake to Avoid:** Never connect a Raspberry Pi GPIO directly to a 5 V signal. The SoC is **NOT 5 V tolerant**. Always use a logic-level converter or a voltage divider when interfacing with 5 V devices (e.g., Arduino Uno). ### 2.1.2 LED Forward Voltage and Current Limiting An LED is a **non-ohmic** semiconductor device. Applying a voltage above its forward voltage (V_f) without a current-limiting resistor causes exponentially rising current that destroys the diode within milliseconds. The current-limiting resistor **R** is calculated using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL): $$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_{LED}}$$ Where: - **V_supply** = GPIO HIGH voltage = **3.3 V** - **V_f** = LED forward voltage ≈ **2.0 V** (for standard red LED) - **I_LED** = desired forward current, typically **5 mA to 15 mA** ### 2.1.3 Switch Interfacing and Pull-Down Resistor A mechanical switch is a **bistable floating device**. When the switch is **OPEN**, the GPIO input pin is electrically *floating* and can randomly read HIGH or LOW due to electromagnetic noise. A **pull-down resistor** (typically **10 kΩ**) ties the input pin to **GND (0 V)** when the switch is open, guaranteeing a definite LOW state. | Switch State | Pull-down action | GPIO Pin Voltage | Logic Read | |---|---|---|---| | Open (not pressed) | Pull-down holds pin at GND | 0 V | **LOW (0)** | | Closed (pressed) | Switch shorts pin to 3.3 V | 3.3 V | **HIGH (1)** | > [!NOTE] > The Raspberry Pi's GPIO pins have **configurable internal pull-up and pull-down resistors** (between 50 kΩ and 65 kΩ). However, in board examinations, the **external pull-down** is preferred for clarity and is required if the switch is far from the Pi. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | $R_{LED}$ | $\frac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_{LED}}$ | Current-limiting resistor for LED | **220** | Ω | | $V_f$ | Empirical (color-dependent) | LED forward voltage drop | 2.0 (red), 3.2 (blue) | V | | $I_{LED}$ | Configured by designer | LED forward current | 5 – 15 | mA | | $P_{R}$ | $I_{LED}^2 \times R_{LED}$ | Power dissipated by resistor | 0.011 | W | | $R_{pull}$ | Empirical (1 kΩ to 100 kΩ) | Pull-up / pull-down resistor | **10 000** | Ω | | $V_{IL}$ | Datasheet constant | Max voltage read as LOW | 0.8 | V | | $V_{IH}$ | Datasheet constant | Min voltage read as HIGH | 2.0 | V | | $T_{debounce}$ | $R \times C$ (RC time) | Software/hardware debounce | 50 – 200 | ms | **Engineering Utility:** This exact circuit topology (output LED + input switch) is the building block of every embedded system — from washing machine control panels to automotive dashboard buttons to industrial Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: LED Current-Limiting Resistor Value We are given: - Supply voltage from Raspberry Pi GPIO: $V_{supply} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ - Red LED forward voltage: $V_f = 2.0 \text{ V}$ - Desired LED forward current: $I_{LED} = 10 \text{ mA} = 0.010 \text{ A}$ **Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the LED loop:** The supply voltage must equal the sum of voltage drops across the LED and the resistor: $$V_{supply} = V_{R} + V_f$$ **Step 2 — Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor:** $$V_R = V_{supply} - V_f$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:** $$V_R = 3.3 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} = 1.3 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Ohm's Law to compute the resistance:** $$R = \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the voltage drop and current:** $$R = \frac{1.3 \text{ V}}{0.010 \text{ A}}$$ $$R = 130 \text{ }\Omega$$ **Step 6 — Choose the nearest standard E12 resistor value (always round UP for safety):** The next higher standard value is **220 Ω**. We must round up because a lower resistance allows higher current, which could damage the LED. $$R_{chosen} = 220 \text{ }\Omega$$ **Step 7 — Verify the actual LED current with the chosen resistor:** $$I_{LED,actual} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_f}{R_{chosen}}$$ $$I_{LED,actual} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{220} = \frac{1.3}{220}$$ $$I_{LED,actual} \approx 5.9 \text{ mA}$$ This is well within the safe operating range (5 – 15 mA) and produces a comfortably bright LED. **Step 8 — Compute the power rating required for the resistor:** $$P_R = I_{LED,actual}^2 \times R_{chosen}$$ $$P_R = (0.0059)^2 \times 220$$ $$P_R = 3.48 \times 10^{-5} \times 220 \approx 0.00766 \text{ W}$$ A standard **¼ W (0.25 W) resistor** provides a safety margin of more than 30×, so it is perfectly safe. ## 3.2 Switch Pull-Down Resistor Selection **Step 1 — Identify the function of the pull-down resistor:** When the push-button is **open**, the GPIO input pin is electrically disconnected. Without a pull-down resistor, the pin would "float" and pick up stray electromagnetic noise, giving random readings. The resistor provides a **defined path to ground**. **Step 2 — Choose a standard value:** For digital inputs, a value between **1 kΩ and 100 kΩ** is acceptable: - Too small (e.g., 100 Ω) → excessive current drain when switch is closed. - Too large (e.g., 1 MΩ) → noise pickup and slow transitions. The textbook-standard value is: $$R_{pull-down} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Compute the current sunk to ground when the switch is closed:** $$I_{pull} = \frac{V_{supply}}{R_{pull-down}} = \frac{3.3}{10\,000} = 0.33 \text{ mA}$$ This is negligible and does not load the GPIO pin. ## 3.3 Software Debouncing Time Constant Mechanical switches exhibit **contact bounce** — a phenomenon where the metal contacts vibrate and produce multiple rapid OPEN/CLOSED transitions for 5 – 20 ms before settling. Software must ignore this. **Step 1 — Choose a safe debounce delay:** $$T_{debounce} = 200 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$ This comfortably exceeds the worst-case bounce duration of 20 ms by a factor of 10. **Step 2 — Implementation logic in Python:** ``` 1. Read GPIO 25 input. 2. Store the read value. 3. Sleep for 200 ms. 4. Read GPIO 25 input again. 5. If both reads match → accept the value. 6. If they differ → discard and re-read. ``` ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Full Truth Table for LED–Switch System | Switch State (GPIO 25) | Switch Voltage | Python Read | LED Command (GPIO 24) | LED State | LED Voltage | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | OPEN (not pressed) | 0 V (pulled to GND) | `False` / 0 | `GPIO.LOW` | **OFF** | 0 V | | CLOSED (pressed) | 3.3 V (shorted to 3.3 V) | `True` / 1 | `GPIO.HIGH` | **ON** | ≈ 2.0 V (across LED) | > [!NOTE] > In this experiment, the LED is configured as **active-HIGH** — it glows when the GPIO output is HIGH (3.3 V). This is the conventional and safest wiring method for Raspberry Pi circuits. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR PSU["3.3V Power Rail
from Raspberry Pi Pin 1"] GND["Ground Rail
Raspberry Pi Pin 6"] RLED["Current Limiting
Resistor 220 ohm"] DIODE["LED Anode to Cathode
Forward Voltage 2.0V"] SW["Tactile Switch
SPST Momentary"] RPULL["Pull-down Resistor
10 kilo ohm"] GPIO24["Raspberry Pi
GPIO 24 OUTPUT"] GPIO25["Raspberry Pi
GPIO 25 INPUT"] MCU["Python Control Loop
while True"] PSU --> SW SW --> GPIO25 GPIO25 --> RPULL RPULL --> GND GPIO24 --> RLED RLED --> DIODE DIODE --> GND GPIO24 --> MCU GPIO25 --> MCU MCU -. GPIO 24 equals GPIO 25 .-> GPIO24 ``` ## 4.2 Software Flowchart of Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start Program"] --> B["Import RPi.GPIO library"] B --> C["Set BCM numbering mode"] C --> D["Configure GPIO 24 as OUTPUT"] D --> E["Configure GPIO 25 as INPUT
with internal pull-down"] E --> F["Enter infinite while loop"] F --> G["Read digital value at GPIO 25"] G --> H{"Is switch HIGH?
value is True?"} H -- Yes --> I["Set GPIO 24 HIGH
LED turns ON"] H -- No --> J["Set GPIO 24 LOW
LED turns OFF"] I --> K["Sleep 200 ms for debounce"] J --> K K --> F F -. KeyboardInterrupt Ctrl+C .-> L["Cleanup GPIO pins"] L --> M["Exit Program"] ``` ## 4.3 Pin Mapping Reference Table ```mermaid graph TB subgraph RPi["Raspberry Pi 40-Pin GPIO Header"] P1["Physical Pin 1
3.3V Power"] P6["Physical Pin 6
Ground"] P18["Physical Pin 18
BCM GPIO 24"] P22["Physical Pin 22
BCM GPIO 25"] end subgraph Circuit["External Breadboard Circuit"] LEDnode["LED with 220 ohm resistor
Anode to GPIO 24"] SWnode["Switch + 10 kilo ohm pull-down
Signal to GPIO 25"] end P18 --> LEDnode P22 --> SWnode P1 --> SWnode P6 --> LEDnode P6 --> SWnode ``` ## 4.4 Reference Python Program (Exam-Friendly Annotated) ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO # Import the GPIO control library import time # Import time module for sleep delay # Use BCM pin numbering (GPIO numbers, not physical pin numbers) GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) # Disable GPIO warnings so the program runs cleanly every time GPIO.setwarnings(False) # Configure GPIO 24 as an OUTPUT pin (drives the LED) LED_PIN = 24 GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) # Configure GPIO 25 as an INPUT pin (reads the switch) # Internal pull-down resistor ensures a clean LOW when switch is open SWITCH_PIN = 25 GPIO.setup(SWITCH_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_DOWN) try: while True: # Read the current state of the switch switch_state = GPIO.input(SWITCH_PIN) # Mirror the switch state onto the LED if switch_state == GPIO.HIGH: GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) # Turn LED ON else: GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) # Turn LED OFF time.sleep(0.2) # 200 ms debounce delay except KeyboardInterrupt: # Reset the GPIO pins to a safe state on Ctrl+C GPIO.cleanup() print("Program terminated safely. GPIO pins cleaned up.") ``` > [!TIP] > **Examiner Tip:** Always wrap your main code in a `try … except KeyboardInterrupt` block and call `GPIO.cleanup()` at the end. Failure to do so is a 2-mark deduction in KTU lab viva evaluations. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam]** *What is GPIO? Differentiate between BCM and BOARD pin numbering modes in Raspberry Pi.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)** refers to the uncommitted digital pins on a Raspberry Pi that can be programmatically configured as either input or output. (1 mark) **BCM Numbering** uses the Broadcom SoC channel numbers (e.g., GPIO 24, GPIO 25). These numbers correspond to the internal register addresses of the processor and remain the same across all Raspberry Pi models. (1 mark) **BOARD Numbering** uses the physical position of the pin on the 40-pin header (e.g., Pin 18, Pin 22). BOARD numbering is hardware-dependent — physical pin 18 on a Pi 3B+ may not be the same physical pin on a Pi 5 header. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why is a current-limiting resistor necessary when connecting an LED to a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin? Calculate its value for a red LED with V_f = 2.0 V and I_LED = 10 mA, given V_supply = 3.3 V.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A current-limiting resistor is essential because an LED is a **non-ohmic** device. Once the applied voltage exceeds the LED's forward voltage (V_f), the current rises exponentially. Without a resistor, the current would exceed the **16 mA maximum rating** of the Raspberry Pi GPIO pin and destroy both the LED and possibly the Broadcom SoC. (1.5 marks) $$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_{LED}} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.010} = 130 \text{ }\Omega$$ Choosing the next standard E12 value, **R = 220 Ω** (1.5 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** In the End Semester Examination, Part B Module questions carry 14 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts: (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain how an LED is interfaced to **GPIO 24** of a Raspberry Pi. Derive the value of the current-limiting resistor. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Components Required (1 mark):** Raspberry Pi, red LED (V_f = 2.0 V), 220 Ω resistor, jumper wires, breadboard. 2. **Circuit Description (2 marks):** The anode of the LED is connected to GPIO 24 (physical pin 18) through a 220 Ω current-limiting resistor. The cathode of the LED is connected to the GND rail (physical pin 6). When GPIO 24 is set HIGH (3.3 V), current flows through the resistor and LED, illuminating it. 3. **Derivation (4 marks):** Applying KVL around the LED loop: $$V_{supply} = V_R + V_f$$ $$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_{LED}} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.010} = 130 \text{ }\Omega$$ Selecting the next higher standard value: **R = 220 Ω**. Actual LED current with 220 Ω: $$I_{LED,actual} = \frac{1.3}{220} \approx 5.9 \text{ mA}$$ This lies safely within the 5 – 15 mA operating range. --- **(b)** Explain the necessity of a pull-down resistor in a switch interfacing circuit connected to **GPIO 25**. Write a Python program to read the switch and mirror its state onto the LED at GPIO 24. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Need for Pull-Down Resistor (3 marks):** A mechanical switch in the OPEN state leaves the GPIO pin electrically floating. Floating pins are susceptible to electromagnetic noise and can randomly read HIGH or LOW. A 10 kΩ pull-down resistor ties the pin firmly to 0 V (GND) when the switch is open, guaranteeing a stable LOW reading. When the switch is closed, it short-circuits the pin to 3.3 V, overriding the pull-down and producing a stable HIGH. 2. **Truth Table (1 mark):** | Switch | GPIO 25 Read | GPIO 24 Output | LED | |---|---|---|---| | Open | LOW | LOW | OFF | | Closed | HIGH | HIGH | ON | 3. **Python Program (3 marks):** ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setwarnings(False) LED_PIN = 24 SWITCH_PIN = 25 GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) GPIO.setup(SWITCH_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_DOWN) try: while True: if GPIO.input(SWITCH_PIN) == GPIO.HIGH: GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) else: GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) time.sleep(0.2) except KeyboardInterrupt: GPIO.cleanup() ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Exam, Adapted]** **(a)** Compare **active-HIGH** and **active-LOW** LED configurations.
