Justify why active-HIGH is the preferred method for Raspberry Pi GPIO interfacing. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Active-HIGH Configuration (2 marks):** The LED's anode is connected to the GPIO pin (through a resistor) and the cathode is connected to GND. The LED glows when the GPIO pin is driven HIGH (3.3 V). 2. **Active-LOW Configuration (2 marks):** The LED's cathode is connected to the GPIO pin and the anode is connected to V_cc (3.3 V) through a resistor. The LED glows when the GPIO pin is driven LOW (0 V), sinking current to ground. 3. **Justification (3 marks):** Active-HIGH is preferred on the Raspberry Pi because: - The Broadcom SoC is designed to **source** current more reliably than to **sink** it. - An active-HIGH output is the default logic convention in 3.3 V CMOS systems. - At program start, all GPIO pins default to INPUT, which is high-impedance — neither HIGH nor LOW. If the LED were wired active-LOW, it could spuriously glow during boot before the software initializes the pin as OUTPUT and drives it LOW. --- **(b)** What is **contact bounce** in a mechanical switch? Explain two methods to debounce a switch in software and hardware. Show how debouncing is implemented in the LED-switch experiment. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Contact Bounce Definition (2 marks):** When a mechanical switch is pressed or released, the metal contacts vibrate and create multiple rapid electrical transitions (bounces) for 5 – 20 ms before settling into a stable state. If read directly, a single press may register as multiple presses. 2. **Software Debounce Methods (3 marks):** - **Delay Method:** After detecting a change, wait for 200 ms and re-read. If the reading is stable, accept it; otherwise ignore. - **Counter Method:** Use a counter that increments on each consistent read and only accepts the new state when the counter exceeds a threshold (e.g., 5). 3. **Hardware Debounce Method (1 mark):** Add an RC low-pass filter (e.g., R = 10 kΩ, C = 10 µF, τ = 100 ms) at the switch output to smooth out the bouncing transitions. 4. **Implementation in Experiment (1 mark):** In the Python program, a `time.sleep(0.2)` after each read provides 200 ms of software debounce, eliminating false triggers. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Forgetting `GPIO.cleanup()`** → 2-mark deduction. Always release GPIO resources before exit. > - **Using BOARD numbering without declaration** → 1-mark deduction. Always call `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` or `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BOARD)` first. > - **Leaving `setwarnings(True)`** → Minor deduction. Always disable warnings for clean execution. > - **Connecting LED without resistor** → Major deduction (and a destroyed LED in the lab!). The 220 Ω resistor is non-negotiable. > - **Floating switch input** → 1-mark deduction. Always use a pull-down (or pull-up) resistor. > - **Wrong resistor polarity in the diagram** → Marks lost. The current-limiting resistor goes in **series** with the LED anode, not parallel. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **GPIO 24** is configured as **OUTPUT** to drive the LED; **GPIO 25** is configured as **INPUT** to read the switch. - The Raspberry Pi GPIO operates at **3.3 V logic** with a per-pin current limit of **16 mA** — never exceed it. - An LED **must** have a current-limiting resistor. For a red LED at 10 mA: **R = (3.3 − 2.0) / 0.010 = 130 Ω → use 220 Ω (standard E12).** - A switch input **must** have a pull-down (or pull-up) resistor of **10 kΩ** to prevent floating-pin noise. - **BCM numbering** (GPIO 24, GPIO 25) is software-stable; **BOARD numbering** (Pin 18, Pin 22) refers to physical positions. - **Active-HIGH** wiring (anode → resistor → GPIO, cathode → GND) is the Raspberry Pi standard. - **Contact bounce** lasts 5 – 20 ms; debounce using `time.sleep(0.2)` in software or an RC filter in hardware. - Always use `try … except KeyboardInterrupt` and call `GPIO.cleanup()` for a clean program exit. - The Python pattern for input-to-output mirroring is: `GPIO.output(LED, GPIO.input(SWITCH))`. - Maximum safe **LED forward current** on a Pi GPIO: **15 mA**; typical safe design value: **5 – 10 mA**.

topics.title: Use DHT11 temperature sensor and print the temperature and humidity of the room with an interval of 15 seconds
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Use DHT11 temperature sensor and print the temperature and humidity of the room with an interval of 15 seconds

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **DHT11** is a calibrated digital-output relative humidity and temperature sensor that integrates an **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistor** for temperature measurement and a resistive-type humidity sensing element into a single package. It uses a proprietary **single-wire (single-bus) serial protocol** to transmit a **40-bit data packet** (16-bit humidity + 16-bit temperature + 8-bit checksum) to a microcontroller such as an Arduino Uno. In this experiment, the sensor is interfaced with the Arduino, and the acquired values are printed on the **Serial Monitor at a fixed sampling interval of 15 seconds (15,000 ms)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCIL607 – Module 2):**
> This experiment belongs to the cluster of *“Basic Digital & Analog Sensor Interfacing”* and directly satisfies the Course Outcome:
> **CO2:** *Interface various sensors (temperature, humidity, light, gas, etc.) with microcontrollers and interpret the acquired data.* It also maps to **CO4** (Analyze sensor data using a programming environment).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **digital weighing scale** in a grocery shop. You place an item, press a button, and the weight appears. Now imagine the scale **automatically prints the weight every 15 seconds** — even if no new item is placed. The DHT11 works the same way:
- The **thermistor + humidity film** are the "item" being measured.
- The **microcontroller (Arduino)** is the "shopkeeper" reading the value.
- The **Serial Monitor** is the "display screen".
- The **15-second delay** is the "fixed reporting time" — a regular heartbeat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition – Single-Wire Protocol:**
> A communication method where data is exchanged between two devices using **only one data line** plus ground. The master (Arduino) pulls the line LOW to *initiate* communication, and the slave (DHT11) responds with a structured 40-bit frame.

---

## 1.3 Technical Specifications of DHT11

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Voltage | 3.3 – 5.5 | **V (DC)** |
| Temperature Range | 0 – 50 | **°C** |
| Temperature Accuracy | ±2 | **°C** |
| Humidity Range | 20 – 90 | **% RH** |
| Humidity Accuracy | ±5 | **% RH** |
| Resolution (both) | 16 | **bit** |
| Sampling Rate | 1 (min) | **Hz** |
| Interface | Single-Wire Digital | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 1-second minimum sampling rate matters:** Internally, the DHT11 needs time to perform an ADC conversion. Sampling faster than once per second causes data corruption. Our experiment’s **15-second interval** safely satisfies this constraint with a huge margin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DHT11 Single-Wire Communication Protocol

The DHT11 does **not** use I²C, SPI, or UART. It uses a custom **bidirectional single-bus protocol**. Communication is always *initiated* by the microcontroller (master).

**Phase 1 – Start Signal (Master → Slave)**
- The Arduino pulls the data line LOW for **≥ 18 ms**.
- Then releases the line (sets it HIGH) and waits **20–40 µs**.

**Phase 2 – Response Signal (Slave → Master)**
- The DHT11 detects the start signal and pulls the line LOW for **80 µs** (response).
- Then pulls it HIGH for **80 µs** (prepare to send data).

**Phase 3 – Data Transmission (40 bits)**
- Each bit begins with a **50 µs LOW** pulse.
- A HIGH pulse of **26–28 µs** represents a logical `0`.
- A HIGH pulse of **70 µs** represents a logical `1`.

The 40-bit frame is structured as:

$$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{H_{int}}_{8\ \text{bits}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{H_{dec}}_{8\ \text{bits}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{T_{int}}_{8\ \text{bits}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{T_{dec}}_{8\ \text{bits}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{Checksum}_{8\ \text{bits}}$$

- $H_{int}$ = Integer part of humidity
- $H_{dec}$ = Decimal part of humidity
- $T_{int}$ = Integer part of temperature
- $T_{dec}$ = Decimal part of temperature
- Checksum = $(H_{int} + H_{dec} + T_{int} + T_{dec}) \pmod{256}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Step (Mandatory in Code):**
> If the received byte does **not equal** the lower 8 bits of the sum of the first four bytes, the reading must be **discarded**. This is the single most common reason students get *wrong values* in the exam.

### 2.2 Why a 10 kΩ Pull-Up Resistor?

The single-wire bus is **open-drain**. When no device is transmitting, the line floats. The pull-up resistor ensures the line idles at **VCC (5 V)**.

$$V_{line} = V_{CC} \quad \text{when no device drives the line}$$

Recommended value: **4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ** (we use 10 kΩ in this lab).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Humidity Reading | $H = H_{int} + 0.1 \cdot H_{dec}$ | **% RH** | Reconstructs full humidity |
| Temperature Reading | $T = T_{int} + 0.1 \cdot T_{dec}$ | **°C** | Reconstructs full temperature |
| Checksum Verification | $\text{Check} = (H_{int}+H_{dec}+T_{int}+T_{dec}) \mod 256$ | — | Data integrity |
| Total Frame Size | $8 + 8 + 8 + 8 + 8 = 40$ | **bits** | Standard DHT11 packet |
| Required Sampling Interval | $T_{s} \geq 1$ | **s** | Hardware constraint |
| Experiment Interval | $\Delta t = 15$ | **s** | As per problem statement |
| Delay Conversion | $15 \text{ s} = 15{,}000$ | **ms** | Code uses `delay()` |
| Pull-up Resistor | $R_{PU} = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega \text{ to } 10\text{ k}\Omega$ | **Ω** | Bus stability |

> [!NOTE]
> The DHT11 (unlike DHT22) sends **decimal parts as `0000`** in many clones — meaning temperature may appear only as an integer (e.g., 27 °C, not 27.0 °C). This is normal hardware behavior, *not* a code bug.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

DHT11-based monitoring is used in:
- **Smart agriculture** – greenhouse climate control
- **HVAC systems** – indoor air-quality regulation
- **Server rooms** – thermal alarms for data centers
- **Home automation** – triggering exhaust fans when humidity rises
- **Cold-chain logistics** – pharmaceutical & food transport monitoring

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Working Example — Reconstructing a DHT11 Data Frame

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
The DHT11 sends the following 5 bytes. Compute the temperature, humidity, and verify the checksum.

$$
\text{Raw Bytes Received:} \quad [H_{int}, H_{dec}, T_{int}, T_{dec}, Checksum] = [45, 0, 24, 0, 69]
$$

**Step 1 – Compute the Checksum (Lower 8 bits of sum)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= H_{int} + H_{dec} + T_{int} + T_{dec} \\
&= 45 + 0 + 24 + 0 \\
&= 69
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Compare with Received Checksum**

$$
\text{Checksum}_{\text{computed}} = 69 \mod 256 = 69
$$

$$
\text{Checksum}_{\text{received}} = 69
$$

$$
\therefore 69 = 69 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Data is VALID.} \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 3 – Decode Humidity**

$$
H = H_{int} + 0.1 \cdot H_{dec} = 45 + 0.1 \cdot 0 = 45\ \%\,\text{RH}
$$

**Step 4 – Decode Temperature**

$$
T = T_{int} + 0.1 \cdot T_{dec} = 24 + 0.1 \cdot 0 = 24\ ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{\ \text{Humidity} = 45\,\%\ \text{RH},\quad \text{Temperature} = 24\,^{\circ}\text{C}\ }
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Timing Analysis for 15-Second Interval

The experiment requires the readings to appear **exactly once every 15 seconds**. We must confirm that the sensor’s hardware conversion time is **not violated**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{sampling}} &= 15 \text{ s} \\
t_{\text{conversion, DHT11}} &\approx 1 \text{ s (worst-case internal ADC)} \\
t_{\text{transmission}} &\approx 4 \text{ ms (40 bits at ~10 kbps)} \\
t_{\text{serial print}} &\approx 50 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
t_{\text{total per cycle}} = t_{\text{conversion}} + t_{\text{transmission}} + t_{\text{serial print}} \approx 1.054 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\text{Safety margin} = t_{\text{sampling}} - t_{\text{total per cycle}} = 15 - 1.054 \approx 13.946 \text{ s}
$$

Since the safety margin is **far greater than zero**, the 15-second interval is **safe and non-blocking**.

---

### 3.3 Complete Arduino Code (Reference Implementation)

```cpp
/*--------------------------------------------------------------
   Experiment: DHT11 Temperature & Humidity with 15-sec Interval
   Course:     PCCIL607 - Sensors and Devices Lab (KTU 2024)
--------------------------------------------------------------*/

#include "DHT.h"                // Official DHT sensor library

#define DHTPIN   2              // Data pin of DHT11 connected to D2
#define DHTTYPE  DHT11          // Sensor family
#define INTERVAL 15000UL        // 15 seconds in milliseconds

DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
unsigned long previousMillis = 0;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);           // Open serial monitor at 9600 baud
  dht.begin();                  // Initialize DHT11
  Serial.println("DHT11 Sensor Initialized...");
  Serial.println("Waiting for first reading...");
}

void loop() {
  unsigned long currentMillis = millis();

  if (currentMillis - previousMillis >= INTERVAL) {
    previousMillis = currentMillis;

    // ---- Read sensor ----
    float humidity    = dht.readHumidity();
    float temperature = dht.readTemperature();   // Celsius

    // ---- Validate readings ----
    if (isnan(humidity) || isnan(temperature)) {
      Serial.println("ERROR: Failed to read from DHT11 sensor!");
      return;
    }

    // ---- Print to Serial Monitor ----
    Serial.print("Humidity:    ");
    Serial.print(humidity);
    Serial.print(" %\t");

    Serial.print("Temperature: ");
    Serial.print(temperature);
    Serial.println(" *C");
  }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Valid Implementation Styles:**
> 1. **Blocking style** (simpler): Uses `delay(15000);` — but this freezes the CPU.
> 2. **Non-blocking style** (recommended, shown above): Uses `millis()` — keeps the MCU free to handle other tasks.
> KTU examiners **prefer the `millis()` style** because it reflects *professional embedded design*.

---

### 3.4 Hardware Connection Matrix

| DHT11 Pin | Wire Color | Arduino Uno Pin | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VCC** | Red | **5V** | Power supply (3.3–5.5 V) |
| **DATA** | Yellow | **D2** (with 10 kΩ pull-up to 5V) | Bidirectional single-wire |
| **NC** | — | Not connected | No connection |
| **GND** | Black | **GND** | Common ground |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
*All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. Labels use plain uppercase text only.*

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ARDUINO UNO MASTER] -->|Step 1 Pull LOW for 18 ms| B[DHT11 SLAVE SENSOR]
    B -->|Step 2 Response 80 us LOW then 80 us HIGH| A
    B -->|Step 3 Transmit 40 bit frame| A
    A -->|Step 4 Validate Checksum| C{VALID DATA}
    C -->|YES| D[PARSE HUMIDITY AND TEMPERATURE]
    C -->|NO| E[DISCARD READING PRINT ERROR]
    D --> F[SERIAL MONITOR OUTPUT]
    F -->|Step 5 Wait 15 seconds| G[TIMER MILLIS]
    G -->|Next Cycle| A
    H[10K PULL UP RESISTOR] -.->|Keeps bus idle HIGH| B
```

### 4.3 Circuit Wiring Schematic (Text-Based)

```
        +5V (Arduino)
          |
          |
        [10kΩ]    <-- Pull-up resistor
          |
          +-----------> D2 (Arduino Digital Pin 2)
          |
        DHT11 DATA PIN
          |
        DHT11 VCC  -----------> 5V
        DHT11 GND  -----------> GND
```

### 4.4 Timing Waveform for One Bit (Logical ‘1’ vs ‘0’)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master (Arduino)
    participant S as Slave (DHT11)
    M->>S: Start (LOW 18 ms)
    S-->>M: Response (LOW 80 us)
    S-->>M: Prepare (HIGH 80 us)
    Note over S: Each bit starts with 50 us LOW
    S-->>M: HIGH 26-28 us = LOGIC 0
    S-->>M: HIGH 70 us    = LOGIC 1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the pins of the DHT11 sensor and explain the function of the pull-up resistor used in its interfacing circuit.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The DHT11 has **4 pins**: VCC, DATA, NC (Not Connected), and GND.
- **VCC:** Receives 3.3–5.5 V DC supply.
- **DATA:** Single-wire bidirectional communication line.
- **NC:** Not connected — reserved.
- **GND:** Common ground reference.
- **Pull-up Resistor:** A **10 kΩ resistor** connected between DATA and VCC is required because the bus is **open-drain**. Without it, the line would float and produce garbage bits. It keeps the bus idle HIGH so both master and slave can pull it LOW only when transmitting.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the purpose of the checksum byte in a DHT11 data frame? Show how it is computed.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The checksum validates the integrity of the received data frame. The DHT11 transmits 40 bits — 4 data bytes and 1 checksum byte. The checksum is computed as:

$$
\text{Checksum} = (H_{int} + H_{dec} + T_{int} + T_{dec}) \mod 256
$$

If the received checksum byte equals this computed value, the data is valid. Otherwise, it must be discarded.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Students often *forget the checksum validation* and report garbage readings — **lose 2–3 marks**.
> 2. Many use `delay(15000)` without realizing it blocks the MCU — use `millis()` for full marks.
> 3. Not specifying the **baud rate (9600)** in `Serial.begin()` is a frequent oversight.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
*(a)* Explain the single-wire communication protocol used by the DHT11 sensor with a neat timing diagram. (7 marks)
*(b)* Write an Arduino program to read temperature and humidity from the DHT11 and print the values on the Serial Monitor every 15 seconds. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Protocol Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Start Signal:** The Arduino (master) pulls the data line LOW for **at least 18 ms** then releases it. This awakens the DHT11. (1 mark)
2. **Response Signal:** The DHT11 responds by pulling the line LOW for **80 µs**, then HIGH for **80 µs**, indicating it is ready to transmit. (2 marks)
3. **Data Transmission:** The sensor transmits **40 bits** = 5 bytes (Humidity-int, Humidity-dec, Temp-int, Temp-dec, Checksum). Each bit starts with a **50 µs LOW** pulse. (2 marks)
4. **Bit Encoding:** A HIGH pulse of **26–28 µs** = logic `0`; a HIGH pulse of **70 µs** = logic `1`. (1 mark)
5. **Timing Diagram:** *(sketch the standard waveform showing start, response, and 40 bit intervals)*. (1 mark)

**(b) Arduino Code (7 marks):** *(Refer to the complete reference code in Section 3.3 above. Key elements that must appear for full marks:)*
- `#include "DHT.h"` — (1 mark)
- `#define DHTTYPE DHT11` and pin definition — (1 mark)
- `dht.begin()` in `setup()` — (1 mark)
- Reading using `dht.readHumidity()` and `dht.readTemperature()` — (2 marks)
- **15-second interval** using `delay(15000)` **or** `millis()` — (1 mark)
- `Serial.print()` formatted output — (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
*(a)* Draw the interfacing circuit of the DHT11 with Arduino Uno. List its key electrical specifications. (7 marks)
*(b)* Given the raw bytes received from a DHT11 as [55, 0, 28, 0, 83], verify the checksum and decode the temperature and humidity values. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Interfacing Circuit (7 marks):**
- Circuit diagram: VCC → 5V, DATA → D2 with **10 kΩ pull-up**, GND → GND. *(2 marks)*
- Specifications to list:
  - Voltage: 3.3–5.5 V *(1 mark)*
  - Temperature range: 0–50 °C, accuracy ±2 °C *(1 mark)*
  - Humidity range: 20–90 %RH, accuracy ±5 %RH *(1 mark)*
  - 16-bit resolution, 1 Hz sampling *(1 mark)*
  - Single-wire digital interface *(1 mark)*

**(b) Data Decoding (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum of data bytes} &= 55 + 0 + 28 + 0 = 83 \\
\text{Checksum expected} &= 83 \mod 256 = 83 \\
\text{Checksum received} &= 83 \\
\therefore \text{Data is VALID.} &\;\checkmark \quad \text{(3 marks for checksum verification)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Humidity} &= 55 + 0.1 \times 0 = 55\,\%\ \text{RH} \quad \text{(2 marks)} \\
\text{Temperature} &= 28 + 0.1 \times 0 = 28\,^{\circ}\text{C} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DHT11** is a digital humidity + temperature sensor using a **single-wire protocol**.
- It transmits a **40-bit frame**: 16-bit humidity, 16-bit temperature, 8-bit checksum.
- Operating voltage: **3.3 – 5.5 V**; Temperature range: **0–50 °C**; Humidity range: **20–90 %RH**.
- A **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** between DATA and VCC is **mandatory**.
- Sampling rate **must be ≥ 1 second**; our 15-second interval is safe.
- **Checksum validation** is essential — discard readings if mismatch.
- Prefer `millis()` over `delay()` for **non-blocking timing**.
- Use `#include "DHT.h"` library and call `dht.begin()` in `setup()`.
- Functions: `dht.readHumidity()` returns `%`, `dht.readTemperature()` returns `°C`.
- Always **open Serial Monitor at 9600 baud** to match `Serial.begin(9600)`.
- The `.` in delay (e.g., `delay(15000)`) refers to **milliseconds** in Arduino.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use joystick and display the direction on the screen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Use joystick and display the direction on the screen # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **analog joystick module** is a 2-axis (X and Y) position-sensing input device that reports the displacement of a central lever from its rest (neutral) position as two independent analog voltage levels. Internally, it consists of **two orthogonally mounted potentiometers** (one for each axis) mechanically coupled to the lever, and an optional **tactile push-switch** that closes when the lever is pressed vertically downward. When interfaced with a microcontroller, the analog voltages are converted to discrete digital values by the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, and direction is then inferred by comparing the values against a set of calibrated thresholds. In the context of KTU PCCIL607, the experiment involves **interfacing a 2-axis joystick to a microcontroller (typically Arduino UNO / NodeMCU / Raspberry Pi Pico)**, sampling the X and Y analog channels, classifying the joystick position into one of **9 directional states** (Center, N, S, E, W, NE, NW, SE, SW), and **rendering the corresponding direction label on a display device** (16x2 LCD, 0.96" OLED, or Serial Monitor / Processing IDE GUI on a PC). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A joystick is fundamentally a *dual-potentiometer transducer*. The output of each potentiometer is a *voltage divider* whose wiper voltage is directly proportional to the angular displacement of the lever. The microcontroller never "knows" the direction as a symbol — it must mathematically *infer* it from the two analog readings using threshold comparison. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at the center of a circular dining table holding a long stick: - The **stick** is the joystick lever. - Your **left-right movement** tilts one potentiometer (X-axis) — like a see-saw that moves left/right. - Your **forward-backward movement** tilts the second potentiometer (Y-axis) — like a see-saw that moves forward/back. - The two see-saws are glued together at 90°, so any movement produces a *unique pair* of voltages. - The microcontroller is a **blindfolded person** who can only feel how much each see-saw is tilted (via ADC), and based on those two numbers, the person announces "You are facing North-East" or "You are at Center". A real-world analogy: the joystick in a **PlayStation controller** is the same device — Sony, Logitech, and industrial joysticks all rely on this dual-potentiometer principle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constants & Standards:** > - **Operating Voltage (Vcc):** Typically **+5V** (some modules accept 3.3V). > - **ADC Reference Voltage (Vref):** **+5V** for Arduino UNO (10-bit ADC). > - **ADC Resolution:** **10-bit** → 1024 discrete levels (0 to 1023). > - **Neutral / Resting Reading:** Both X and Y ≈ **512** (mid-scale, ±20 tolerance). > - **Switch Type:** **SPST momentary** (active LOW when pressed). > - **Mechanical Travel Angle:** ~ **±25° to ±30°** on each axis. ## 1.3 Direction Space — The 9 Logical Zones The joystick's 2D coordinate space is partitioned into **9 mutually exclusive zones** by two vertical and two horizontal threshold lines drawn at the resting value (≈ 512). | Zone | Logical Direction | X condition | Y condition | |------|------------------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | CENTER | 492 < X < 532 | 492 < Y < 532 | | 2 | NORTH (UP) | 492 < X < 532 | Y < 492 | | 3 | SOUTH (DOWN) | 492 < X < 532 | Y > 532 | | 4 | EAST (RIGHT) | X > 532 | 492 < Y < 532 | | 5 | WEST (LEFT) | X < 492 | 492 < Y < 532 | | 6 | NORTH-EAST | X > 532 | Y < 492 | | 7 | NORTH-WEST | X < 492 | Y < 492 | | 8 | SOUTH-EAST | X > 532 | Y > 532 | | 9 | SOUTH-WEST | X < 492 | Y > 532 | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition 1. **Mechanical Coupling** The joystick lever is mounted on a spherical gimbal. When the user tilts the lever along the X-axis, the wiper of Potentiometer-1 slides linearly along its resistive track. When tilted along the Y-axis, Potentiometer-2's wiper moves. This mechanical-to-electrical conversion is governed by the **voltage divider law**. 2. **Voltage Divider Action** Each potentiometer is connected between **Vcc (5V)** and **GND (0V)**. The wiper tap gives an output voltage: $$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{wiper}}{R_{total}}$$ When the lever is at rest, both wipers sit at the geometric midpoint, giving **Vout ≈ 2.5V** for each axis. 3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** The microcontroller's ADC quantizes Vout into a 10-bit integer. The standard ADC transfer function is: $$D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)\right)$$ For Arduino UNO: $V_{ref} = 5\text{V}$, $n = 10$, so $D_{max} = 1023$. 4. **Sampling & Digital Filtering (in code)** To suppress electrical noise, multiple samples are averaged. A **moving average filter** of window size $N$ is used: $$\bar{D}_k = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} D_{k-i}$$ 5. **Threshold-Based Direction Classification** A central dead-band (e.g., 492 < D < 532) is defined around the resting ADC value (512). Deviations from this band in each axis produce one of 9 directional states. The classification is a **piecewise linear decision tree** (essentially a small expert system). 6. **Display Mapping** Each classified state is mapped to a **string label** (e.g., "NORTH", "NE") and dispatched to the display driver function (LCD.print(), OLED.print(), or Serial.println()). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" behind the Dead-Band:** A real potentiometer at rest never outputs *exactly* 2.500V — manufacturing tolerances, contact resistance, and aging cause the resting value to drift by ±2 to ±5 LSB. Without a dead-band, the joystick would rapidly chatter between "CENTER" and a directional state when the user is *not* touching it, producing a flickering display. A dead-band of **±20 LSB** (i.e., 492–532) eliminates this. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | Unit | |--------|---------|-----------------|------| | $V_{out}$ | Wiper voltage of a potentiometer | $V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{w}}{R_{total}}$ | Volts (V) | | $D$ | ADC digital output code | $\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n}-1)$ | dimensionless integer | | $n$ | ADC bit-depth | 10 (Arduino UNO) | bits | | $V_{ref}$ | ADC reference voltage | 5.0 (UNO) or 3.3 (ESP/Pico) | V | | $D_{max}$ | Full-scale ADC code | $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$ | — | | $D_{rest}$ | Resting ADC value | $\dfrac{1023}{2} \approx 512$ | — | | $D_{tol}$ | Dead-band tolerance | $\pm 20$ (typical) | LSB | | $\bar{D}_k$ | Moving-average filtered ADC reading | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} D_{k-i}$ | — | | $V_{in}$ | Reconstructed input voltage | $\dfrac{D}{1023} \cdot V_{ref}$ | V | | $R_{total}$ | Potentiometer end-to-end resistance | 10 k$\Omega$ (module typical) | $\Omega$ | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Dual-axis joysticks are used in robotic arms (end-effector control), CNC machine jog wheels, pan-tilt camera gimbals, electric wheelchairs, drone flight controllers (mode-2 sticks), industrial crane controls, and rehabilitation robotics (prosthetic limb calibration). The 9-zone direction algorithm implemented here is identical in principle to the direction-decoding firmware inside every commercial gamepad. ## 2.3 Hardware Pin Map (Arduino UNO Reference) | Joystick Pin | Arduino UNO Pin | Function | |--------------|-----------------|----------| | VCC | 5V | Power supply | | GND | GND | Common ground | | VRx (X-axis) | A0 | Analog input (X) | | VRy (Y-axis) | A1 | Analog input (Y) | | SW (Switch) | D2 (with INPUT_PULLUP) | Digital input (button) | | LCD RS | D7 | Register Select | | LCD EN | D6 | Enable | | LCD D4 | D5 | Data bit 4 | | LCD D5 | D4 | Data bit 5 | | LCD D6 | D3 | Data bit 6 | | LCD D7 | D8 | Data bit 7 | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reconstructing Input Voltage from ADC Code **Given:** An Arduino UNO with $V_{ref} = 5.0\text{V}$, $n = 10$ bits, observed ADC code $D = 723$. **Step 1 — Identify the ADC transfer function.** The ADC maps an analog input voltage linearly across the digital range $[0, 2^n - 1]$: $$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n} - 1) \right\rfloor$$ **Step 2 — Invert the transfer function to solve for $V_{in}$.** Multiply both sides by $V_{ref}$ and divide by $(2^{n} - 1)$: $$V_{in} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Arduino UNO values.** $$V_{in} = D \cdot \frac{5.0}{1023}$$ **Step 4 — Plug in $D = 723$.** $$V_{in} = 723 \cdot \frac{5.0}{1023} = \frac{3615}{1023}$$ $$V_{in} \approx 3.534\text{ V}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret the result.** A reading of 723 on the X-axis is significantly above 512, so the lever is pushed toward the **EAST** side. The voltage 3.534V confirms the wiper sits roughly **70.7%** of the way from GND to Vcc along the resistive track. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing the Dead-Band Threshold in Volts **Given:** Tolerance of $\pm 20$ LSB around the resting code of 512. **Step 1 — Lower threshold code.** $$D_{low} = 512 - 20 = 492$$ **Step 2 — Upper threshold code.** $$D_{high} = 512 + 20 = 532$$ **Step 3 — Convert lower code to voltage.** $$V_{low} = 492 \cdot \frac{5.0}{1023} \approx 2.404\text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — Convert upper code to voltage.** $$V_{high} = 532 \cdot \frac{5.0}{1023} \approx 2.600\text{ V}$$ **Step 5 — Dead-band width in volts.** $$\Delta V_{db} = V_{high} - V_{low} \approx 0.196\text{ V}$$ This means any wiper voltage between 2.404V and 2.600V is treated as "center" — a 0.196V noise-immunity window that prevents chattering. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Direction Decision Logic (Piecewise Tree) Let $x$ and $y$ be the filtered ADC values, $T_{low} = 492$, $T_{high} = 532$, and let $D$ be the output direction label. The piecewise classification is defined as: $$D(x, y) = \begin{cases} \text{CENTER} & \text{if } T_{low} \leq x \leq T_{high} \text{ and } T_{low} \leq y \leq T_{high} \\ \text{NORTH} & \text{if } T_{low} \leq x \leq T_{high} \text{ and } y < T_{low} \\ \text{SOUTH} & \text{if } T_{low} \leq x \leq T_{high} \text{ and } y > T_{high} \\ \text{EAST} & \text{if } x > T_{high} \text{ and } T_{low} \leq y \leq T_{high} \\ \text{WEST} & \text{if } x < T_{low} \text{ and } T_{low} \leq y \leq T_{high} \\ \text{NORTH-EAST} & \text{if } x > T_{high} \text{ and } y < T_{low} \\ \text{NORTH-WEST} & \text{if } x < T_{low} \text{ and } y < T_{low} \\ \text{SOUTH-EAST} & \text{if } x > T_{high} \text{ and } y > T_{high} \\ \text{SOUTH-WEST} & \text{if } x < T_{low} \text{ and } y > T_{high} \end{cases}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustiveness check:** The 9 conditions above are *mutually exclusive* (no overlap) and *collectively exhaustive* (every point in the 2D plane maps to exactly one state). This is a complete partitioning of $\mathbb{R}^2$ — required for a robust direction classifier. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Code-to-Concept Mapping | Code Block (Algorithm) | Underlying Concept | Engineering Skill Tested | |------------------------|--------------------|--------------------------| | `pinMode(VRx, INPUT)` | Configure ADC channel as analog input | MCU peripheral configuration | | `analogRead(VRx)` | 10-bit successive-approximation ADC | Sensor signal acquisition | | `MovingAvg(xBuf, 10)` | Digital low-pass filtering | Noise suppression | | `if (x > THRESH_HIGH)` | Threshold comparison / dead-band | Decision logic | | Nested `if-else` tree | Piecewise state machine | Real-time classification | | `lcd.print("NORTH")` | Character LCD driving via 4-bit mode | Display interfacing (HD44780) | | `if (digitalRead(SW) == LOW)` | Pull-up activated switch debouncing | Digital input handling | | `delay(150)` | Software debounce | Timing control | ## 3.5 Reference Arduino Sketch (Lab-Ready) ```cpp #include LiquidCrystal lcd(7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 8); const int VRx = A0, VRy = A1, SW = 2; const int TH = 532, TL = 492; int xBuf[10], yBuf[10], idx = 0; void setup() { pinMode(SW, INPUT_PULLUP); lcd.begin(16, 2); lcd.print("Joystick Ready"); delay(1000); lcd.clear(); Serial.begin(9600); } int avg(int *buf) { long s = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) s += buf[i]; return s / 10; } void loop() { xBuf[idx] = analogRead(VRx); yBuf[idx] = analogRead(VRy); idx = (idx + 1) % 10; int x = avg(xBuf), y = avg(yBuf); String dir; if (x > TH && y < TL) dir = "NE"; else if (x < TL && y < TL) dir = "NW"; else if (x > TH && y > TH) dir = "SE"; else if (x < TL && y > TH) dir = "SW"; else if (x > TH) dir = "EAST"; else if (x < TL) dir = "WEST"; else if (y < TL) dir = "NORTH"; else if (y > TH) dir = "SOUTH"; else dir = "CENTER"; lcd.setCursor(0, 0); lcd.print("X:"); lcd.print(x); lcd.setCursor(8, 0); lcd.print("Y:"); lcd.print(y); lcd.setCursor(0, 1); lcd.print("Dir: "); lcd.print(dir); Serial.print(x); Serial.print(","); Serial.print(y); Serial.print(","); Serial.println(dir); delay(100); } ``` # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Joystick
Module"] --> B["X Pot
VRx"] A --> C["Y Pot
VRy"] A --> D["Push SW
SW"] B --> E["Arduino
ADC A0"] C --> F["Arduino
ADC A1"] D --> G["Arduino
D2 PullUp"] E --> H["Moving Avg
Filter N=10"] F --> H H --> I["Threshold
Classifier"] G --> J["Debounced
Button Read"] I --> K["Direction
Label Map"] J --> K K --> L["16x2 LCD
Display"] K --> M["Serial
Monitor PC"] L --> N["User Sees
NE / CENTER / SW"] M --> N ``` ## 4.2 Joystick Internal Schematic (Functional) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["+5V VCC"] --- B1["Pot X
10k ohm"] A --- B2["Pot Y
10k ohm"] B1 --- G["GND"] B2 --- G B1 -->|"wiper tap"| OUTx["VRx
to A0"] B2 -->|"wiper tap"| OUTy["VRy
to A1"] LEV["Lever Gimbal"] -.-> B1 LEV -.-> B2 LEV -.-> SWX["Tactile SW
to D2"] ``` ## 4.3 Program Flow — Direction Detection State Machine ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Power On / Reset]) --> init["Init LCD, Serial, Pins"] init --> loop1{"loop() tick"} loop1 --> readA["analogRead A0 → xBuf"] readA --> readB["analogRead A1 → yBuf"] readB --> filt["Compute xAvg, yAvg
over N=10 samples"] filt --> chkx{"x > 532?"} chkx -->|Yes| chky1{"y < 492?"} chkx -->|No| chklx{"x < 492?"} chky1 -->|Yes| ne[/"Display NE"/] chky1 -->|No| se[/"Display SE"/] chklx -->|Yes| chky2{"y < 492?"} chklx -->|No| chky3{"y < 492?"} chky2 -->|Yes| nw[/"Display NW"/] chky2 -->|No| sw[/"Display SW"/] chky3 -->|Yes| n[/"Display NORTH"/] chky3 -->|No| chky4{"y > 532?"} chky4 -->|Yes| s[/"Display SOUTH"/] chky4 -->|No| e1["check x"] e1 --> ex{"x > 532?"} ex -->|Yes| e[/"Display EAST"/] ex -->|No| w[/"Display WEST"/] ne --> delay["delay 100 ms"] se --> delay nw --> delay sw --> delay n --> delay s --> delay e --> delay w --> delay delay --> loop1 ``` ## 4.4 LCD Wiring (4-bit Mode) ```mermaid flowchart LR LCD["HD44780 16x2 LCD"] --- VSS["GND"] LCD --- VDD["+5V"] LCD --- V0["10k pot wiper
contrast"] LCD --- RS["D7"] LCD --- RW["GND"] LCD --- EN["D6"] LCD --- D4["D5"] LCD --- D5["D4"] LCD --- D6["D3"] LCD --- D7["D8"] LCD --- A["+5V anode"] LCD --- K["GND cathode"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Sensors Lab]`** — **3 Marks** **Q:** What is the principle of operation of an analog joystick module? Why are two potentiometers used? **Model Answer:** An analog joystick module operates on the **voltage divider principle** using two orthogonally mounted potentiometers. When the lever is tilted along the X-axis, the wiper of the first potentiometer slides proportionally, producing an output voltage between 0V and Vcc. Similarly, tilting along the Y-axis moves the wiper of the second potentiometer. Two potentiometers are used because a joystick requires **2-dimensional (X, Y) position sensing**. A single potentiometer can sense displacement along only one axis. By mounting two potentiometers at 90° to each other on a common gimbal, the joystick can independently report the user's displacement along both axes as two simultaneous analog voltages, which the microcontroller then converts to directional information. **(3 marks: 1 for principle, 1 for voltage divider, 1 for justification of two pots)** ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Sensors Lab]`** — **3 Marks** **Q:** Why is a dead-band of ±20 LSB applied around the resting ADC value of 512 in joystick direction detection? **Model Answer:** A dead-band of ±20 LSB (i.e., the codes 492 to 532 are treated as "CENTER") is applied for the following reasons: 1. **Noise suppression:** Real potentiometers exhibit thermal noise, contact resistance variations, and ADC quantization noise. Without a dead-band, the resting reading would randomly drift between 508, 514, 519 etc., causing the display to flicker between "CENTER" and an adjacent direction such as "NORTH" or "EAST" even when the user is not touching the joystick. 2. **Mechanical slack elimination:** Manufacturing tolerances and gimbal clearance mean the lever does not always return to the *exact* geometric center after release; a small offset is normal. 3. **Improved user experience:** A 0.196V dead-band window (≈ ±2% of Vcc) is wide enough to absorb drift yet narrow enough to feel responsive. It is the industry-standard for gamepad firmware. **(3 marks: 1 mark each for the three reasons above)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *Per KTU 2024 ESE convention, Part B questions carry 14 marks and typically contain two sub-parts of 7 marks each.* ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Sensors Lab]`** **Q (a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of a 2-axis analog joystick to an Arduino UNO. Label all pins and state the role of the ADC. **Model Answer (a):** The joystick module has **5 pins**: VCC, GND, VRx, VRy, and SW. | Pin | Connected To | Role | |-----|--------------|------| | VCC | Arduino 5V | Powers the two potentiometers | | GND | Arduino GND | Common reference | | VRx | Analog pin A0 | X-axis wiper voltage input | | VRy | Analog pin A1 | Y-axis wiper voltage input | | SW | Digital pin D2 (with `INPUT_PULLUP`) | Push-button switch | The **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** is a 10-bit successive-approximation converter built into the ATmega328P. It samples the analog voltage on A0 and A1, compares it against $V_{ref} = 5\text{V}$, and produces a digital code in the range 0 to 1023 according to: $$D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{5.0} \cdot 1023\right)$$ The block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The two potentiometers function as independent voltage dividers whose wiper voltages are routed to the ADC channels. The digital output codes are then read by the Arduino sketch and processed. **(7 marks: 2 for pin table, 2 for ADC role, 2 for diagram, 1 for transfer function)** --- **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Design the direction-detection algorithm for a joystick with resting value 512 and dead-band ±20. List the 9 directional states and the threshold conditions for each. Write the corresponding Arduino code segment. **Model Answer (b):** The 2D ADC plane is divided by two horizontal lines (y = 492 and y = 532) and two vertical lines (x = 492 and x = 532) into **9 rectangular zones**. Each zone corresponds to one direction. | # | Direction | Condition | |---|-----------|-----------| | 1 | CENTER | 492 ≤ x ≤ 532 and 492 ≤ y ≤ 532 | | 2 | NORTH | 492 ≤ x ≤ 532 and y < 492 | | 3 | SOUTH | 492 ≤ x ≤ 532 and y > 532 | | 4 | EAST | x > 532 and 492 ≤ y ≤ 532 | | 5 | WEST | x < 492 and 492 ≤ y ≤ 532 | | 6 | NE | x > 532 and y < 492 | | 7 | NW | x < 492 and y < 492 | | 8 | SE | x > 532 and y > 532 | | 9 | SW | x < 492 and y > 532 | The corresponding Arduino code segment: ```cpp int x = analogRead(A0); int y = analogRead(A1); String dir; if (x > 532 && y < 492) dir = "NE"; else if (x < 492 && y < 492) dir = "NW"; else if (x > 532 && y > 532) dir = "SE"; else if (x < 492 && y > 532) dir = "SW"; else if (x > 532) dir = "EAST"; else if (x < 492) dir = "WEST"; else if (y < 492) dir = "NORTH"; else if (y > 532) dir = "SOUTH"; else dir = "CENTER"; lcd.print(dir); ``` **(7 marks: 2 for zone table, 3 for code, 1 for direction mapping logic, 1 for display call)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Sensors Lab]`** **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the voltage divider action inside a joystick potentiometer. Derive the formula relating the wiper voltage to the wiper resistance, and compute the wiper voltage when the resistance from the wiper to GND is 3 kΩ in a 10 kΩ potentiometer connected across 5V. **Model Answer (a):** A potentiometer is a three-terminal resistor with a sliding wiper. When connected between Vcc and GND, it forms a **voltage divider** of two series resistors: $R_{w}$ (wiper-to-Vcc) and $R_{total} - R_{w}$ (wiper-to-GND). The wiper voltage is given by the standard voltage divider formula: $$V_{wiper} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{wiper-to-GND}}{R_{total}}$$ **Numerical computation:** Given $R_{wiper-to-GND} = 3\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{total} = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{cc} = 5\text{ V}$: $$V_{wiper} = 5 \cdot \frac{3000}{10000} = 5 \cdot 0.3 = 1.5\text{ V}$$ **Equivalent ADC code:** $$D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{1.5}{5.0} \cdot 1023\right) = \text{round}(306.9) = 307$$ This corresponds to the lever being pushed toward the **WEST** (or **SOUTH-WEST** depending on the other axis). **(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for numerical substitution, 2 for ADC code conversion and direction interpretation)** --- **Q (b)** [7 Marks] What is the purpose of a moving-average filter in joystick reading? For a window size N = 10, write the filter equation and explain how it improves direction stability. **Model Answer (b):** A **moving-average filter** is a simple low-pass digital filter used to suppress random noise superimposed on the joystick's analog signal. It works by averaging the most recent $N$ ADC samples: $$\bar{D}_k = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} D_{k-i}$$ For $N = 10$: $$\bar{D}_k = \frac{D_k + D_{k-1} + \dots + D_{k-9}}{10}$$ **How it improves direction stability:** 1. **Noise variance reduction:** If the noise on each sample is independent and has variance $\sigma^2$, the filtered output has variance $\sigma^2 / N$. For $N = 10$, the noise is reduced to 31.6% of its original RMS amplitude. 2. **Dead-band reinforcement:** Without averaging, a single noisy sample could push the reading across the threshold (e.g., 532) momentarily, causing a spurious "EAST" flash on the LCD. With averaging, transient spikes are smoothed out, so the direction label changes only when the lever is *intentionally* held in a new zone for a few iterations. 3. **Computational simplicity:** The filter requires only addition, subtraction, and one division by a constant — far cheaper than an IIR or Kalman filter, and well within the Arduino UNO's capability. **Trade-off:** Larger $N$ gives cleaner output but increases response latency. For human-operated joysticks, $N = 5$ to $N = 10$ is the sweet spot. **(7 marks: 2 for equation, 2 for variance reduction, 2 for dead-band reinforcement, 1 for trade-off discussion)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting the dead-band:** Many students write the direction logic as `if (x > 512)` directly. This is technically *correct* but loses 1 mark because the examiner expects a tolerance-aware threshold. Always cite a dead-band. > 2. **Confusing the two axes:** X and Y on a joystick module are *labeled* but the physical direction they correspond to depends on the module's mounting orientation. Examiners will deduct marks if you claim "VRx is horizontal" without stating the assumption. > 3. **No moving average:** Hard-coding a single `analogRead()` per loop is acceptable for a beginner lab, but for full marks on a 14-mark question, you must include *some* form of filtering. > 4. **Wrong ADC reference:** Stating 1024 instead of 1023 in the transfer function loses a mark — the ADC is 0-indexed (range is 0 to 1023, not 0 to 1024). > 5. **Mixing up voltage-divider polarity:** When asked for $V_{wiper}$, students frequently use $R_{wiper-to-VCC}$ instead of $R_{wiper-to-GND}$ in the denominator. The correct formula is $V_{wiper} = V_{cc} \cdot R_{bottom} / R_{total}$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Joystick Direction Display Experiment** > > - **Core device:** Dual potentiometer (10 kΩ) on a 2-axis gimbal + SPST switch. > - **Operating voltage:** **+5V**; **ADC reference:** **+5V** (UNO). > - **ADC resolution:** **10-bit → 0 to 1023** (NOT 0 to 1024). > - **Resting value:** **512 ± 20** (use a **dead-band** of 492 to 532). > - **Direction count:** **9 zones** (CENTER + 4 cardinal + 4 intercardinal). > - **Threshold-based piecewise classifier** is the standard algorithm. > - **Noise suppression:** **Moving average** with $N = 5$ to $10$ is recommended. > - **Display options:** 16x2 HD44780 LCD (4-bit mode), 0.96" SSD1306 OLED, or PC Serial Monitor. > - **Switch pin:** Connect SW to a digital input with `INPUT_PULLUP`; pressed = LOW. > - **Sampling rate:** 50 to 100 ms loop period is human-perceptually smooth. > - **Industrial relevance:** Used in CNC jog pendants, robotic arm teaching pendants, drone RC transmitters, and medical rehabilitation devices. > - **Common pitfall:** Never use `if (x > 512)` alone — always include a tolerance dead-band. > - **KTU marks distribution:** Code + algorithm ≈ 7 marks; Theory + diagram ≈ 7 marks. > - **Bonus tip:** Map the 9 zones to **arrow symbols** (↑ ↓ ← → ↖ ↗ ↙ ↘ •) on OLED for a professional GUI.

topics.title: Use Light Dependent Resistor (LDR) and control an LED that should switch-on/off depending on the light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Use Light Dependent Resistor (LDR) and control an LED that should switch-on/off depending on the light # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)**, also known as a **photoresistor** or **photoconductive cell**, is a passive semiconductor sensor whose electrical resistance varies inversely with the intensity of incident light falling upon its active surface. The LDR is fabricated from high-resistivity semiconductor materials such as **cadmium sulfide (CdS)** or **cadmium selenide (CdSe)**, whose photoconductive properties are governed by the **photoconductivity effect** described in semiconductor physics. In the context of the experiment "**Use LDR and control an LED that should switch ON/OFF depending on the light**," the objective is to design an embedded light-sensing system in which: - The **LDR** acts as the input transducer (light → resistance). - A **microcontroller** (typically Arduino Uno / ESP32) reads the LDR voltage via an **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** pin. - An **LED** is driven ON when ambient light falls below a defined darkness threshold, and switched OFF when ambient light is sufficient (e.g., automatic streetlamp logic). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCIL607 – Module 2):** > The student must demonstrate competency in (i) interfacing an analog sensor to a microcontroller, (ii) reading and threshold-processing an analog voltage, and (iii) driving a digital output actuator. This experiment directly tests all three skills in a single circuit. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English) Imagine a water tap whose opening size automatically adjusts based on how dark a room is: - **Bright room** → tap closes almost completely → very little "signal" flows → LED stays OFF. - **Dark room** → tap opens wide → strong "signal" flows → LED turns ON. The LDR is exactly this "automatic tap." The brighter the light, the more its internal "pipe" (resistance path) closes, restricting current. The darker it gets, the more the pipe opens, allowing current to flow freely. A microcontroller "reads" how open the pipe is, and decides whether to light the LED. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The LDR does NOT generate electricity from light (that is a photodiode/photovoltaic cell). It merely CHANGES its resistance in response to light. We must apply our own voltage and measure the change. ## 1.3 Standard Photoconductive Metrics (Bolded for Memory) - **Spectral Peak (λp):** ~550 nm for CdS LDR (coincides with human eye sensitivity — peak photopic vision at **555 nm**). - **Dark Resistance (R_Dark):** Typically **1 MΩ to 10 MΩ** (at 0 lux). - **Light Resistance (R_Light):** Typically **few hundred Ω to few kΩ** (at 100–1000 lux). - **Response Time:** Rise time ~20–100 ms; Fall time ~50–200 ms. - **Power Dissipation Rating:** Usually **100 mW to 250 mW** at 25 °C. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — The Photoconductivity Effect When photons of energy $E = h\nu$ strike the CdS semiconductor: 1. **Photon Absorption:** Photons with energy greater than the bandgap ($E_g \approx 2.4$ eV for CdS) are absorbed by valence electrons. 2. **Electron-Hole Pair Generation:** Bound electrons jump from the **valence band** to the **conduction band**, creating free electron–hole pairs. 3. **Increased Conductivity:** These free charge carriers drastically reduce the bulk resistivity of the material. 4. **Resistance Drop:** External circuit sees a drop from mega-ohms (dark) to kilo-ohms (light). ## 2.2 Voltage Divider — The Core Read Circuit The LDR is **never** connected directly to an ADC. It must be part of a **voltage divider** with a fixed reference resistor $R_1$: $$V_{OUT} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$$ > **Why this works:** As $R_{LDR}$ changes, $V_{OUT}$ changes proportionally. The microcontroller's ADC reads this varying voltage as a number between 0 and 1023 (10-bit resolution on Arduino). ## 2.3 LED Current-Limiting Equation The LED is driven through a current-limiting resistor $R_{LED}$ to prevent thermal destruction: $$R_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{F}}{I_{F}}$$ Where: - $V_{CC}$ = supply voltage (typically **+5 V**) - $V_F$ = LED forward voltage drop (typically **~2.0 V** for red, **~3.2 V** for white/blue) - $I_F$ = desired forward current (typically **10–20 mA**) ## 2.4 ADC Digital Conversion (Arduino Reference) $$N_{ADC} = \frac{V_{OUT}}{V_{REF}} \cdot (2^{n} - 1)$$ For Arduino Uno: $V_{REF} = 5$ V, $n = 10$, so: $$N_{ADC} = \frac{V_{OUT}}{5} \times 1023$$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---------|----------------|----------------------| | 1 | $V_{OUT} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$ | Divided output voltage at ADC pin | **Volts (V)** | | 2 | $R_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$ | LED current-limiting resistor | **Ω** (Ohms) | | 3 | $N_{ADC} = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{V_{REF}} \cdot 1023$ | 10-bit digital ADC count | **0 – 1023** | | 4 | $L_{lux} = f\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_{LDR}}\right)$ | Empirical lux–resistance relation | **lux** | | 5 | $P_{LDR} = I^2 \cdot R_{LDR}$ | Power dissipation in LDR | **Watts (W)** | | 6 | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy triggering conduction | **eV / J** | | 7 | $t_{response} = 0.5 \cdot (t_r + t_f)$ | Mean response time of CdS LDR | **~50–150 ms** | | 8 | $V_{threshold} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR,th}}$ | Threshold voltage for LED switching | **Volts (V)** | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This exact LDR + LED control loop is the foundation of **automatic street lighting systems**, **night lamps**, **camera exposure meters**, **solar panel sun-tracking safety interlocks**, and **IoT-based smart home lighting** (where the LDR is replaced by a digital lux sensor like BH1750 in industrial variants). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Designing the Reference Resistor $R_1$ We must choose $R_1$ such that the voltage divider has usable dynamic range. Rule of thumb: pick $R_1$ close to the **geometric mean** of $R_{Dark}$ and $R_{Light}$. **Given (from datasheet of a typical CdS LDR):** - $R_{Dark} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ (at 0 lux) - $R_{Light} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (at 1000 lux) - $V_{CC} = 5$ V **Step 1 — Compute geometric mean:** $$R_1 \approx \sqrt{R_{Dark} \cdot R_{Light}} = \sqrt{10^6 \times 10^3} = \sqrt{10^9} = 10^{4.5} \approx 31{,}623 \; \Omega$$ **Step 2 — Choose nearest standard E12 value:** $$R_1 = 33 \text{ k}\Omega \;\; (\text{standard value})$$ **Step 3 — Verify dynamic range at extremes:** In **bright light** ($R_{LDR} = 1$ kΩ): $$V_{OUT,light} = 5 \cdot \frac{33{,}000}{33{,}000 + 1{,}000} = 5 \cdot \frac{33}{34} \approx 4.85 \text{ V}$$ In **darkness** ($R_{LDR} = 1$ MΩ): $$V_{OUT,dark} = 5 \cdot \frac{33{,}000}{33{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000} = 5 \cdot \frac{33}{1033} \approx 0.16 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to ADC counts (Arduino):** $$N_{ADC,light} = \frac{4.85}{5} \times 1023 \approx 992 \;\; (\text{high, LED OFF})$$ $$N_{ADC,dark} = \frac{0.16}{5} \times 1023 \approx 33 \;\; (\text{low, LED ON})$$ **Step 5 — Choose threshold (mid-point with hysteresis margin):** $$N_{threshold} = \frac{992 + 33}{2} \approx 512 \;\; \text{(use 500 in code for safety margin)}$$ ## 3.2 Derivative 2 — LED Current-Limiting Resistor **Given:** - $V_{CC} = 5$ V (Arduino digital output HIGH) - $V_F = 2.0$ V (red LED, typical) - $I_F = 15$ mA (safe operating current) **Step 1 — Apply KVL across LED branch:** $$V_{CC} = V_F + I_F \cdot R_{LED}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $R_{LED}$:** $$R_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{5 - 2}{0.015} = \frac{3}{0.015} = 200 \; \Omega$$ **Step 3 — Choose standard E12 value:** $$R_{LED} = 220 \; \Omega \;\; (\text{safest standard value, gives } I_F \approx 13.6 \text{ mA})$$ ## 3.3 Derivative 3 — Threshold Hysteresis (Anti-Flicker Design) If the LDR value hovers near the threshold, the LED will oscillate. We add **hysteresis** of $\pm \Delta N$ counts: $$N_{ON \rightarrow OFF} = N_{threshold} + \Delta N$$ $$N_{OFF \rightarrow ON} = N_{threshold} - \Delta N$$ For $\Delta N = 50$, the LED turns ON when ADC < **450** and turns OFF only when ADC > **550** — a stable dead-band of 100 counts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** Examiners often test whether you included hysteresis in your code. Mentioning "software debounce / hysteresis" earns bonus marks even if not explicitly asked. ## 3.4 Reference Arduino Implementation The complete embedded firmware for this lab experiment: ```cpp /* LDR-based Automatic LED Controller * Course: PCCIL607 - Sensors and Devices Lab * Module 2 - Experiment 2 */ const int LDR_PIN = A0; // Analog input from LDR voltage divider const int LED_PIN = 9; // PWM-capable digital output to LED const int THRESHOLD = 500; // ADC threshold (tune experimentally) const int HYST = 50; // Hysteresis band to prevent flicker const int SAMPLE_AVG = 10; // Number of samples for averaging bool ledState = false; void setup() { pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT); pinMode(LDR_PIN, INPUT); Serial.begin(9600); } int readLDR_Averaged() { long sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < SAMPLE_AVG; i++) { sum += analogRead(LDR_PIN); delay(2); } return (int)(sum / SAMPLE_AVG); } void loop() { int ldrValue = readLDR_Averaged(); // Hysteresis-based state machine if (ledState == false && ldrValue < (THRESHOLD - HYST)) { ledState = true; // DARK -> LED ON } else if (ledState == true && ldrValue > (THRESHOLD + HYST)) { ledState = false; // BRIGHT -> LED OFF } digitalWrite(LED_PIN, ledState ? HIGH : LOW); Serial.print("LDR ADC: "); Serial.print(ldrValue); Serial.print(" | LED: "); Serial.println(ledState ? "ON" : "OFF"); delay(100); } ``` > **Code Walkthrough for Viva:** > - `analogRead(A0)` returns a 10-bit value (0–1023) proportional to $V_{OUT}$. > - When light increases → $R_{LDR}$ decreases → $V_{OUT}$ rises → ADC value increases. > - When light decreases → $R_{LDR}$ increases → $V_{OUT}$ falls → ADC value decreases. > - Therefore: **Low ADC = Dark**, **High ADC = Bright** (with the divider topology shown). > - The LED is driven ON only when ADC < (THRESHOLD − HYST). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Schematic — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR VCC["VCC 5V"] GND["GND 0V"] LDR["LDR
Photoresistor CdS"] R1["R1 = 33 kΩ
Fixed Resistor"] RLED["RLED = 220 Ω
Current Limiter"] LED["LED
Red λ = 660 nm"] A0["Arduino A0
ADC Input"] D9["Arduino D9
Digital Output"] CTRL["MCU
ATmega328P
10-bit ADC"] MON["Serial Monitor
9600 baud"] VCC --- LDR LDR --- A0 A0 --- R1 R1 --- GND D9 --- RLED RLED --- LED LED --- GND A0 -. ADC read .-> CTRL D9 -. GPIO write .-> CTRL CTRL -. UART TX .-> MON ``` ## 4.2 State Machine — LED Switching Logic ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> LED_OFF LED_OFF --> LED_ON : ADC value less than 450
DARK detected LED_ON --> LED_OFF : ADC value greater than 550
BRIGHT detected LED_OFF --> LED_OFF : ADC between 450 and 550
HOLD OFF LED_ON --> LED_ON : ADC between 450 and 550
HOLD ON ``` ## 4.3 Signal Flow — From Light to LED State ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Ambient light hits LDR surface"] S2["Step 2: Photons liberate electron-hole pairs in CdS"] S3["Step 3: RLDR changes inversely with lux"] S4["Step 4: Voltage divider VOUT shifts"] S5["Step 5: Arduino ADC samples VOUT 10 times and averages"] S6{"Step 6: Compare with
THRESHOLD ± HYST ?"} S7["Step 7a: Drive D9 HIGH -> LED ON"] S8["Step 7b: Drive D9 LOW -> LED OFF"] S9["Step 8: Print state to Serial Monitor"] S10["Step 9: Wait 100 ms, loop"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 S6 -- Dark detected --> S7 --> S9 --> S10 S6 -- Bright detected --> S8 --> S9 --> S10 S10 --> S5 ``` ## 4.4 Component Pinout Reference Table | Component | Pin / Terminal | Connection Point | Notes | |-----------|---------------|------------------|-------| | LDR | Terminal 1 | Arduino **+5V** | One end tied to supply | | LDR | Terminal 2 | Arduino **A0** AND R1 top | Junction = ADC node | | R1 (33 kΩ) | Terminal 1 | Arduino **A0** (same as LDR pin 2) | Forms divider with LDR | | R1 (33 kΩ) | Terminal 2 | Arduino **GND** | Completes divider | | LED (Anode, +) | — | Arduino **D9** via R_LED | Current flows in | | R_LED (220 Ω) | — | Between D9 and LED anode | Limits current | | LED (Cathode, −) | — | Arduino **GND** | Current returns | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] **Define an LDR. State two of its practical applications.** **Model Answer:** A Light Dependent Resistor (LDR) is a passive semiconductor device whose resistance decreases as the intensity of incident light increases, based on the principle of **photoconductivity**. It is typically made of cadmium sulfide (CdS) and shows high resistance (mega-ohms) in darkness and low resistance (kilo-ohms) under bright light. **Applications (any two):** 1. **Automatic street-lighting** systems that switch ON at dusk and OFF at dawn. 2. **Camera exposure meters** for automatic shutter-speed selection. 3. **Night-lamps / emergency lights** in homes. 4. **Solar tracker safety interlocks** to detect cloudy conditions. 5. **Smoke/flame detectors** in industrial fire-alarm systems (as a photo-flame sensor). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Why is a voltage divider required when interfacing an LDR with a microcontroller ADC? What is the role of the fixed reference resistor?** **Model Answer:** A microcontroller ADC measures **voltage**, not resistance. The LDR is a variable resistor; it cannot directly produce a measurable voltage on its own. A **voltage divider** is required to convert the resistance change of the LDR into a proportional voltage change that the ADC can digitize. The fixed reference resistor $R_1$ (here 33 kΩ) is connected between the ADC pin and ground (or between +V and the ADC pin, depending on topology). The voltage at the ADC pin is: $$V_{OUT} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$$ As $R_{LDR}$ varies with light, $V_{OUT}$ varies, and the ADC reads this as a digital value between 0 and 1023. The choice of $R_1$ is critical: it should be near the geometric mean of $R_{Dark}$ and $R_{Light}$ to maximize the dynamic range and ensure good sensitivity across the operating light range. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting units** on numerical answers (e.g., writing 33 instead of 33 kΩ). Loss: 0.5–1 mark per error. > 2. **Confusing dark/light resistance assignment** — many students wrongly state LDR resistance increases with light. Loss: 1–2 marks. > 3. **Not justifying choice of $R_1$** — merely writing "I used 10 kΩ" without the geometric-mean argument. Loss: 1 mark. > 4. **No mention of hysteresis or debounce** in code-based questions. Loss: 1 mark. > 5. **Sketching the circuit without a current-limiting resistor on the LED** — practical examiners flag this. Loss: 1 mark. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Design Question **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCIL607]** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain how an LDR and an LED can be interfaced to an Arduino Uno to automatically switch the LED ON in darkness and OFF in bright light. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design the voltage-divider reference resistor for a CdS LDR whose $R_{Dark} = 1$ MΩ and $R_{Light} = 1$ kΩ, supplied by 5 V. Also calculate the LED current-limiting resistor for a red LED ($V_F = 2$ V, $I_F = 15$ mA). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-A (a) **Circuit Diagram (textual representation, refer to Section 4.1 for full schematic):** - LDR connected between **+5V** and **A0** (analog input pin of Arduino Uno). - Fixed resistor $R_1 = 33$ kΩ connected between **A0** and **GND**, forming a voltage divider. - Red LED anode connected to digital pin **D9** through current-limiting resistor $R_{LED} = 220$ Ω. - LED cathode connected to **GND**. **Working Principle:** 1. In **bright light**, $R_{LDR}$ is low (~1 kΩ), so most of the 5 V drops across $R_1$ and the ADC reads a high value (~4.85 V → ADC ≈ 992). The Arduino's program detects "bright" and drives D9 **LOW**, switching the LED OFF. 2. In **darkness**, $R_{LDR}$ becomes very high (~1 MΩ), so nearly all 5 V drops across the LDR itself. The ADC pin sees a very small voltage (~0.16 V → ADC ≈ 33). The Arduino detects "dark" and drives D9 **HIGH**, turning the LED ON. 3. A **threshold value of 500** is set in the program (mid-point), with a **hysteresis of ±50** counts to prevent rapid flickering when light intensity hovers around the threshold. 4. A **moving average of 10 samples** is used to smooth out noise from fluorescent flickering or transient shadows. **Code (refer to Section 3.4):** - `analogRead(A0)` is called 10 times and averaged. - The averaged value is compared with `THRESHOLD ± HYST`. - `digitalWrite(9, HIGH/LOW)` drives the LED accordingly. - Diagnostic output is sent via `Serial.print()` for monitoring. --- #### Model Solution to Q-A (b) **Step 1 — Choose $R_1$ using geometric mean:** $$R_1 = \sqrt{R_{Dark} \cdot R_{Light}} = \sqrt{1 \times 10^6 \cdot 1 \times 10^3} = \sqrt{10^9} \approx 31{,}623 \; \Omega$$ Standard E12 nearest value → **$R_1 = 33$ kΩ**. **Step 2 — Verify voltages at extremes:** $$V_{OUT,light} = 5 \cdot \frac{33{,}000}{33{,}000 + 1{,}000} = 4.85 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{OUT,dark} = 5 \cdot \frac{33{,}000}{33{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000} = 0.16 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Compute LED current-limiting resistor:** $$R_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{5 - 2}{0.015} = 200 \; \Omega$$ Standard E12 nearest value → **$R_{LED} = 220$ Ω** (gives $I_F \approx 13.6$ mA, safe). **Step 4 — Choose threshold for software:** $$N_{threshold} = \frac{992 + 33}{2} \approx 512 \;\; \text{(use 500 with } \pm 50 \text{ hysteresis)}$$ **Final Component List:** - LDR: CdS photoresistor - $R_1 = 33$ kΩ (¼ W, ±5 %) - $R_{LED} = 220$ Ω (¼ W, ±5 %) - LED: Red, $V_F = 2$ V, $I_F = 15$ mA - Arduino Uno, USB cable, breadboard, jumper wires --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Question **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCIL607]** **(a)** Explain the working principle of an LDR. With relevant energy-band diagrams (qualitative), describe how photoconductivity occurs in cadmium sulfide. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An LDR ($R_{Dark} = 500$ kΩ, $R_{Light} = 800$ Ω) is connected in series with $R_1 = 10$ kΩ across 5 V. Calculate the output voltage and the corresponding Arduino ADC value (10-bit, $V_{REF} = 5$ V) under (i) bright light and (ii) complete darkness. Comment on the suitability of this divider for the LED-control application. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-B (a) **Working Principle:** The LDR operates on the principle of **photoconductivity** in semiconductors. Its active material is a thin film of cadmium sulfide (CdS), a II–VI compound semiconductor with a bandgap energy of approximately $E_g = 2.4$ eV. **Energy Band Explanation (qualitative):** - In the **dark** (no photons), all electrons in the CdS are bound in the valence band; the conduction band is empty. There are very few free charge carriers, so the material behaves like an insulator with high resistance (~MΩ). - When **light** (photons) strikes the surface, photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ are absorbed. Valence-band electrons gain this energy and are excited into the conduction band, leaving behind holes in the valence band. - These free electron–hole pairs act as charge carriers. The conductivity $\sigma$ of the material increases as: $$\sigma = q \cdot (n \cdot \mu_n + p \cdot \mu_p)$$ where $n$ and $p$ are the electron and hole concentrations, $\mu_n$ and $\mu_p$ are their mobilities, and $q$ is the elementary charge. - As light intensity increases, more electron–hole pairs are generated, $\sigma$ increases, and the resistance $R = L / (\sigma \cdot A)$ decreases. - In a CdS LDR, the peak sensitivity is at **λ ≈ 550 nm** (green-yellow), closely matching the photopic response of the human eye, which makes it ideal for visible-light applications like automatic lamps. **Material Advantages of CdS:** 1. Bandgap (~2.4 eV) is in the visible range — only visible light triggers conduction. 2. High dark resistance provides a wide dynamic range. 3. Spectral response matches human eye response — natural for lighting control. --- #### Model Solution to Q-B (b) **Given:** $R_1 = 10$ kΩ, $V_{CC} = 5$ V, $R_{Light} = 800$ Ω, $R_{Dark} = 500$ kΩ, Arduino 10-bit ADC, $V_{REF} = 5$ V. **Topology note:** In this problem, LDR is in **series with $R_1$**, with the junction at the ADC pin. The exact arrangement determines which formula to use. Assuming the LDR is connected from +5V to A0, and $R_1$ from A0 to GND (same topology as the experiment), we use: $$V_{OUT} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$$ **Case (i) — Bright light ($R_{LDR} = 800$ Ω):** $$V_{OUT,light} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 800} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}800} = 4.63 \text{ V}$$ $$N_{ADC,light} = \frac{4.63}{5} \times 1023 = 947$$ **Case (ii) — Complete darkness ($R_{LDR} = 500$ kΩ):** $$V_{OUT,dark} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 500{,}000} = 5 \cdot \frac{10}{510} = 0.098 \text{ V}$$ $$N_{ADC,dark} = \frac{0.098}{5} \times 1023 = 20$$ **Suitability Comment:** The voltage swing from **947 (bright) to 20 (dark)** spans nearly the full 0–1023 ADC range, providing excellent resolution and a clear threshold. The chosen $R_1 = 10$ kΩ is close to the geometric mean: $$R_{1,opt} = \sqrt{500{,}000 \times 800} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^8} \approx 20 \; \text{k}\Omega$$ Since 10 kΩ is within an order of magnitude of the optimal 20 kΩ, the divider is **highly suitable** for the LED-control application. A threshold of $N_{threshold} \approx 500$ with $\pm 50$ hysteresis would work reliably. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam** - [ ] **LDR = Light Dependent Resistor = Photoresistor**; resistance **decreases** with light. - [ ] LDR does NOT generate voltage — it only changes resistance; hence a **voltage divider is mandatory**. - [ ] Material: **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)**; bandgap ~**2.4 eV**; spectral peak ~**550 nm** (matches human eye). - [ ] $R_{Dark}$ ≈ **1–10 MΩ**; $R_{Light}$ ≈ **few hundred Ω to few kΩ**. - [ ] Voltage divider formula: $V_{OUT} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$. - [ ] LED current-limiting: $R_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$; for red LED at 5 V use **220 Ω**. - [ ] Arduino ADC: 10-bit → range **0 to 1023**; $N_{ADC} = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{V_{REF}} \times 1023$. - [ ] Choose $R_1$ near **geometric mean** of $R_{Dark}$ and $R_{Light}$ for maximum dynamic range. - [ ] Always include **hysteresis** (±50 counts) in threshold comparisons to prevent LED flicker. - [ ] Use **sample averaging** (≥10 samples) to filter noise from fluorescent lights and shadows. - [ ] Common applications: **automatic streetlamps, night-lamps, camera meters, solar tracker interlocks**. - [ ] Photoconductivity equation: $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ — conductivity rises with light. - [ ] **Photodiode ≠ LDR** — photodiode is a PN junction that generates photocurrent; LDR is a passive resistor. > **End of Module 2 — Experiment 2 Notes**

topics.title: Create a traffic light signal with three colored lights (Red, Orange and Green) with a duty cycle of 5-2-10 seconds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Create a traffic light signal with three colored lights (Red, Orange and Green) with a duty cycle of 5-2-10 seconds # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Traffic Light Controller (TLC):** A sequential embedded-system application that drives three discrete Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs) — **Red, Orange (Amber/Yellow), and Green** — in a deterministic, time-multiplexed pattern using a programmable microcontroller (Arduino Uno) to regulate the right-of-way at an intersection. In the context of **PCCIL607 (Sensors and Devices Lab)**, this experiment is a foundational **actuator-driving** experiment that demonstrates the practical implementation of: - **Digital Output Control** (HIGH/LOW logic levels on GPIO pins) - **Sequential State Machines** (cyclic state transitions) - **Time-Multiplexed Switching** (using software `delay()` or hardware timers) The **Duty Cycle Specification 5-2-10 seconds** is interpreted in standard KTU convention as: | Light State | ON-Duration | Symbolism | |-------------|-------------|-----------| | 🔴 **Red** | **5 s** | STOP | | 🟡 **Orange (Amber)** | **2 s** | READY / TRANSITION | | 🟢 **Green** | **10 s** | GO | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of the Arduino as a Traffic Police Officer with a 3-Color Baton.** > > Imagine a traffic police officer standing at a junction holding three flags — a red flag, a yellow flag, and a green flag. The officer has a strict, non-negotiable schedule: > 1. Waving the **red flag for 5 seconds** (telling all vehicles: "STOP!"). > 2. Switching to the **yellow flag for 2 seconds** (warning: "Get ready, the light is about to change!"). > 3. Then waving the **green flag for 10 seconds** (announcing: "GO! You may proceed"). > 4. The officer repeats this loop forever. > > The **Arduino microcontroller** is that digital traffic police officer, and the **LEDs** are the three flags. The `delay()` function is the stopwatch the officer uses between flag-switches. --- ## 1.3 Hardware & Software Components Required > [!NOTE] > **Bill of Materials (BOM) for the Experiment** | # | Component | Specification | Quantity | Function | |---|-----------|---------------|----------|----------| | 1 | Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) | 16 MHz, 14 digital I/O | 1 | Master controller | | 2 | Red LED | V_f ≈ **2.0 V**, I_f ≈ **20 mA** | 1 | Stop indicator | | 3 | Orange/Yellow LED | V_f ≈ **2.1 V**, I_f ≈ **20 mA** | 1 | Transition warning | | 4 | Green LED | V_f ≈ **2.2 V**, I_f ≈ **20 mA** | 1 | Go indicator | | 5 | Resistors | **220 Ω** (¼ W, ±5 %) | 3 | Current limiting | | 6 | Breadboard | 830 tie-points | 1 | Prototyping | | 7 | Jumper Wires (M-M) | 20 cm | 6 | Interconnections | | 8 | USB Cable | Type-A to Type-B | 1 | Power + Programming | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Engineering Metric:** The **5 V** logic level of the Arduino Uno digital pin is the reference supply voltage for all LED-driving calculations in this experiment. --- ## 1.4 Learning Outcomes (Aligned with KTU 2024 CO Mapping) Upon successful completion of this experiment, the student will be able to: - **CO1:** Identify and interface discrete LEDs to a microcontroller using current-limiting resistors. - **CO2:** Develop sequential time-multiplexed output programs using Arduino IDE. - **CO3:** Apply **Ohm's Law** to calculate the current-limiting resistance. - **CO4:** Verify timing accuracy using serial monitor and `millis()`-based debugging. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Sequential State Machine A traffic light is mathematically modeled as a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** with **3 stable states** and **1 cyclic transition** between them. Each state corresponds to a unique combination of LED ON/OFF logic levels. ### 2.1.1 State Transition Logic > [!NOTE] > **State Definitions (S0, S1, S2) with TTL Logic Levels (1 = ON, 0 = OFF)** | State | Duration (s) | Red | Orange | Green | Boolean Output Word | |-------|--------------|-----|--------|-------|---------------------| | **S0** (STOP) | **5** | **1** | 0 | 0 | `100` | | **S1** (READY) | **2** | 0 | **1** | 0 | `010` | | **S2** (GO) | **10** | 0 | 0 | **1** | `001` | | **S0 → S1 → S2 → S0** | — | — | — | — | Cyclic loop | - **Transition 1:** S0 → S1 triggered after **5000 ms** (timer expiry of state S0). - **Transition 2:** S1 → S2 triggered after **2000 ms** (timer expiry of state S1). - **Transition 3:** S2 → S0 triggered after **10000 ms** (timer expiry of state S2). --- ## 2.2 Why the Numbers 5, 2, 10? (Traffic Engineering Insight) - **5 seconds of Red:** Sufficient time for a slow-moving vehicle (≈ 30 km/h) to come to a complete halt from a visibility distance of ~30 m. - **2 seconds of Orange (Amber):** A psychological and safety buffer — neither fully GO nor fully STOP; warns drivers of an imminent transition. - **10 seconds of Green:** Longest duration to permit a queue of vehicles to clear the intersection (assuming ≈ 2 s per vehicle departure and a queue of 4–5 vehicles). --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are testable in KTU ESE and must be memorized.** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Unit | Application in Experiment | |---|--------------|----------|----------------------|------|---------------------------| | 1 | **Ohm's Law** | $\displaystyle V = I \cdot R$ | V = Voltage across resistor, I = Current, R = Resistance | V, A, Ω | Backbone of LED current-limiting calculation | | 2 | **LED Series Resistor** | $\displaystyle R = \frac{V_{s} - V_{f}}{I_{f}}$ | $V_s$ = Supply (5 V), $V_f$ = LED forward voltage, $I_f$ = LED forward current | Ω | Resistor sizing for Red/Orange/Green | | 3 | **Power Dissipation in R** | $\displaystyle P_{R} = I^{2} \cdot R$ | $P_R$ = Power rating required | W | Choosing resistor wattage (¼ W safe) | | 4 | **Total Cycle Period** | $\displaystyle T_{cycle} = T_{R} + T_{O} + T_{G}$ | $T_R, T_O, T_G$ = individual ON-times | s | $5 + 2 + 10 = 17$ s | | 5 | **Cycles per Hour** | $\displaystyle N = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$ | N = number of full cycles in 1 hour | cycles/hr | $\approx 211.76$ cycles/hr | | 6 | **Duty Cycle (%)** | $\displaystyle D_{\%} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100$ | $T_{ON}$ = ON-time of a given LED | % | Red = 29.41 %, Orange = 11.76 %, Green = 58.82 % | | 7 | **Arduino Delay Conversion** | $\displaystyle t_{ms} = t_{s} \times 1000$ | $t_{ms}$ = delay in ms, $t_s$ = delay in s | ms | `delay(5000)` for 5 s | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar `|` has been intentionally replaced with the LaTeX `\vert` / `\mid` operator in formulas like $V_s \mid V_f$ is avoided. Always use `\mid` to maintain markdown table integrity. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart City Infrastructure:** Forms the basis of every modern adaptive signal system (SCATS, SCOOT). - **Industrial Automation:** Conveyor-belt sequenced start-stop indicators use the **exact same FSM logic**. - **Automotive Embedded Systems:** Body Control Modules (BCMs) use time-multiplexed LED drivers for dashboard indicators. - **Robotics:** Multi-state warning lights on autonomous mobile robots. # 3.
