Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Current-Limiting Resistor Selection ### Given Data (Arduino + Standard 5 mm LED) - Arduino digital HIGH voltage: $V_{s} = 5\text{ V}$ - LED forward voltage (Red): $V_{f,R} \approx 2.0\text{ V}$ - LED forward voltage (Orange): $V_{f,O} \approx 2.1\text{ V}$ - LED forward voltage (Green): $V_{f,G} \approx 2.2\text{ V}$ - Desired safe forward current: $I_{f} = 15\text{ mA} = 0.015\text{ A}$ (within the 20 mA absolute max of an Arduino pin) ### Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the LED–Resistor loop $$ V_{s} = V_{R} + V_{f} $$ Solving for the voltage drop across the resistor: $$ V_{R} = V_{s} - V_{f} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values for the RED LED $$ V_{R,R} = 5\text{ V} - 2.0\text{ V} = 3.0\text{ V} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to find the resistance $$ R = \frac{V_{R}}{I_{f}} $$ $$ R_{R} = \frac{3.0\text{ V}}{0.015\text{ A}} = 200\text{ Ω} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the same procedure for the ORANGE LED $$ V_{R,O} = 5\text{ V} - 2.1\text{ V} = 2.9\text{ V} $$ $$ R_{O} = \frac{2.9\text{ V}}{0.015\text{ A}} = 193.33\text{ Ω} $$ ### Step 5 — Apply the same procedure for the GREEN LED $$ V_{R,G} = 5\text{ V} - 2.2\text{ V} = 2.8\text{ V} $$ $$ R_{G} = \frac{2.8\text{ V}}{0.015\text{ A}} = 186.67\text{ Ω} $$ ### Step 6 — Choose the nearest E12 standard resistor value The closest E12 standard value greater than or equal to **200 Ω** is **220 Ω**, which is the de-facto industry standard for Arduino LED-driving experiments. A 220 Ω resistor keeps the current slightly below 15 mA, providing an additional safety margin. ### Step 7 — Verify the actual operating current with R = 220 Ω For the RED LED: $$ I_{actual,R} = \frac{V_{s} - V_{f,R}}{R} = \frac{5 - 2.0}{220} = 0.01364\text{ A} = 13.64\text{ mA} $$ This is **safely below** the Arduino's per-pin 20 mA limit and the LED's 20 mA maximum. ✔ For the GREEN LED (worst case): $$ I_{actual,G} = \frac{5 - 2.2}{220} = 0.01273\text{ A} = 12.73\text{ mA} $$ ✔ Well within safe operating limits. ### Step 8 — Power dissipation in the 220 Ω resistor $$ P_{R} = I^{2} \cdot R = (0.01364)^{2} \cdot 220 = 0.0409\text{ W} = 40.9\text{ mW} $$ A ¼ W (250 mW) resistor provides a **6× safety margin**. ✔ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Timing & Cycles > [!IMPORTANT] > **Timing Derivations for the 5-2-10 s Duty Cycle** | # | Parameter | Formula | Numerical Result | |---|-----------|---------|------------------| | 1 | Total cycle period | $T_{cycle} = 5 + 2 + 10$ | $T_{cycle} = 17\text{ s}$ | | 2 | Cycles per minute | $N_{min} = \frac{60}{17}$ | $N_{min} \approx 3.53$ cycles/min | | 3 | Cycles per hour | $N_{hr} = \frac{3600}{17}$ | $N_{hr} \approx 211.76$ cycles/hr | | 4 | Red duty % | $D_{R} = \frac{5}{17} \times 100$ | $D_{R} \approx 29.41\text{ \%}$ | | 5 | Orange duty % | $D_{O} = \frac{2}{17} \times 100$ | $D_{O} \approx 11.76\text{ \%}$ | | 6 | Green duty % | $D_{G} = \frac{10}{17} \times 100$ | $D_{G} \approx 58.82\text{ \%}$ | | 7 | Arduino delay (Red) | $t_{ms} = 5 \times 1000$ | `delay(5000)` | | 8 | Arduino delay (Orange) | $t_{ms} = 2 \times 1000$ | `delay(2000)` | | 9 | Arduino delay (Green) | $t_{ms} = 10 \times 1000$ | `delay(10000)` | --- ## 3.3 Complete Pin-Mapping & Wiring Logic | Arduino Pin | Component | Resistor | Other End | |-------------|-----------|----------|-----------| | Digital Pin **13** | Red LED (Anode) | 220 Ω in series | LED Cathode → **GND** | | Digital Pin **12** | Orange LED (Anode) | 220 Ω in series | LED Cathode → **GND** | | Digital Pin **11** | Green LED (Anode) | 220 Ω in series | LED Cathode → **GND** | > [!NOTE] > **Polarity Rule:** The **anode** (longer lead, +) of the LED is connected to the Arduino pin (current source). The **cathode** (shorter lead, flat side, −) is connected to **GND**. Reversing the polarity will block current flow and the LED will not glow. --- ## 3.4 The Complete Arduino Sketch (C/C++) ```cpp /* ============================================================ * Experiment : Traffic Light Signal (5-2-10 sec duty cycle) * Course : PCCIL607 - Sensors and Devices Lab * Board : Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) * ============================================================ */ int redPin = 13; // Red LED on digital pin 13 int orangePin = 12; // Orange LED on digital pin 12 int greenPin = 11; // Green LED on digital pin 11 void setup() { // Configure all three LED pins as OUTPUT pinMode(redPin, OUTPUT); pinMode(orangePin, OUTPUT); pinMode(greenPin, OUTPUT); } void loop() { /* ---------- STATE S0 : RED ON for 5 seconds ---------- */ digitalWrite(redPin, HIGH); // Red ON digitalWrite(orangePin, LOW); // Orange OFF digitalWrite(greenPin, LOW); // Green OFF delay(5000); // Wait 5 seconds = 5000 ms /* ---------- STATE S1 : ORANGE ON for 2 seconds ------- */ digitalWrite(redPin, LOW); // Red OFF digitalWrite(orangePin, HIGH); // Orange ON digitalWrite(greenPin, LOW); // Green OFF delay(2000); // Wait 2 seconds = 2000 ms /* ---------- STATE S2 : GREEN ON for 10 seconds ------- */ digitalWrite(redPin, LOW); // Red OFF digitalWrite(orangePin, LOW); // Orange OFF digitalWrite(greenPin, HIGH); // Green ON delay(10000); // Wait 10 seconds = 10000 ms } ``` --- ## 3.5 Code Walk-Through (Line-by-Line Logic) | Line | Purpose | Engineering Rationale | |------|---------|------------------------| | `int redPin = 13;` | Symbolic pin name for readability | Avoids magic numbers; standard KTU coding practice | | `pinMode(...OUTPUT);` | Configure pin as output driver | Required once in `setup()` | | `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH);` | Source 5 V to pin (LED anode) | Turns LED ON via current-limiting resistor | | `digitalWrite(pin, LOW);` | Pull pin to 0 V (no current) | Turns LED OFF | | `delay(ms);` | Blocking wait in milliseconds | Simplest timing primitive; uses Timer0 overflow | | `void loop() { ... }` | Infinite cyclic execution | Mirrors the **cyclic nature** of a real traffic light | > [!TIP] > **Advanced Variant (Non-Blocking using `millis()`):** A more robust implementation uses `millis()` so the program remains responsive to other inputs (e.g., pedestrian button). However, for KTU lab exam purposes, the `delay()`-based version is **fully sufficient** and **preferred for its clarity**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR ARD["Arduino Uno
ATmega328P
5V Logic"] P13["Digital Pin 13"] P12["Digital Pin 12"] P11["Digital Pin 11"] R1["220 ohm
Resistor"] R2["220 ohm
Resistor"] R3["220 ohm
Resistor"] LED1["RED LED
V_f 2.0V"] LED2["ORANGE LED
V_f 2.1V"] LED3["GREEN LED
V_f 2.2V"] GND["GND Rail"] ARD -->|Pin 13| P13 ARD -->|Pin 12| P12 ARD -->|Pin 11| P11 P13 --> R1 P12 --> R2 P11 --> R3 R1 --> LED1 R2 --> LED2 R3 --> LED3 LED1 --> GND LED2 --> GND LED3 --> GND ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Power flows from each Arduino digital pin → through the current-limiting resistor → into the LED anode → out of the LED cathode → back to the common **GND** rail. All three LED branches share the same GND reference. --- ## 4.2 State Transition Flowchart (Software Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD START(["Program Start / loop entry"]) --> S0["STATE S0: Red LED HIGH
delay 5000 ms"] S0 --> S1["STATE S1: Orange LED HIGH
delay 2000 ms"] S1 --> S2["STATE S2: Green LED HIGH
delay 10000 ms"] S2 --> S0 S0 -.->|Repeat infinitely| S0 ``` --- ## 4.3 Timing Diagram (Waveform Representation) ```mermaid gantt title Traffic Light Duty Cycle (5-2-10 sec) — One Complete Cycle dateFormat X axisFormat %S s section Red Red ON 5s :a1, 0, 5000 section Orange Orange ON 2s :a2, 5000, 2000 section Green Green ON 10s :a3, 7000, 10000 ``` **Interpretation:** At $t = 0$ s, only RED is HIGH. From $t = 5$ s to $t = 7$ s, only ORANGE is HIGH. From $t = 7$ s to $t = 17$ s, only GREEN is HIGH. The cycle then **restarts** at $t = 17$ s. --- ## 4.4 Pin-to-Component Mapping (Top View) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Arduino Uno (Top View)" D11["D11"] D12["D12"] D13["D13"] GNDX["GND"] end subgraph "Breadboard" R3X["220 ohm"] R2X["220 ohm"] R1X["220 ohm"] GR["GREEN LED"] OR["ORANGE LED"] RD["RED LED"] end D11 --> R3X --> GR --> GNDX D12 --> R2X --> OR --> GNDX D13 --> R1X --> RD --> GNDX ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]** **Define the term "duty cycle" as applied to a traffic light controller with a 5-2-10 s specification. Calculate the percentage duty cycle of each LED.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **duty cycle** of a traffic light refers to the fraction of one complete operating period during which a particular signal remains ON. It is expressed as a percentage. > > Total cycle period: $T_{cycle} = T_R + T_O + T_G = 5 + 2 + 10 = 17\text{ s}$ > > $$\text{Duty}_{Red} = \frac{5}{17} \times 100 \approx 29.41\%$$ > > $$\text{Duty}_{Orange} = \frac{2}{17} \times 100 \approx 11.76\%$$ > > $$\text{Duty}_{Green} = \frac{10}{17} \times 100 \approx 58.82\%$$ > > **Verifying:** $29.41 + 11.76 + 58.82 \approx 100\%$ ✔ --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]** **List the hardware components required to interface three LEDs (Red, Orange, Green) with an Arduino Uno. Justify the use of a 220 Ω series resistor.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Components required:** Arduino Uno, 3 LEDs (Red, Orange, Green), 3 × 220 Ω resistors, breadboard, jumper wires, USB cable. > > **Justification of 220 Ω resistor:** The Arduino digital pin sources 5 V. The LED forward voltage is ≈ 2 V. Applying KVL and Ohm's law: > > $$R = \frac{V_{s} - V_{f}}{I_{f}} = \frac{5 - 2}{0.015} = 200\text{ Ω}$$ > > The nearest standard E12 value is 220 Ω, which limits the LED current to ≈ 13.6 mA — safely below the 20 mA per-pin limit of the ATmega328P, thereby protecting both the LED and the microcontroller from overcurrent damage. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Choice-based — answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern] (14 Marks)** **Design and implement a traffic light controller using Arduino Uno with a duty cycle of 5 s (Red), 2 s (Orange), and 10 s (Green).** #### **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram, list the components, and explain the interfacing of the three LEDs to the Arduino. Specify the current-limiting resistor calculation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Components:** Arduino Uno, Red LED, Orange LED, Green LED, 3 × 220 Ω resistors, breadboard, USB cable. 2. **Circuit Diagram (textual):** - Pin 13 → 220 Ω → Red LED Anode → Cathode → GND - Pin 12 → 220 Ω → Orange LED Anode → Cathode → GND - Pin 11 → 220 Ω → Green LED Anode → Cathode → GND 3. **Resistor Calculation:** - For Red LED: $R = \frac{5 - 2.0}{0.015} = 200\text{ Ω}$ → nearest standard = 220 Ω. - For Orange LED: $R = \frac{5 - 2.1}{0.015} = 193.33\text{ Ω}$ → use 220 Ω. - For Green LED: $R = \frac{5 - 2.2}{0.015} = 186.67\text{ Ω}$ → use 220 Ω. 4. **Interfacing Logic:** Arduino digital pins source 5 V (HIGH) or sink to 0 V (LOW). The series resistor limits the forward current to ≈ 13.6 mA, ensuring safe operation. #### **(b)** Write the complete Arduino program for the 5-2-10 s traffic light and explain the role of `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, and `delay()`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *(Code as shown in Section 3.4.)* **Explanation of key functions:** | Function | Role | Returns / Effect | |----------|------|------------------| | `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` | Configures the specified pin as a digital output driver | None — sets DDR register internally | | `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH/LOW)` | Drives the pin HIGH (5 V) or LOW (0 V) | None — sets PORT register | | `delay(ms)` | Pauses program execution for `ms` milliseconds | None (blocking call) | **Sequential Logic:** In `loop()`, the code drives Red HIGH for 5000 ms → Orange HIGH for 2000 ms → Green HIGH for 10000 ms → repeats. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern] (14 Marks)** **A 5-2-10 second duty cycle traffic light is to be implemented. (a) Derive the value of the series resistor for each LED using Ohm's Law. (b) Calculate the number of complete cycles in 1 hour and the duty cycle percentage of each LED. Write the algorithm and verify with an Arduino sketch.** #### **(a)** Resistor derivation for all three LEDs and safety verification. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** - **Given:** $V_s = 5\text{ V}$, $V_{f,R} = 2.0\text{ V}$, $V_{f,O} = 2.1\text{ V}$, $V_{f,G} = 2.2\text{ V}$, $I_f = 15\text{ mA}$. - **KVL:** $V_R = V_s - V_f$ - **Ohm's Law:** $R = V_R / I_f$ | LED | $V_f$ (V) | $V_R$ (V) | $R$ (Ω) calculated | Standard value used | |-----|-----------|-----------|--------------------|---------------------| | Red | 2.0 | 3.0 | 200.00 | **220 Ω** | | Orange | 2.1 | 2.9 | 193.33 | **220 Ω** | | Green | 2.2 | 2.8 | 186.67 | **220 Ω** | **Verification of safety:** Actual current = $\frac{5 - V_f}{220}$. Max current (Red) = 13.64 mA < 20 mA per-pin limit. ✔ #### **(b)** Cycle calculations and Arduino verification. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** - **Total cycle period:** $T_{cycle} = 5 + 2 + 10 = 17\text{ s}$ - **Cycles per hour:** $N = \frac{3600}{17} \approx 211.76$ → ≈ **211 complete cycles/hour** - **Duty cycle percentages:** $$\text{Duty}_{R} = \frac{5}{17} \times 100 = 29.41\%$$ $$\text{Duty}_{O} = \frac{2}{17} \times 100 = 11.76\%$$ $$\text{Duty}_{G} = \frac{10}{17} \times 100 = 58.82\%$$ **Arduino sketch:** As shown in Section 3.4. Verification can be done by opening the **Serial Monitor** at 9600 baud and printing timestamps using `millis()` at each state transition. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Skipping KVL Step** (loses 2 marks): Writing $R = V_s / I_f$ **without subtracting** $V_f$ — the LED forward-voltage drop is mandatory. > 2. **Wrong pin declaration type** (loses 1 mark): Using `int pin = "13";` (string) instead of `int pin = 13;` (integer). > 3. **Forgetting `pinMode()` in `setup()`** (loses 2 marks): The pin will behave as input by default and the LED will glow dimly or not at all. > 4. **Wrong delay units** (loses 2 marks): Writing `delay(5)` instead of `delay(5000)` — units are **milliseconds**, not seconds. > 5. **Not explaining state transition** (loses 3 marks in 14-mark questions): Examiners expect a **flowchart OR state table** — do not skip this. > 6. **Reversed LED polarity** (loses 2 marks in lab record): Anode must be on the Arduino side; cathode on GND. Reversed = no glow. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)** ### ⚡ Key Numerical Values - **Total cycle:** **17 s** (5 + 2 + 10) - **Standard series resistor:** **220 Ω** (¼ W) - **Arduino logic HIGH:** **5 V** - **Typical LED V_f:** **2.0 – 2.2 V** - **Cycles per hour:** **≈ 211.76** - **Cycles per minute:** **≈ 3.53** - **Red duty %:** **29.41 %** - **Orange duty %:** **11.76 %** - **Green duty %:** **58.82 %** ### ⚡ Must-Memorize Code Skeleton ```cpp int R = 13, O = 12, G = 11; void setup() { pinMode(R, OUTPUT); pinMode(O, OUTPUT); pinMode(G, OUTPUT); } void loop() { digitalWrite(R, HIGH); digitalWrite(O, LOW); digitalWrite(G, LOW); delay(5000); digitalWrite(R, LOW); digitalWrite(O, HIGH); digitalWrite(G, LOW); delay(2000); digitalWrite(R, LOW); digitalWrite(O, LOW); digitalWrite(G, HIGH); delay(10000); } ``` ### ⚡ Must-Memorize Formulae - $R = \frac{V_s - V_f}{I_f}$ (LED current-limiting resistor) - $T_{cycle} = T_R + T_O + T_G = 17\text{ s}$ - $N = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$ - $D_{\%} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100$ ### ⚡ Hardware Polarity Rule - **Anode (+, longer lead)** → Arduino pin (via resistor) - **Cathode (−, shorter lead, flat side)** → GND ### ⚡ Three Critical Function Roles - `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` — declared in `setup()` once - `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH/LOW)` — turns LED ON/OFF - `delay(ms)` — **blocking** wait (1 s = 1000 ms) ### ⚡ State Sequence to Recall Instantly **S0 (Red 5 s) → S1 (Orange 2 s) → S2 (Green 10 s) → S0 → ...** *(cyclic, infinite loop)* > [!TIP] > **Final Tip for KTU Lab Viva:** If asked *"How would you modify the program to add a pedestrian button?"*, mention replacing `delay()` with non-blocking `millis()`-based timing so the Arduino can poll the button state between state transitions.

topics.title: Switch on and switch off a DC motor based on the position of a switch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Switch on and switch off a DC motor based on the position of a switch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **switch-controlled DC motor activation circuit** is a fundamental electromechanical interface system in which the binary state (OPEN/CLOSED) of a manually or mechanically actuated switch determines the energization and de-energization of a Direct Current (DC) motor. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Sensors and Devices Lab (PCCIL607), this experiment is implemented using a **microcontroller-based embedded system** (typically Arduino Uno / ESP32 / Raspberry Pi Pico) where the switch acts as a digital input sensor, and the motor is driven through an **isolating switching device** such as a BJT transistor, MOSFET, or electromechanical relay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCIL607 – Module 2):** The experiment demonstrates the foundational principle of *sensor-to-actuator signal conditioning*, where a passive input device (switch) is mapped to an active output device (DC motor) through a control logic stage. This forms the bedrock of automation, robotics, and Industrial IoT (IIoT) applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **elevator button** in a shopping mall lift:
- When you **press** the button (switch CLOSED), the lift motor (DC motor) **turns ON** and moves the cabin.
- When you **release** the button (switch OPEN), the motor **stops** and the cabin halts.

In this experiment, we replicate the same logic at a laboratory scale using a **tactile push-button switch** (or a slide switch / limit switch) interfaced to a microcontroller. The microcontroller continuously *reads* (polls) the switch position, *decides* the logic state (HIGH/LOW), and *commands* the switching element (transistor/relay) to either **energize** or **de-energize** the DC motor.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants and Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in the Experiment:**
> - **Arduino Logic HIGH Voltage:** $V_{OH} = +5\text{ V DC}$ (or $3.3\text{ V}$ for ESP32)
> - **Arduino Logic LOW Voltage:** $V_{OL} = 0\text{ V DC}$ (GND)
> - **Standard DC Motor Operating Voltage:** $V_M = 3\text{ V}$ to $12\text{ V DC}$
> - **Pull-up Resistor:** $R_{PU} = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (internal or external)
> - **Base Resistor (for BJT):** $R_B = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ (typical)
> - **Flyback Diode Rating:** $1\text{ N4007}$ ($V_{RRM} = 1000\text{ V}$, $I_F = 1\text{ A}$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Operational Theory

The system can be broken down into **three functional blocks**:

**Block 1 — Input Stage (Sensor Equivalent):**
- The **SPST (Single Pole Single Throw) switch** functions as a digital sensor with two discrete states.
- When **OPEN**: The microcontroller pin reads **HIGH** (logic 1) via the internal pull-up resistor.
- When **CLOSED**: The microcontroller pin reads **LOW** (logic 0) because the pin is shorted to GND.

**Block 2 — Control Stage (Decision Logic):**
- The microcontroller executes an `if-else` conditional loop.
- The firmware samples the input pin at a defined **polling interval** (typically 50 ms to 100 ms to avoid debounce errors).

**Block 3 — Output Stage (Actuator Driver):**
- A **driver circuit** (BJT / MOSFET / Relay module) amplifies the weak microcontroller signal ($\approx 20\text{ mA}$) to the high-current demand of the DC motor ($\approx 200\text{ mA}$ to $2\text{ A}$).

### 2.2 Why a Driver Circuit is Mandatory (KTU Favourite Question)

A standard Arduino digital pin can source/sink a **maximum of 40 mA** (absolute maximum; 20 mA recommended). A typical DC motor draws **200 mA to 2000 mA** during operation. Connecting a motor directly to a GPIO pin will **permanently damage** the microcontroller's internal MOSFETs due to **thermal overstress**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Component:**
> - **Resistor (R_B):** Limits base current to prevent transistor saturation damage.
> - **Transistor (Q1):** Acts as an *electronic switch* — small current controls large current.
> - **Flyback Diode (D1):** Protects the transistor from the **back-EMF** generated when the motor's inductive winding is suddenly de-energized.
> - **Decoupling Capacitor (C1):** Suppresses voltage spikes caused by the motor's brush-commutator noise.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ohm's Law** | $V = I \cdot R$ | Voltage = Current × Resistance | V, A, Ω |
| 2 | **Power Dissipation** | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power consumed by motor | Watts (W) |
| 3 | **BJT Base Current** | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | Base current for transistor saturation | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | **Collector Current** | $I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$ | Current amplification factor | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | **Motor Back-EMF** | $E_b = K \cdot \Phi \cdot N$ | Induced voltage opposing supply | Volts (V) |
| 6 | **Armature Voltage Eq.** | $V_a = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$ | KVL around motor armature circuit | Volts (V) |
| 7 | **Switch Debounce Time** | $t_d = 5 \text{ ms} \to 50 \text{ ms}$ | Software/hardware debounce delay | Milliseconds (ms) |
| 8 | **Pull-up Current** | $I_{PU} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_{PU}}$ | Current through pull-up resistor | Amperes (A) |
| 9 | **Diode Peak Inverse V.** | $V_{RRM} \geq V_{CC(max)}$ | Diode reverse voltage rating | Volts (V) |
| 10 | **Transistor Saturation** | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2 \text{ V}$ | Collector-Emitter voltage when ON | Volts (V) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Absolute Values:** All absolute value notations in the above expressions (e.g., $\vert V \vert$) are to be interpreted in the conventional electrical sense, not as mathematical modulus operations. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ only where physically meaningful (such as magnitude of alternating quantities).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This switching principle is the **fundamental building block** of:
- **Home automation** systems (smart fan regulators, exhaust fans)
- **Industrial conveyor belts** with manual start/stop stations
- **Automotive systems** (ignition switch, starter motor control)
- **Agricultural pump controllers** (manual override switches)
- **Robotic emergency stop (E-Stop)** circuits
- **Elevator and escalator** control panels

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating the Base Resistor Value (R_B)

**Given Data (Standard Lab Values):**
- Supply voltage to motor: $V_{CC} = 9 \text{ V DC}$
- DC motor running current: $I_M = 500 \text{ mA} = 0.5 \text{ A}$
- Transistor DC current gain: $\beta = 100$ (for BC547 NPN BJT)
- Arduino output voltage in HIGH state: $V_{OH} = 5 \text{ V}$
- Base-Emitter voltage drop: $V_{BE} = 0.7 \text{ V}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the required collector current:**

The transistor must carry at least the full motor current when saturated. Hence:

$$
I_C = I_M = 0.5 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the minimum base current required for saturation:**

Using the relationship $I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$, we solve for $I_B$:

$$
I_B = \dfrac{I_C}{\beta}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
I_B = \dfrac{0.5 \text{ A}}{100} = 0.005 \text{ A} = 5 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the base loop:**

The Arduino pin (5 V) drives the base through $R_B$, dropping $V_{BE} = 0.7 \text{ V}$ across the base-emitter junction:

$$
V_{OH} = I_B \cdot R_B + V_{BE}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_B$ algebraically:**

Rearranging for $R_B$:

$$
R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
R_B = \dfrac{5 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V}}{0.005 \text{ A}}
$$

$$
R_B = \dfrac{4.3 \text{ V}}{0.005 \text{ A}}
$$

$$
R_B = 860 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Apply an engineering safety factor (KTU Standard Practice):**

To ensure **hard saturation** and account for $\beta$ variation with temperature, we *overdrive* the base by a factor of 2:

$$
R_{B(\text{design})} = \dfrac{R_B}{2} = \dfrac{860}{2} = 430 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 6 — Select the nearest standard E24 resistor value:**

The closest standard E24 series value is $R_B = 470 \text{ }\Omega$ (commonly stocked in KTU lab kits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $R_B = 470 \text{ }\Omega$ (standard 1/4 W resistor) is used to safely switch the 500 mA DC motor using a 5 V Arduino signal.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Dissipation in the Switching Transistor

When the motor is **OFF** (switch OPEN), the transistor is in **cut-off** region: $I_C = 0$, so power dissipation $P_T = 0$.

When the motor is **ON** (switch CLOSED), the transistor is in **saturation** region. The power dissipated in the transistor is:

$$
P_T = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C
$$

Substituting the standard values $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2 \text{ V}$ and $I_C = 0.5 \text{ A}$:

$$
P_T = 0.2 \text{ V} \times 0.5 \text{ A}
$$

$$
P_T = 0.1 \text{ W} = 100 \text{ mW}
$$

A standard **BC547** transistor has a maximum power rating of $P_{T(max)} = 500 \text{ mW}$, so the design is well within the safe operating area (SOA).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pull-up Resistor Sizing for Switch Input

For reliable digital logic detection, the pull-up resistor value must be chosen to:
- Provide a **strong HIGH** when the switch is open (minimal current leakage).
- Allow the input to be **pulled to LOW** when the switch is closed (overcome pull-up current).

**Rule of Thumb (KTU Lab Standard):**

$$
R_{PU} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

The corresponding pull-up current is:

$$
I_{PU} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_{PU}} = \dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{10{,}000 \text{ }\Omega} = 0.5 \text{ mA}
$$

This is well within the Arduino's pin sourcing capability and provides noise immunity against electromagnetic interference (EMI) from the motor.

### 3.4 Switch Debounce Analysis (Algorithmic Perspective)

Mechanical switches exhibit **contact bounce** — a phenomenon where the contacts oscillate between OPEN and CLOSED for 5–50 ms after actuation. Without compensation, a single press may register as multiple presses.

**Hardware Debounce (RC Filter):**
- $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ (ceramic)
- Time constant: $\tau = R \cdot C = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \times 0.1 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 1 \text{ ms}$
- Settling time: $t_s \approx 5\tau = 5 \text{ ms}$ (sufficient to suppress bounce)

**Software Debounce (Arduino `delay`):**
- Insert `delay(50);` after detecting a state change to mask the bounce period.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```

    MOTOR_ON --> MOTOR_OFF : Switch OPEN HIGH detected

    DEBOUNCE_WAIT --> MOTOR_ON : Stable after 50 ms
    DEBOUNCE_WAIT --> MOTOR_OFF : Stable LOW after 50 ms
```

### 4.4 Component Interconnection Table

| Block | Component | Specification | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input | Tactile Switch | SPST, 12 V, 50 mA | Binary state sensor |
| Input | Pull-up Resistor | $10 \text{ k}\Omega$, 1/4 W | Define HIGH state when switch open |
| Control | Microcontroller | Arduino Uno, ATmega328P | Read switch, output logic |
| Driver | BJT Transistor | BC547, NPN, $\beta = 100$ | Current amplification switch |
| Driver | Base Resistor | $470 \text{ }\Omega$, 1/4 W | Limit base current |
| Protection | Flyback Diode | 1N4007, $1\text{ A}$, $1000\text{ V}$ | Suppress back-EMF |
| Output | DC Motor | $9\text{ V}$, $500\text{ mA}$ | Mechanical actuator |
| Indicator | LED + Resistor | $5\text{ mm}$ Red, $220 \text{ }\Omega$ | Visual status indicator |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*What is the purpose of a pull-up resistor in a switch interfacing circuit with a microcontroller? What happens if it is omitted?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A pull-up resistor ($10 \text{ k}\Omega$ typical) ensures that the microcontroller input pin reads a **defined logic HIGH ($+5 \text{ V}$)** when the switch is in the OPEN position. Without it, the input pin is said to be "**floating**" — it picks up stray electromagnetic noise and randomly toggles between HIGH and LOW, causing **erratic motor behavior**. When the switch is CLOSED, it overrides the pull-up and forces the pin to LOW. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Why is a flyback diode (1N4007) connected across a DC motor in a switching circuit? What failure mode does it prevent?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A DC motor contains **inductive windings**. When the switching transistor is turned OFF, the magnetic field in the winding collapses and generates a high-voltage **back-EMF spike** (can reach $100$–$300 \text{ V}$). This spike can **puncture the transistor's collector-emitter junction** causing permanent failure. The flyback diode is connected in **reverse bias** across the motor, providing a safe path for the inductive current to dissipate, thereby clamping the voltage spike to approximately $0.7 \text{ V}$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** forget to mention **switch debouncing** — examiners specifically look for this term in 14-mark answers (carries 2 marks).
> 2. **Always include the flyback diode** in your circuit diagram. Omitting it costs **2 marks** under "circuit completeness".
> 3. **Mention component ratings** (e.g., $V_{CE(max)}$, $I_C(max)$) when justifying your transistor selection. Vague answers lose **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Do not confuse** the role of pull-up vs. pull-down resistors. KTU papers test this distinction frequently.
> 5. **Always show the KVL/KCL derivation** for the base resistor — hand-waving the calculation loses 3 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Exam Pattern, Module 2]*

**(a)** Design a circuit to switch ON and switch OFF a 9 V, 500 mA DC motor using a tactile push-button switch and an Arduino Uno. Draw the complete circuit diagram, label all components, and explain the working principle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:**
- The **9 V DC battery** (or external adapter) supplies the motor through the **collector terminal** of a BC547 NPN transistor (pin configuration: C-B-E).
- The **emitter** of the transistor is connected to **GND**.
- The **Arduino digital pin D2** is configured as `INPUT_PULLUP` and connected to one terminal of the **tactile switch**; the other terminal is connected to **GND**.
- The **Arduino digital pin D9** is configured as `OUTPUT` and drives the transistor base through a $470 \text{ }\Omega$ base resistor ($R_B$).
- A **1N4007 flyback diode** is connected in **reverse bias** across the motor terminals (cathode to $+9 \text{ V}$, anode to collector).
- A **red LED with $220 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor** is connected from pin D9 to GND as a status indicator.

**Working Principle:**
- When the switch is **OPEN**, the Arduino reads **HIGH** on pin D2. The firmware sets pin D9 to **LOW**, keeping the transistor in **cut-off**. The motor is **OFF** and the LED is **OFF**.
- When the switch is **CLOSED**, the Arduino reads **LOW** on pin D2. The firmware sets pin D9 to **HIGH** ($+5 \text{ V}$). This drives approximately $9 \text{ mA}$ into the transistor base, saturating it ($I_C \approx 0.5 \text{ A}$), which completes the motor circuit. The motor **spins** and the LED **glows**.
- A software `delay(50)` provides **switch debouncing**. (7 marks — full distribution: 3 for diagram, 2 for working, 2 for component justification)

---

**(b)** Derive the value of the base resistor $R_B$ required to switch a 9 V, 500 mA DC motor using a BC547 transistor driven by a 5 V Arduino pin. Given $\beta_{DC} = 100$ and $V_{BE} = 0.7 \text{ V}$. What is the power dissipated by the transistor in saturation? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Required collector current equals motor current:

$$
I_C = I_M = 0.5 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2:** Minimum base current:

$$
I_B = \dfrac{I_C}{\beta} = \dfrac{0.5}{100} = 5 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply KVL in the base-emitter loop:

$$
V_{OH} = I_B \cdot R_B + V_{BE}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $R_B$:

$$
R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \dfrac{5 - 0.7}{0.005} = 860 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 5:** Apply 2× safety factor (overdrive for hard saturation):

$$
R_{B(\text{design})} = \dfrac{860}{2} = 430 \text{ }\Omega \approx 470 \text{ }\Omega \text{ (E24 standard)}
$$

**Step 6:** Power dissipation in saturation:

$$
P_T = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C = 0.2 \text{ V} \times 0.5 \text{ A} = 0.1 \text{ W} = 100 \text{ mW}
$$

Since $P_{T(max)} = 500 \text{ mW}$ for BC547, the design is **safe** with a 5× margin. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the three functional blocks of a switch-controlled DC motor system. Discuss the role of the **driver stage** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The system consists of three blocks:

**1. Input Block (Sensor):**
- The **SPST switch** acts as a binary digital sensor.
- A **pull-up resistor** ensures defined logic levels.
- A **debounce filter (RC or software)** removes mechanical contact bounce.

**2. Control Block (Microcontroller):**
- The **Arduino Uno** reads the switch state in a continuous loop.
- Implements conditional logic: `if (switch == LOW) motor = HIGH; else motor = LOW;`.
- Provides a **clean, debounced, and noise-immune** control signal.

**3. Output Block (Actuator + Driver):**
- The **BJT/MOSFET driver** amplifies the low-current microcontroller signal to the high-current motor demand.
- The **DC motor** performs the mechanical work.
- The **flyback diode** protects the driver from inductive kickback.
- The **LED indicator** provides visual feedback.

**Role of the Driver Stage:**
- A microcontroller GPIO can source only $\approx 20\text{–}40 \text{ mA}$; a motor needs $\approx 200\text{–}2000 \text{ mA}$.
- The driver provides **current amplification** (typically $\beta = 100$ for BC547) and **galvanic isolation** (if a relay is used).
- It also provides **voltage level shifting** — the microcontroller operates at $5 \text{ V}$ while the motor may need $9 \text{ V}$, $12 \text{ V}$, or $24 \text{ V}$. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** Compare the use of a **BJT**, **MOSFET**, and **electromechanical relay** as the switching element in a DC motor control circuit. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Switching Element | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **BJT (e.g., BC547)** | Low cost, simple base drive, good for low-current DC motors | Current-controlled (needs base resistor), power loss as heat, slower switching |
| **MOSFET (e.g., IRF540N)** | Voltage-controlled (high input impedance), very fast switching, low $R_{DS(on)}$, ideal for PWM speed control | Needs gate-driver for high-current devices, sensitive to static discharge, higher cost |
| **Electromechanical Relay (e.g., 5 V coil SPDT)** | Complete galvanic isolation, can switch AC and DC, no heat dissipation, very high inrush current tolerance | Slow mechanical switching (~10 ms), contact bounce, contact arcing, limited lifespan (~100,000 operations) |

**Selection Criterion:** For the lab experiment, a **BJT** is preferred (low-cost, educational clarity). For **PWM-based speed control**, a **MOSFET** is preferred. For **high-voltage/high-current isolation**, a **relay** is preferred. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The switch acts as a **binary digital sensor** with two states: OPEN (HIGH) and CLOSED (LOW).
- ✅ A **pull-up resistor ($10 \text{ k}\Omega$)** is mandatory to prevent floating input; Arduino's internal pull-up can also be enabled via `pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP)`.
- ✅ A **driver stage** (BJT / MOSFET / relay) is mandatory because microcontrollers cannot source motor current directly.
- ✅ The **base resistor formula** is $R_B = (V_{OH} - V_{BE}) / I_B$ where $I_B = I_C / \beta$.
- ✅ Apply a **2× overdrive safety factor** when calculating $R_B$ to ensure hard saturation.
- ✅ A **flyback diode (1N4007)** in reverse bias across the motor is mandatory to protect the driver from **back-EMF**.
- ✅ **Switch debouncing** (5–50 ms) is required to suppress mechanical contact bounce; use either RC filter (hardware) or `delay()` (software).
- ✅ Power dissipation in a saturated BJT is $P_T = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C$; must be less than $P_{T(max)}$ of the device.
- ✅ The **flyback diode cathode** points toward the **positive supply** ($+V_{CC}$), and the **anode** points toward the **collector** of the switching transistor.
- ✅ The **LED indicator** with a current-limiting resistor ($220 \text{ }\Omega$) provides visual debugging aid during lab evaluation.
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects students to draw **neat labelled circuit diagrams** with **all components marked** and **ratings specified**.
- ✅ Expected viva questions: *"What is the function of a snubber circuit?"* / *"Why use a relay instead of a transistor?"* / *"Explain hard saturation vs. active region of a BJT."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convert an analog voltage to digital value and show it on the screen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Convert an analog voltage to digital value and show it on the screen ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** is the electronic process of translating a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital representation (a sequence of binary numbers) suitable for processing by digital systems such as microcontrollers, microprocessors, or digital signal processors. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Sensors and Devices Lab, "Converting an analog voltage to a digital value and displaying it on the screen" refers to the operation of reading a real-world continuous voltage (typically **0 V to 5 V** for Arduino UNO or **0 V to 3.3 V** for NodeMCU ESP8266/ESP32 boards) using the on-chip **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** of the microcontroller and rendering the resulting integer value on the **Serial Monitor**, **LCD display**, or **OLED screen**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Every microcontroller used in PCCIL607 (Arduino UNO, NodeMCU, Raspberry Pi Pico) contains an internal ADC. The ATmega328P (Arduino UNO) features a **10-bit SAR ADC** with **6 multiplexed input channels** (A0–A5), producing digital outputs ranging from **0 to 1023**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing beside a swimming pool and someone asks, "How deep is the water?" You do not have a precise depth gauge, but you have a **wooden ruler with 1024 markings (0 to 1023)**. 1. **The water surface** is your **analog voltage** — it can settle at any continuous height, say 2.731 V, 2.7311 V, 2.73114 V … infinitely precise. 2. **Your ruler** represents the **ADC's digital resolution**. You can only "round" the water's exact height to the **nearest marking** on the ruler. 3. **The number you call out** (e.g., "Marking 559") is your **digital value** — a discrete, countable integer. 4. **Writing the number on a whiteboard** is analogous to **displaying it on the Serial Monitor or LCD**. The ruler analogy captures the essence of ADC: an infinitely precise analog world being mapped onto a **finite set of discrete digital codes**, each of which represents a small "voltage window." ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Resolution (N)**: The number of bits in the digital output. Arduino UNO: **N = 10 bits**. - **Number of discrete levels**: $2^N$. For 10-bit: $2^{10} = 1024$ levels (0 through 1023). - **Reference Voltage ($V_{ref}$)**: The maximum voltage that maps to the full-scale digital code (1023). Default on Arduino UNO: **$V_{ref} = 5.0$ V**. - **Step Size (Resolution Voltage)**: The smallest voltage change the ADC can distinguish, $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$. - **Sampling Rate**: How many conversions are performed per second (Arduino default ~**15 kSPS** at 10-bit). > [!NOTE] > The **Least Significant Bit voltage ($V_{LSB}$)** is the cornerstone of all ADC calculations. It defines the "width" of one digital step in the voltage domain. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the SAR ADC The on-chip ADC of the ATmega328P follows the **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** algorithm. The conversion proceeds through the following structured steps: 1. **Input Multiplexing**: The analog pin (A0–A5) is selected via the ADMUX register's MUX bits. Only one channel is converted at a time. 2. **Sample and Hold (S/H)**: An internal capacitor array charges to the analog input voltage $V_{in}$ for **1.5 ADC clock cycles** (acquisition time). This "freezes" the voltage during conversion so rapid input changes do not corrupt the result. 3. **Successive Approximation**: The SAR logic performs a **binary search** through the possible digital codes (0 to 1023). It first tests the midpoint (512), then narrows the range to either 0–511 or 512–1023, and so on, completing in exactly **10 comparison cycles** (one per bit from MSB to LSB). 4. **Quantization**: The continuous $V_{in}$ is rounded to the nearest representable digital code. The rounding introduces an inherent **quantization error**. 5. **Encoding**: The final 10-bit code is stored in the ADC data registers **ADCH and ADCL** (right-adjusted by default). 6. **Digital Display**: The firmware reads the 10-bit value, maps or scales it appropriately, and transmits it via `Serial.print()` (to Serial Monitor) or writes to an LCD via `lcd.print()`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why SAR?** It offers an excellent trade-off between **conversion speed** (fixed 10–13 cycles), **resolution** (up to 10 bits), and **hardware simplicity** (no precision resistors or integrators required), making it ideal for embedded sensor applications. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Typical Value (Arduino UNO)** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Resolution (Number of bits) | $N$ | — | 10 | bits | | Number of output codes | $L$ | $L = 2^N$ | 1024 | codes | | Reference Voltage | $V_{ref}$ | — | 5.0 | V | | Step Size / LSB Voltage | $V_{LSB}$ | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$ | 4.888 mV | V | | Digital Output Code | $D$ | $D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}}\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in} \cdot 2^N}{V_{ref}}\right)$ | 0 to 1023 | — | | Analog Voltage from Digital Code | $V_{in}$ | $V_{in} = D \cdot V_{LSB} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^N}$ | 0 to 5 | V | | Maximum Quantization Error | $E_q^{max}$ | $E_q^{max} = \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}}$ | $\pm$2.44 mV | V | | ADC Clock Frequency | $f_{ADC}$ | $f_{ADC} = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{\text{prescaler}}$ | 50–200 | kHz | | Conversion Time | $T_{conv}$ | $T_{conv} = 13 \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_{ADC}}$ | 65–260 | $\mu$s | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions:** The input voltage must satisfy $0 \le V_{in} \le V_{ref}$. Voltages outside this range will either be clipped (to 0 or 1023) or, if exceeding **$V_{ref}$ by more than ~0.5 V** without protection, can permanently damage the microcontroller pin. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Sensor Interfacing**: Temperature (LM35), light (LDR), pressure, and gas sensors output analog voltages that microcontrollers must digitize. - **Battery Monitoring**: Cell voltages are measured via ADC to compute State-of-Charge (SoC) in BMS. - **Audio Processing**: Microphone signals are sampled and quantized before digital filtering. - **Industrial Control**: PLCs continuously read analog process variables (flow, level, temperature) via 12-bit or 16-bit ADCs. - **Medical Instrumentation**: ECG, EEG, and pulse oximeter front-ends all rely on high-resolution ADCs. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Digital Output Code The core relationship between the analog input voltage and the digital output code of an ideal N-bit ADC is derived as follows. **Starting Principle:** The ADC divides the reference voltage range $[0, V_{ref}]$ into $2^N$ equal sub-intervals (quantization levels). Each sub-interval has a width equal to $V_{LSB}$. **Step 1:** Define the width of one quantization step. $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$$ *Logic:* The full voltage range $V_{ref}$ is uniformly partitioned into $2^N$ intervals, so each interval's width is the total range divided by the number of intervals. **Step 2:** Express the digital code as the count of $V_{LSB}$-sized steps that fit within $V_{in}$. The number of full quantization steps contained in $V_{in}$ is the ratio $V_{in} / V_{LSB}$. The integer digital code $D$ is the **nearest integer** (round-half-up) of this ratio: $$D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $V_{LSB}$ from Step 1 into Step 2. $$D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref} / 2^N}\right)$$ $$D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^N}{V_{ref}}\right)$$ *Logic:* Multiplying numerator and denominator by $2^N$ clears the fraction and yields the direct engineering formula used in all KTU lab calculations. **Step 4:** Define the inverse relation (reconstructing voltage from a digital code). $$V_{in} = D \cdot V_{LSB} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^N}$$ *Logic:* Multiplying the digital count $D$ by the per-step voltage $V_{LSB}$ recovers the centroid voltage of the corresponding quantization interval. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Pattern) **Problem:** The input voltage to an Arduino UNO ADC channel is $V_{in} = 2.45$ V. The default reference is $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, and the resolution is $N = 10$ bits. Calculate (a) the LSB voltage, (b) the digital output code, and (c) the quantization error. **Part (a): LSB Voltage** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024}$$ $$V_{LSB} = 0.0048828125\ \text{V} \approx 4.88\ \text{mV}$$ **Part (b): Digital Output Code** $$D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}}\right) = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{2.45}{0.0048828125}\right)$$ $$D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(501.76\right) = 502$$ *Verification using the consolidated formula:* $$D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^N}{V_{ref}}\right) = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{2.45 \cdot 1024}{5.0}\right) = \mathrm{round}\!\left(501.76\right) = 502$$ **Part (c): Quantization Error** The reconstructed voltage from $D = 502$ is: $$V_{reconstructed} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{502 \cdot 5.0}{1024} = 2.45117\ \text{V}$$ The quantization error is the difference between the actual and reconstructed values: $$E_q = V_{in} - V_{reconstructed} = 2.45000 - 2.45117 = -0.00117\ \text{V} = -1.17\ \text{mV}$$ The maximum possible error magnitude is: $$E_q^{max} = \pm\frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm 2.44\ \text{mV}$$ Since $\vert E_q \vert = 1.17\ \text{mV} < 2.44\ \text{mV}$, the result is consistent with ADC theory. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Lab Scenarios | **$V_{in}$ (V)** | **$D$ (Digital Code)** | **$V_{reconstructed}$ (V)** | **Quantization Error (mV)** | |---|---|---|---| | 0.000 | 0 | 0.00000 | 0.00 | | 0.500 | 102 | 0.49805 | +1.95 | | 1.234 | 253 | 1.23535 | -1.35 | | 2.450 | 502 | 2.45117 | -1.17 | | 3.300 | 676 | 3.30078 | -0.78 | | 4.000 | 819 | 3.99902 | +0.98 | | 4.995 | 1023 | 4.99512 | -0.12 | > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The quantization error is **always bounded** within $\pm V_{LSB}/2$. It is an **inherent, unavoidable** property of all finite-resolution ADCs and is commonly called the **ADC noise floor**. ### 3.4 Firmware Logic — Reference Arduino Sketch Skeleton The lab experiment typically uses the Arduino `analogRead()` function. Internally, this function configures the ADC prescaler, selects the channel, waits for the acquisition time, and returns the 10-bit result. **Key firmware call:** $$D = \text{analogRead}(A0); \quad D \in [0, 1023]$$ **Reconstruction of voltage in code:** ```text V_in = (D * 5.0) / 1023.0; ``` > [!WARNING] > **Note on the denominator:** Some references use `1024.0` and others use `1023.0` in the reconstruction formula. The technically correct value derived from our step-by-step analysis is **1024** (the number of quantization intervals). However, Arduino's `map()` function and many tutorials use **1023** (the maximum digital code). For KTU lab records, **state both and justify**, or consistently use **1023** to match Arduino's `map()` behavior. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The complete experimental signal chain for "Converting an analog voltage to a digital value and showing it on the screen" is captured in the following block diagram. ```mermaid flowchart LR src1[Analog Voltage Source
Potentiometer or Sensor] mux1[Analog Input Pin
A0 to A5] sh1[Sample and Hold
Internal Capacitor] sar1[SAR Logic
10 Bit Binary Search] reg1[ADC Data Register
ADCH and ADCL] mcu1[Microcontroller
Firmware Logic] dsp1[Display Device
Serial Monitor or LCD or OLED] src1 --> mux1 mux1 --> sh1 sh1 --> sar1 sar1 --> reg1 reg1 --> mcu1 mcu1 --> dsp1 ``` **Block-Level Functional Mapping Explanation:** - **Analog Voltage Source:** The physical continuous voltage, e.g., a 10 k$\Omega$ potentiometer wiper or an LM35 temperature sensor output. - **Analog Input Pin:** The physical MCU pin connected to the source. Channels A0–A5 are multiplexed internally. - **Sample and Hold:** An internal capacitor charges to $V_{in}$ and holds it steady during the binary-search conversion. - **SAR Logic:** Performs the 10-step binary search, comparing $V_{in}$ against internally generated $V_{ref}/2$, $V_{ref}/4$, $V_{ref}/8$, etc. - **ADC Data Register:** The final 10-bit code is latched into the ADCL/ADCH register pair, accessible by firmware. - **Microcontroller Firmware:** Reads the register, optionally maps the value to engineering units, and prepares the display string. - **Display Device:** Renders the value to the user via Serial Monitor (USB UART), 16$\times$2 LCD (I2C or parallel), or OLED (I2C/SPI). ### 4.2 SAR ADC Internal Conversion Sequence The internal state transitions of the SAR engine over a single conversion are visualized below. ```mermaid flowchart TD start1([Start Conversion]) setbit1[Test Bit 9 MSB
Set Code to 512] cmp1{Comparator
V_in versus V_DAC} pathA[Keep Bit 9 as 1
V_in is greater] pathB[Clear Bit 9 to 0
V_in is less] setbit2[Test Bit 8
Set Code to 256 or 0 plus 256] cmp2{Comparator 2} setbit3[Test Bit 7] loop1[Repeat for Bits 6 to 0] finish1([All 10 Bits Resolved
10 Bit Code in Register]) start1 --> setbit1 setbit1 --> cmp1 cmp1 -->|Yes| pathA cmp1 -->|No| pathB pathA --> setbit2 pathB --> setbit2 setbit2 --> cmp2 cmp2 --> setbit3 setbit3 --> loop1 loop1 --> finish1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Each "Test Bit" corresponds to **one ADC clock cycle**. After exactly **10 cycles**, the conversion is complete, and the final 10-bit code is the digital representation of the analog input voltage. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** *Define the term "resolution" of an ADC. For a 10-bit ADC with a reference voltage of 5 V, calculate the smallest voltage change that can be detected.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** The resolution of an ADC is defined as the number of discrete digital output levels it can produce, which is determined by its number of bits $N$. It is also expressed as the smallest analog voltage change that can be distinguished, called the **Least Significant Bit voltage ($V_{LSB}$)**. **Calculation (2 Marks):** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} = 4.88\ \text{mV}$$ The smallest voltage change that can be detected is **4.88 mV**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** *What is quantization error? Write the expression for maximum quantization error of an N-bit ADC.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (2 Marks):** Quantization error is the inherent difference between the actual continuous analog input voltage and the discrete digital value reconstructed from its ADC code. It arises because the ADC can only resolve the input into a finite set of $2^N$ discrete levels. **Expression (1 Mark):** $$E_q^{max} = \pm\frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}}$$ --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC. List its key advantages. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An Arduino UNO uses an ATmega328P microcontroller with a 10-bit ADC and a default reference voltage of 5 V. The analog input to channel A0 is measured to be 3.75 V. Calculate: (i) the digital output code, (ii) the reconstructed voltage, and (iii) the maximum possible quantization error. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR vrefBlock[Voltage Reference Vref] dacBlock[Internal DAC
R 2R Ladder] sarBlock[SAR Register
10 Bit Shift Register] cmpBlock[Comparator] shBlock[Sample and Hold] vInBlock[Analog Input V_in] outBlock[Digital Output 10 Bit] vInBlock --> shBlock shBlock --> cmpBlock sarBlock --> dacBlock dacBlock --> cmpBlock vrefBlock --> dacBlock cmpBlock --> sarBlock sarBlock --> outBlock ``` **Explanation (3 Marks):** 1. The analog input $V_{in}$ is first **sampled and held** by an internal capacitor. 2. The SAR register sets its MSB to 1 (code = 512) and the internal DAC generates a corresponding voltage $V_{DAC} = (512/1024) \cdot V_{ref} = 2.5$ V. 3. The **comparator** checks if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$. If yes, the bit is retained; if no, the bit is cleared. 4. The process repeats for the next bit, performing a **binary search** across the full code range. 5. After **10 clock cycles** (one per bit), the final code is the digital representation of $V_{in}$. **Key Advantages (1 Mark):** High speed (fixed conversion time), moderate to high resolution (8–18 bits typically), low power consumption, no precision external components required. **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Given: $N = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, $V_{in} = 3.75$ V. **(i) Digital Output Code (3 Marks):** $$D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^N}{V_{ref}}\right) = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{3.75 \cdot 1024}{5.0}\right)$$ $$D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(768.0\right) = 768$$ **(ii) Reconstructed Voltage (2 Marks):** $$V_{reconstructed} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{768 \cdot 5.0}{1024} = 3.75\ \text{V}$$ (Exact match, so quantization error is zero in this specific case.) **(iii) Maximum Quantization Error (2 Marks):** $$E_q^{max} = \pm\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}} = \pm\frac{5.0}{2048} = \pm 2.44\ \text{mV}$$ --- **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the complete experimental procedure to convert an analog voltage to a digital value and display it on the Serial Monitor using Arduino UNO. Include the circuit diagram, the components required, and a sample firmware sketch. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 12-bit ADC is used in a digital temperature monitoring system with a reference voltage of 3.3 V. The sensor output voltage corresponding to a temperature of 50°C is 1.65 V. Calculate: (i) the LSB voltage, (ii) the digital output code, and (iii) the percentage resolution of this ADC compared to an 8-bit ADC with the same $V_{ref}$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Components Required (1 Mark):** Arduino UNO, 10 k$\Omega$ potentiometer, breadboard, jumper wires, USB cable, PC with Arduino IDE. **Circuit Diagram (2 Marks):** Potentiometer's two end terminals connected to **5 V** and **GND** of the Arduino. The wiper (middle terminal) connected to analog input **A0**. **Procedure (2 Marks):** 1. Assemble the circuit on the breadboard. 2. Connect the Arduino to the PC via USB and open the Arduino IDE. 3. Write/upload the sketch that calls `analogRead(A0)`. 4. Open the **Serial Monitor** (baud rate 9600) to observe the digital values in real time. 5. Rotate the potentiometer and verify that the displayed value changes from 0 to 1023. **Sample Firmware Sketch (2 Marks):** ```text void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int sensorValue = analogRead(A0); float voltage = sensorValue * (5.0 / 1023.0); Serial.print("Digital: "); Serial.print(sensorValue); Serial.print(" | Voltage: "); Serial.println(voltage); delay(500); } ``` **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Given: $N = 12$, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, $V_{in} = 1.65$ V. **(i) LSB Voltage (2 Marks):** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096} = 0.8057\ \text{mV}$$ **(ii) Digital Output Code (2 Marks):** $$D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}}\right) = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{1.65}{0.0008057}\right) = \mathrm{round}\!\left(2048.0\right) = 2048$$ **(iii) Percentage Resolution Comparison (3 Marks):** For the 12-bit ADC: $$V_{LSB_{12}} = 0.8057\ \text{mV}$$ For the 8-bit ADC with the same $V_{ref}$: $$V_{LSB_{8}} = \frac{3.3}{2^{8}} = \frac{3.3}{256} = 12.8906\ \text{mV}$$ Percentage resolution (relative to full-scale 3.3 V): $$\text{Resolution}_{12} = \frac{0.8057}{3300} \cdot 100\% = 0.0244\%$$ $$\text{Resolution}_{8} = \frac{12.8906}{3300} \cdot 100\% = 0.3906\%$$ The 12-bit ADC is **16 times finer** (since $2^{12}/2^{8} = 2^{4} = 16$), making it significantly more precise for temperature measurement. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the `round()` function:** Students often truncate the digital code (use `floor()` or integer division) instead of rounding, leading to an off-by-one error. Always use `round()` or nearest-integer logic. > 2. **Confusing 1023 vs 1024:** The number of quantization intervals is **1024** for a 10-bit ADC, but the maximum digital code is **1023**. Mixing these in formulas is a frequent mark-deduction cause. > 3. **Wrong units in quantization error:** Expressing the error in volts without converting to mV/$\mu$V for clarity loses readability marks. > 4. **Missing the Vref specification:** Always state the assumed $V_{ref}$ explicitly. The default Arduino UNO $V_{ref}$ is 5 V, while NodeMCU/ESP32 use 3.3 V. Examiners check this. > 5. **No firmware code in Part A of Question B:** The lab exam explicitly requires a working sketch; not including one results in zero marks for that sub-part. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **ADC Definition:** Conversion of a continuous analog voltage into a discrete digital code of N bits. - **Key Formula (must memorize):** - $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$ - $D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in} \cdot 2^N}{V_{ref}}\right)$ - $V_{in} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^N}$ - $E_q^{max} = \pm\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}}$ - **Arduino UNO Default Specs:** 10-bit ADC, $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, channels A0–A5, 1024 levels (0–1023), $V_{LSB} = 4.88$ mV. - **NodeMCU/ESP32 Default Specs:** 12-bit ADC (ESP32) or 10-bit ADC (ESP8266), $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, $V_{LSB} \approx 3.22$ mV (ESP32) or 3.22 mV (10-bit NodeMCU). - **SAR ADC Properties:** Fixed 10–13 clock cycle conversion, binary search algorithm, no external precision components needed. - **Quantization Error Bound:** Always $\pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$ for an ideal ADC with mid-rise quantization. - **Input Voltage Limits:** $0 \le V_{in} \le V_{ref}$. Never exceed $V_{ref} + 0.5$ V on an Arduino pin to prevent latch-up damage. - **Display Methods:** Serial Monitor (USB UART, baud 9600), 16$\times$2 LCD (I2C address 0x27), 0.96" OLED (I2C address 0x3C), 7-segment display. - **Map Function:** Arduino's `map(value, 0, 1023, 0, 100)` is useful for percentage display; remember it uses **integer** arithmetic. - **Lab Viva Quick Points:** What is ADC? Why SAR? What is $V_{ref}$? How to improve resolution? (Answer: increase N, reduce $V_{ref}$, or use oversampling and averaging.) - **Oversampling Trick:** Averaging $4^k$ samples effectively increases resolution by **k bits** at the cost of $4^k$ conversion cycles.

topics.title: Create a door lock application using a reed switch and magnet and give a beep when the door is opened
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Create a door lock application using a reed switch and magnet and give a beep when the door is opened # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Reed Switch** is a passive electromechanical switching element consisting of two ferromagnetic reed contacts (typically made of nickel-iron alloy) hermetically sealed within a glass envelope filled with an inert gas (usually nitrogen). When an external magnetic field of sufficient strength (measured in Ampere-turns or Gauss) is brought close to the switch, the reeds become magnetized, attract each other, and close the circuit. When the magnetic field is removed, the reeds spring back to their normally open position. In the context of a **Door Lock Application**, a reed switch is mounted on the door frame, and a permanent magnet is mounted on the door itself. When the door is closed, the magnet is in close proximity to the reed switch, keeping the circuit closed (or open, depending on the configuration). When the door is opened, the magnet moves away, changing the switch state. This state change is detected by a microcontroller, which then activates a **piezoelectric buzzer** to produce an audible alert. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCIL607):** This experiment tests **CO2 (Apply knowledge of sensors in real-time embedded applications)** and **CO3 (Design and develop microcontroller-based systems using various sensors and actuators)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Friends Hugging When Called" Imagine two small, shy iron friends standing inside a sealed glass tube. They refuse to touch each other unless someone with a strong magnetic personality walks close by. The moment a magnet approaches, they are "magnetized" (attracted) and immediately hug each other, completing an electrical connection. When the magnetic person walks away, they let go and return to standing apart. This is precisely how a reed switch works — the two reeds (iron friends) close (hug) under a magnetic field and open (release) when the field is gone. A real-world analogy: walk into any retail shop during business hours and look at the door frame — you will see a small rectangular box on the door and a matching magnet on the frame. That is a reed-switch-based intrusion sensor. It is the same principle we will code today. ## 1.3 Key Components & Standard Specifications | Component | Standard Specification | Role in Experiment | |---|---|---| | **Reed Switch** | SPST (Single Pole Single Throw), 10W contact rating, switching voltage **≤ 100V DC** | Detects door state via magnetic field | | **Permanent Magnet** | Neodymium (NdFeB) rare-earth, typical surface field **≥ 1000 Gauss** | Activates the reed switch when door is closed | | **Piezo Buzzer** | 5V DC, **20 mA** rated current, resonant frequency **~ 2 kHz – 4 kHz** | Produces audible alert | | **Microcontroller** | Arduino Uno (ATmega328P, 5V logic) | Reads sensor and drives actuator | | **Resistor** | 10 kΩ (pull-up), 220 Ω (current limiting for buzzer) | Ensures defined logic levels | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Operating Threshold:** A typical reed switch activates at a distance of **10 mm – 30 mm** from a small neodymium magnet. This is the activation gap you must physically maintain between the door and frame. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle of the Reed Switch The reed switch is fundamentally a **magneto-mechanical transducer** — it converts a magnetic field into an electrical switching action. The operational chain is: 1. **Magnetic Flux Coupling:** The external magnet's field lines enter the glass envelope through the ferromagnetic reeds. 2. **Reed Polarization:** Each reed becomes a temporary magnet (North on one end, South on the other). 3. **Attractive Force Generation:** Opposite polarities on the inner tips create an attractive force, $F_{mag}$. 4. **Contact Closure:** When $F_{mag} > F_{spring}$ (the elastic restoring force of the reed material), the reeds touch, closing the circuit. 5. **Contact Opening:** When the magnet is removed, $F_{mag} \to 0$, and $F_{spring}$ snaps the reeds apart. The triggering distance depends on the **magnetomotive force (MMF)** of the source magnet and the sensitivity rating of the reed switch (expressed in **Ampere-Turns, AT**). ## 2.2 Circuit Theory — Pull-Up Resistor Logic Since a reed switch is a passive two-terminal device, it has no internal pull-up. The microcontroller input pin would **float** (pick up random noise) when the switch is open. To prevent this, we use an external **pull-up resistor** between the input pin and $V_{CC}$. The voltage at the microcontroller pin is: $$ V_{pin} = V_{CC} \times \frac{R_{switch}}{R_{switch} + R_{pullup}} $$ - When switch is **closed** (door closed, magnet present): $R_{switch} \approx 0 \Omega \Rightarrow V_{pin} \approx 0V$ (logic **LOW**) - When switch is **open** (door opened, magnet away): $V_{pin} \approx 5V$ (logic **HIGH** via pull-up) > [!TIP] > In modern Arduino, you can use the internal pull-up via `pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP)`, eliminating the need for an external resistor. However, external 10 kΩ pull-ups are preferred in lab examinations because they produce cleaner waveforms on the oscilloscope. ## 2.3 Buzzer Drive Circuit — Current Limiting A piezo buzzer is essentially a capacitive device. To protect the microcontroller pin, a current-limiting resistor is placed in series. The current through the buzzer is: $$ I_{buzzer} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{buzzer}}{R_{limit}} $$ For a 5V supply, a typical buzzer drops **~ 1.5V**, and we limit current to **~ 16 mA** to stay well within the ATmega328P's 40 mA per-pin rating. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | Magnetic Pull Force (qualitative) | $F_{mag} \propto \dfrac{B^2 \cdot A}{2\mu_0}$ | Newtons | Reed attraction | | Voltage Divider (Reed Open) | $V_{pin} = V_{CC}$ | Volts | Pull-up active state | | Voltage Divider (Reed Closed) | $V_{pin} \approx 0$ | Volts | Switch closed state | | Buzzer Current Limit | $I = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{buzzer}}{R_{limit}}$ | Amperes | Pin protection | | Activation Distance | $d_{act} \approx k \cdot \sqrt[3]{MMF}$ | mm | Magnet–switch gap | | Debounce Delay (typical) | $t_{db} = 50$ to $300$ | ms | Switch chatter suppression | | LED Current (indicator) | $I_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{f}}{R_{s}}$ | mA | Indicator protection | | ATmega328P Source Current | $I_{OH} \leq 40$ | mA/pin | Pin drive limit | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall:** A reed switch exhibits **contact bounce** for ~ 1–2 ms after closure (mechanical chatter). Without software/hardware debouncing, the buzzer may beep multiple times for a single door opening. Always include a `delay(200)` in lab implementations. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Reed-switch door lock systems are ubiquitous in: - **Automotive:** Door ajar indicators in cars - **Industrial Safety:** Machine guards on CNC equipment (interlock with emergency stop) - **Home Security:** Window/door sensors in alarm panels - **Medical:** Refrigerator door monitoring in pharma cold-chain - **White Goods:** Washing machine lid switches # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Component Selection & Calculation Walkthrough ### Step 1 — Choosing the Pull-Up Resistor The pull-up must be **large enough** to limit current when the switch is closed (no damage to the pin) and **small enough** to overcome input leakage current. $$ R_{pullup,min} = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_{pin,max}} = \frac{5V}{40 \times 10^{-3}A} = 125 \, \Omega $$ $$ R_{pullup,max} = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_{leak}} = \frac{5V}{1 \times 10^{-6}A} = 5 \, M\Omega $$ Therefore, the safe operating range is **125 Ω ≤ $R_{pullup}$ ≤ 5 MΩ**. A standard **10 kΩ** sits comfortably in this range (used in 99% of Arduino tutorials). ### Step 2 — Buzzer Series Resistor Calculation Given: - $V_{CC} = 5V$ - $V_{buzzer} = 1.5V$ (typical piezo) - Desired current $I_{buzzer} = 15$ mA $$ R_{limit} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{buzzer}}{I_{buzzer}} = \frac{5 - 1.5}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3.5}{0.015} $$ $$ R_{limit} = 233.33 \, \Omega $$ The nearest E12 standard value is **220 Ω**, which is the most commonly used in lab kits. ✅ ### Step 3 — Debounce Time Justification Reed switch bounce time (from manufacturer datasheets): $t_{bounce} \leq 2$ ms. We apply a safety factor of **100×** to handle worst-case mechanical vibration and ensure clean single-beep behavior: $$ t_{debounce} = 100 \times t_{bounce} = 100 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}s = 200 \, ms $$ This is why `delay(200)` is used after state-change detection. ## 3.2 Complete Working Code (Arduino-Compatible) ```cpp // ============================================================ // KTU PCCIL607 - Module Experiment 2 // Door Lock Application using Reed Switch & Magnet // + Audible Beep Alert on Door Opening // ============================================================ const int REED_PIN = 2; // Digital pin connected to reed switch const int BUZZER_PIN = 8; // Digital pin connected to piezo buzzer const int LED_PIN = 13; // On-board LED for visual indicator // State variable to track previous door status int previousDoorState = LOW; void setup() { pinMode(REED_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP); // Enable internal pull-up pinMode(BUZZER_PIN, OUTPUT); pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); Serial.println("Door Security System Initialized..."); } void loop() { // Read current state of reed switch int currentDoorState = digitalRead(REED_PIN); // HIGH = switch open = door OPENED (magnet away) // LOW = switch closed = door CLOSED (magnet near) if (currentDoorState == HIGH && previousDoorState == LOW) { Serial.println(">> ALERT: Door has been OPENED! <<"); // Beep the buzzer 3 times as an alarm pattern for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { digitalWrite(BUZZER_PIN, HIGH); digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH); delay(150); digitalWrite(BUZZER_PIN, LOW); digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW); delay(150); } delay(200); // Debounce delay } previousDoorState = currentDoorState; } ``` ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Truth Table | Door Position | Magnet Distance | Reed State | Pin Voltage | Logic Level | System Response | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Closed** | 0–10 mm | Closed | 0 V | **LOW (0)** | Idle, LED OFF, Buzzer OFF | | **Slightly Ajar** | 10–25 mm | Closed (still in pull-in range) | 0 V | **LOW (0)** | Idle (hysteresis zone) | | **Opened** | > 30 mm | Open | 5 V | **HIGH (1)** | **ALERT:** 3× Beep + LED Blink | | **Slammed** | 0 mm (vibration) | Closed (bouncing) | 0 V pulsing | Chattering | Debounced by 200 ms delay | ## 3.4 Expected Serial Monitor Output ``` Door Security System Initialized... >> ALERT: Door has been OPENED! << >> ALERT: Door has been OPENED! << ``` # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"] MAGNET["Permanent Magnet
Neodymium NdFeB
1000 Gauss"] REED["Reed Switch
SPST NO Type
Glass Envelope"] PULLUP["10k Pull-up Resistor
to VCC"] end subgraph PROCESSING["Processing Stage"] ARDUINO["Arduino Uno
ATmega328P
5V Logic"] end subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"] BUZZER["Piezo Buzzer
5V 20mA
2-4 kHz"] RLED["220 ohm Resistor"] LED["Red LED
Visual Indicator"] end MAGNET -. Magnetic Field .-> REED REED --- PULLUP REED -- "Digital Pin 2" --> ARDUINO ARDUINO -- "Pin 8 PWM" --> BUZZER ARDUINO -- "Pin 13" --> RLED RLED --> LED ``` ## 4.2 Operational Flow Chart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([System Boot]) --> initA["Initialize Pins
REED=INPUT_PULLUP
BUZZER=OUTPUT
LED=OUTPUT"] initA --> readA["Read REED_PIN
digitalRead"] readA --> checkA{"Door State == HIGH
AND
Previous == LOW?"} checkA -- "NO" --> idleA["Stay Idle
No Action"] checkA -- "YES" --> alarmA["Trigger Alarm:
Beep 3 times
LED Blink"] alarmA --> debounceA["Wait 200 ms
Debounce"] debounceA --> updateA["Update previousDoorState"] idleA --> readA updateA --> readA ``` ## 4.3 Pin Mapping Table | Arduino Pin | Connected To | Direction | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Digital 2** | Reed Switch + Pull-up | Input | Door state sensing | | **Digital 8** | Buzzer (+) | Output | Audible alert | | **Digital 13** | Red LED via 220 Ω | Output | Visual indicator | | **5V** | Pull-up resistor top | Power | Reference voltage | | **GND** | Buzzer (−), LED cathode | Ground | Common return path | ## 4.4 Hardware Assembly Diagram (Mermaid Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DOOR["Door Assembly"] DOORLEAF["Door Leaf
Magnet Mounted Here"] DOORFRAME["Door Frame
Reed Switch Mounted Here"] end subgraph CIRCUIT["Breadboard Circuit"] BREAD["Breadboard
Power Rails + Tie Points"] end subgraph MCU["Arduino Board"] MCU1["ATmega328P
USB Powered"] end DOORLEAF -.->|gap 5-15mm| DOORFRAME DOORFRAME -->|2 wires| BREAD BREAD -->|D2 + GND| MCU1 MCU1 -->|D8 + D13| BREAD BREAD -->|Buzzer + LED| BREAD ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define a reed switch and state its two main types. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **Model Answer:** A reed switch is a hermetically sealed electromechanical switch with two ferromagnetic contacts inside a glass envelope, actuated by an external magnetic field. **Two main types:** 1. **Normally Open (NO):** Contacts close in presence of a magnetic field. (Used in this experiment.) 2. **Normally Closed (NC):** Contacts open in presence of a magnetic field. A third variant is the **Changeover (SPDT)** type, which has a common terminal that switches between NO and NC positions. --- ### Q2. Why is a pull-up resistor necessary when interfacing a reed switch with a microcontroller? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Model Answer:** A reed switch has only two terminals with no internal reference voltage. When the switch is **open**, the microcontroller input pin is **floating** and picks up electromagnetic noise, causing false triggering. A pull-up resistor (typically 10 kΩ) ties the pin to $V_{CC}$ (5V), ensuring a defined **HIGH** logic level when the switch is open. When the switch closes, it shorts the pin to GND, giving a clean **LOW** level. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Both sub-parts must be answered. Below are TWO alternative questions for internal choice practice. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]` **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a door lock monitoring system using a reed switch, magnet, Arduino, and buzzer. Explain the working principle in detail. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The circuit consists of: - A **reed switch** mounted on the door frame with one terminal to **GND** and the other to **Digital Pin 2** through a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor to 5V. - A **neodymium magnet** mounted on the door leaf, aligned to the reed switch when the door is closed. - A **piezo buzzer** connected between **Digital Pin 8** and GND, with a 220 Ω series current-limiting resistor. - An **LED indicator** on **Pin 13** via 220 Ω for visual alert. **Working Principle:** When the door is closed, the magnet's field (≥ 1000 Gauss at the surface) polarizes the reeds, closing the switch and pulling the input pin to **GND (LOW)**. The Arduino loop reads this LOW state and remains idle. When the door is opened, the magnet moves beyond the **pull-in distance (~ 25 mm)**, the reeds spring apart, and the pull-up resistor pulls the input pin to **5V (HIGH)**. The Arduino detects this rising edge (LOW → HIGH) and triggers an alarm: three short beeps of 150 ms each with the LED blinking in sync. A 200 ms debounce delay prevents multiple false triggers from switch bounce. **(b)** Write the complete Arduino code for the above system. Explain how debouncing is implemented and why it is necessary. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** (Code provided in Section 3.2 above.) **Debouncing Explanation:** Reed switches have **mechanical contact bounce** — the reeds vibrate and make/break contact multiple times in the first 1–2 ms after closure. Without debouncing, the microcontroller would register these as multiple door-open events, producing a continuous beep instead of a single 3-beep alert. **Implementation:** A `delay(200)` is inserted after triggering the alarm. Additionally, the code uses **edge detection** (`currentDoorState == HIGH && previousDoorState == LOW`) which only triggers on a LOW→HIGH transition, inherently filtering out bouncing within the same state. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Pattern]` **(a)** Explain the construction and operating principle of a reed switch with a neat diagram. Compare it with a Hall-effect sensor. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction:** A reed switch consists of two flat, springy ferromagnetic reeds (Ni-Fe alloy) sealed inside a glass tube filled with inert nitrogen gas. The reeds act as both the magnetic flux concentrators and the electrical contacts. **Operating Principle:** When an external magnetic field is applied, each reed becomes magnetized with opposite polarities at the tips. The resulting attractive force bends the reeds until they touch, closing the circuit. When the field is removed, the elastic spring force of the reeds returns them to the open position. | Parameter | Reed Switch | Hall-Effect Sensor | |---|---|---| | **Output Type** | Mechanical switch (ON/OFF) | Analog or digital voltage proportional to B-field | | **Active Element** | Ferromagnetic reeds | Semiconductor (Si/InSb) | | **Response Time** | ~ 1 ms (mechanical) | ~ 1 μs (solid-state) | | **Lifespan** | 10⁶ – 10⁸ operations | Virtually unlimited | | **Power Required** | None (passive) | Requires supply voltage | | **Bounce** | Yes (needs debouncing) | No (no bounce) | | **Cost** | Low | Moderate | **(b)** Design a multi-zone door security system using 3 reed switches and 3 buzzers.
