Sketch the block diagram and write the algorithm. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (Mermaid):** ```mermaid flowchart LR RS1["Reed Switch 1
Front Door"] --> A1["Arduino
Pins 2,3,4"] RS2["Reed Switch 2
Back Door"] --> A1 RS3["Reed Switch 3
Window"] --> A1 A1 --> B1["Buzzer 1
Pin 8"] A1 --> B2["Buzzer 2
Pin 9"] A1 --> B3["Buzzer 3
Pin 10"] ``` **Algorithm:** 1. Initialize pins 2, 3, 4 as `INPUT_PULLUP`. 2. Initialize pins 8, 9, 10 as `OUTPUT` for buzzers. 3. In `loop()`: read all three reed switches. 4. If Switch 1 is HIGH → Beep Buzzer 1 (1000 Hz pattern). 5. If Switch 2 is HIGH → Beep Buzzer 2 (1500 Hz pattern). 6. If Switch 3 is HIGH → Beep Buzzer 3 (2000 Hz pattern). 7. Apply 200 ms debounce per channel. 8. Print zone identification to Serial Monitor. This allows the user to know **which entry point** was breached, a feature not possible with a single-switch system. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Viva Trap:** Examiners often ask *"What happens if you reverse the magnet polarity?"* — Answer: Nothing changes functionally; a reed switch is polarity-agnostic because magnetization is induced, not fixed. > 2. **Code Trap:** Writing `digitalRead` without `INPUT_PULLUP` and *forgetting* to enable the pull-up causes random buzzer triggering from electrical noise. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this. > 3. **Wiring Trap:** Connecting the buzzer directly between 5V and a pin (without series resistor) will work briefly but stresses the pin's internal MOSFET. Always include the 220 Ω resistor. > 4. **Conceptual Trap:** Confusing "magnetic field" with "magnetic flux." Use *magnetic field strength B* (in Tesla or Gauss) when discussing reed switch activation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **reed switch** is a passive magnetic-field-actuated mechanical switch sealed in a glass envelope. - ✅ A **permanent magnet** (typically neodymium) provides the activating magnetic field. - ✅ The **pull-up resistor (10 kΩ)** is mandatory to define logic levels when the switch is open. - ✅ The **buzzer** requires a **220 Ω current-limiting resistor** to protect the Arduino pin. - ✅ **Debouncing (200 ms)** is critical to prevent false multiple triggers from switch bounce. - ✅ **Edge detection** (`prev != current`) is the preferred software debounce technique. - ✅ Typical **activation distance** is **10–30 mm** for a small neodymium magnet. - ✅ The reed switch is **polarity-independent** — orientation of the magnet does not matter. - ✅ Reed switches are **passive** (no power required), unlike Hall-effect sensors. - ✅ Always use **`INPUT_PULLUP`** in `pinMode()` to enable the internal pull-up resistor in Arduino. - ✅ The Serial Monitor baud rate must be set to **9600** to match `Serial.begin(9600)`. - ✅ In KTU viva, be ready to explain the **difference between reed switch and Hall effect sensor**. - ✅ The expected serial output on door opening is: `>> ALERT: Door has been OPENED! <<` - ✅ The alarm pattern in this experiment is **3 short beeps of 150 ms** with **150 ms** gaps in between.

topics.title: Control a 230V device (Bulb) with Raspberry Pi using a relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Control a 230V device (Bulb) with Raspberry Pi using a relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Relay-based switching** is an electromechanical isolation technique that enables a low-power, low-voltage controller (such as the Raspberry Pi's **3.3 V GPIO** pins) to safely switch a high-power AC load (such as a **230 V, 50 Hz** incandescent bulb) without any direct electrical contact between the two circuits. The Raspberry Pi GPIO pin energizes a coil inside the relay; the magnetic field generated by the coil mechanically actuates a switch (the **Common-Changeover-No Normally Open / Normally Closed** contacts) that physically connects or disconnects the AC mains to the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCIL607 – Module 2):** This experiment falls under the "Interfacing Actuators and Switching Devices" cluster, where students must demonstrate hardware isolation, GPIO configuration (BCM numbering), relay driver circuitry, and basic AC load switching.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorman" Model
Think of the relay as a **doorman standing at the door of a high-voltage room**:

- The Raspberry Pi GPIO is the **building receptionist** who is *not allowed* inside the high-voltage room.
- The receptionist does **not** touch the 230 V bulb directly. Instead, he shouts a command ("energize the coil!").
- The **doorman (relay coil)** hears the command and uses his **own strength** (magnetic force from the 12 V / 5 V coil) to **open or close the door** (switch the COM-NO contact).
- The **bulb (230 V AC load)** is inside the high-voltage room and is connected/disconnected purely by the doorman.
- This way, the **receptionist (Pi)** and the **bulb** never come into physical (electrical) contact — the doorman provides **galvanic isolation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Galvanic Isolation** means there is *zero conductive path* between the control circuit (Raspberry Pi) and the load circuit (230 V mains). The only coupling is magnetic/mechanical. This is the single most important safety property exploited in industrial control.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards Used
- **Mains Voltage (India / Kerala):** **230 V AC, 50 Hz**
- **Raspberry Pi GPIO Logic High:** **3.3 V DC** (NOT 5 V — this is a frequent board-fry mistake)
- **Maximum GPIO Source/Sink Current:** **≈ 16 mA per pin (recommended 2–5 mA)**
- **Typical Relay Coil Voltage:** **5 V DC** (most relay modules) or 12 V DC
- **Relay Coil Current:** **≈ 70–100 mA** (for a single 5 V Songle SRD-05VDC-SL-C)
- **IEC Safety Standard for Lab Mains Wiring:** **IEC 61010**, double-insulated
- **Standard Bulb (Incandescent) for Test:** **230 V / 60 W / 100 W**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of an Electromechanical Relay
1. **Energization Phase:** When the Raspberry Pi GPIO outputs **HIGH (3.3 V)**, current flows into the relay coil.
2. **Magnetic Field Generation:** The coil (a solenoid wound on an iron core) produces a magnetic flux **Φ** proportional to the number of turns *N* and current *I*.
3. **Mechanical Attraction:** The magnetic field attracts a ferrous armature that is mechanically linked to the moving contact.
4. **Contact Switching:** The armature moves the contact from the **Normally Closed (NC)** terminal to the **Normally Open (NO)** terminal, completing the AC circuit.
5. **De-energization:** When GPIO goes **LOW**, a **flyback diode** across the coil dissipates the back-EMF, and a spring returns the contact to NC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the flyback diode?** When the coil is de-energized, the collapsing magnetic field induces a high reverse voltage spike ($V_L = -L \cdot dI/dt$) that can reach hundreds of volts. The **1N4007** diode clamps this spike and protects the GPIO transistor.

### 2.2 The Need for a Driver Transistor
The Raspberry Pi GPIO can safely source only about **3–5 mA** at 3.3 V, but a typical 5 V relay coil demands **~70–100 mA**. Directly driving the relay from the GPIO will **not switch reliably** and may **damage the SoC**. Hence an NPN transistor (commonly **BC547** or the S8050 on relay boards) is used as a **low-side switch**:

| Parameter | Raspberry Pi GPIO | Relay Coil Demand |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage | 3.3 V | 5 V |
| Current | ~3–5 mA | ~70–100 mA |
| Power | ~10 mW | ~500 mW |

The GPIO drives the **base** of the transistor through a base resistor $R_B$, and the relay coil is connected between **Vcc (+5 V)** and the **collector**. When the GPIO is HIGH, the transistor saturates and energizes the coil.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{CC}$ | Relay coil supply voltage | **5** | V | External supply to relay module Vcc pin |
| $V_{GPIO}$ | GPIO logic HIGH voltage | **3.3** | V | DO NOT connect to 5 V |
| $V_{BE}$ | Base-emitter voltage (Si NPN) | **0.7** | V | Cut-in voltage of BC547 |
| $I_C$ | Relay coil current | $V_{CC} / R_{coil}$ | A | Measured ~70 mA for SRD-05VDC |
| $I_B$ | Base current required | $I_C / \beta$ | A | $\beta$ (DC gain) of BC547 ≈ 100–200 |
| $R_B$ | Base resistor | $(V_{GPIO} - V_{BE}) / I_B$ | $\Omega$ | Computed, typically 1 kΩ – 4.7 kΩ |
| $V_{flyback}$ | Flyback spike voltage | $-L \cdot dI/dt$ | V | Clamped by 1N4007 |
| $P_{bulb}$ | Bulb power | 60 W / 100 W | W | Standard KTU lab test value |
| $I_{AC}$ | AC current drawn by bulb | $P / V$ | A | For 100 W bulb → ~0.43 A |
| $P_{switch\_rating}$ | Relay contact rating | 10 A / 250 V AC | — | Always check before connecting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-pipe safeguard applied:** All absolute-value / condition notations in the formulas above use descriptive words instead of the `\|` symbol to protect markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Why an Optocoupler is Preferred in Industrial Designs
While student relay modules typically use a simple transistor driver, **industrial-grade isolation** is achieved with an **optocoupler (e.g., PC817)**:
- An **LED** on the input side is driven by the GPIO.
- A **phototransistor** on the output side conducts only when light from the LED falls on it.
- This provides **4 kV galvanic isolation** between Pi and the relay coil.
- The Raspberry Pi then drives the optocoupler LED, and the optocoupler output drives the relay coil — **double isolation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Base Resistor Calculation for Transistor Driver

**Given:** Raspberry Pi GPIO HIGH = 3.3 V, Relay coil resistance (measured) = 70 Ω, Transistor β = 100 (worst case), V_BE = 0.7 V.

**Step 1 — Compute the coil current required.**

$$I_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the typical values (V_CC = 5 V, V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V, R_coil = 70 Ω).**

$$I_C = \frac{5 - 0.2}{70} = \frac{4.8}{70}$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division.**

$$I_C = 0.0686 \text{ A} \approx 68.6 \text{ mA}$$

This is the **collector current** the relay coil needs.

**Step 4 — Compute the required base current to saturate the transistor.**

$$I_B = \frac{I_C}{\beta} = \frac{68.6 \text{ mA}}{100} = 0.686 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 5 — Apply an overdrive factor of 2 (to ensure hard saturation).**

$$I_{B(actual)} = 2 \times I_B = 1.37 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the base resistor $R_B$ from KVL on the input loop.**

$$V_{GPIO} = I_B \cdot R_B + V_{BE}$$

$$R_B = \frac{V_{GPIO} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \frac{3.3 - 0.7}{1.37 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_B = \frac{2.6}{0.00137} = 1897.8 \text{ }\Omega \approx 1.8 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 7 — Choose the nearest standard E12 value.**

$$R_B = 1.8 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (standard E12 value)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> On most commercial relay modules, the manufacturer has already placed a **S8050 NPN transistor + 1 kΩ base resistor + 1N4007 flyback diode** on the PCB. The student only wires the GPIO pin to the **IN** header.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — AC Bulb Current and Relay Contact De-rating

**Given:** Bulb = 100 W, Mains = 230 V, 50 Hz. Relay contact rating = 10 A, 250 V AC.

**Step 1 — Compute RMS current drawn by bulb.**

$$I_{AC(RMS)} = \frac{P}{V_{RMS}} = \frac{100}{230} = 0.4348 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Peak current (for relay contact inrush consideration, since tungsten bulbs draw ~10× at switch-on).**

$$I_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{RMS} = 1.414 \times 0.4348 = 0.6148 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Inrush current (cold tungsten filament ≈ 10× steady-state).**

$$I_{inrush} = 10 \times I_{RMS} = 10 \times 0.4348 = 4.348 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with relay contact rating.**

$$I_{inrush} = 4.348 \text{ A} \ll 10 \text{ A (relay rating)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Therefore a standard 10 A relay module is **safe** for a 100 W bulb, with a derating factor of **~43%** under inrush and only **~4.3%** under steady state. For loads with high inrush (motors, transformers), use **snubber circuits** or **solid-state relays (SSR)**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heatsink / Thermal Check on the Transistor

Power dissipated by the S8050 when relay is ON:

$$P_D = V_{CE(sat)} \times I_C = 0.2 \times 0.0686 = 0.0137 \text{ W}$$

Maximum dissipation of S8050 (TO-92 package, no heatsink): $P_{D(max)} = 0.625$ W at 25 °C.

$$\text{Thermal margin} = \frac{P_{D(max)} - P_D}{P_{D(max)}} \times 100 = \frac{0.625 - 0.0137}{0.625} \times 100 = 97.8\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transistor runs at only **~2.2%** of its rated dissipation — comfortable thermal margin for lab use.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — GPIO Pin Choices (BCM Numbering)

| BCM Pin | Physical Pin | Recommended Use | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GPIO 17** | Pin 11 | Relay IN1 (most common in KTU labs) | Default choice for this experiment |
| GPIO 27 | Pin 13 | Relay IN2 (for 2-channel modules) | Use if module has 2 channels |
| GPIO 22 | Pin 15 | Relay IN3 | Optional |
| 3V3 | Pin 1 | Relay Vcc (only for 3.3 V logic relays) | Use **only** if relay is 3.3 V rated |
| 5V | Pin 2/4 | Relay Vcc (for 5 V coil modules) | Connect to module Vcc, NOT to GPIO |
| GND | Pin 6/9/... | Common ground for Pi ↔ Relay module | **Mandatory** connection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Lab Pitfall:** Some students connect the relay **Vcc to 5 V** but the **IN pin to 3.3 V GPIO**. The transistor inside the relay module has its emitter at GND, so the 3.3 V GPIO HIGH still turns on the base transistor. This is **acceptable**. But connecting **5 V to a GPIO pin** will **permanently damage** the Pi's SoC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Overall System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PiCtrl["Raspberry Pi BCM 17 GPIO"] -->|3.3 V HIGH| TrDriver["NPN Transistor BC547 Base"]
    TrDriver -->|Saturated| RelayCoil["Relay Coil 5 V DC"]
    RelayCoil -->|Magnetic Flux| Armature["Armature Pulls COM to NO"]
    Armature -->|Closed Contact| ACLoad["230 V AC Bulb 60 W"]
    ACSource["230 V AC 50 Hz Mains"] -->|L Phase| COM["Relay COM Terminal"]
    Flyback["1N4007 Flyback Diode"] -.->|Suppresses Spike| RelayCoil
    GNDR["Common GND"] --> TrDriver
    GNDR --> PiCtrl
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Flowchart — Bulb ON / OFF Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init: GPIO.setmode BCM GPIO.setup 17 OUT
    Init --> BulbOff: GPIO.output 17 LOW
    BulbOff --> BulbOn: User Input OR Timer Trigger
    BulbOn --> RelayEnergize: GPIO.output 17 HIGH
    RelayEnergize --> CoilMagnetic: Current 70 mA through coil
    CoilMagnetic --> ContactClose: NO contact closes
    ContactClose --> BulbLit: 230 V applied to bulb
    BulbLit --> BulbOff: GPIO.output 17 LOW
    BulbOff --> Cleanup: GPIO.cleanup
    Cleanup --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Pin Connection Map (Pi ↔ Relay Module)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RPi["Raspberry Pi 40-Pin Header"]
        Pin1["Pin 1 - 3V3"] --- Pin2["Pin 2 - 5V"]
        Pin6["Pin 6 - GND"] --- Pin11["Pin 11 - GPIO17 BCM"]
    end
    subgraph Relay["5 V Single-Channel Relay Module"]
        Vcc["Vcc"] --- Gnd["GND"] --- In["IN"]
    end
    Pin2 -.->|Red 5V Wire| Vcc
    Pin6 -.->|Black GND Wire| Gnd
    Pin11 -.->|Yellow Signal| In
    subgraph Load["230 V AC Side"]
        Live["L Live 230V"] --- Com["COM"]
        No["NO"] --- Bulb["Bulb 60W"]
        Bulb --- Neutral["N Neutral"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ControlPlane["Control Plane Low Voltage 3.3 V DC"]
        AppLayer["Application Layer Python Logic"]
        GPIOLayer["GPIO Layer BCM Pin 17"]
        DriverLayer["Transistor Driver BC547 S8050"]
    end
    subgraph IsolationBoundary["Galvanic Isolation Boundary"]
        CoilMag["Relay Coil Magnetic Coupling"]
    end
    subgraph PowerPlane["Power Plane High Voltage 230 V AC"]
        ContactSwitch["COM-NO Contact Switch"]
        LoadBulb["230 V 60 W Bulb"]
    end
    AppLayer --> GPIOLayer --> DriverLayer --> CoilMag
    CoilMag --> ContactSwitch --> LoadBulb
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **galvanic isolation boundary** is the most safety-critical conceptual layer. The student must understand that **no copper wire** crosses this boundary — only magnetic flux does. This is the foundation of industrial relay logic (PLC racks, contactors, motor starters).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**A1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCIL607, May 2024]**
*Why can't a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin directly drive a 230 V AC bulb? List two reasons.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Voltage Mismatch:** The Pi GPIO outputs a maximum of **3.3 V**, but the bulb requires **230 V AC**. There is no way to step up 3.3 V DC to 230 V AC using only the GPIO pin.
2. **Current Mismatch:** The GPIO can safely source only **~3–5 mA**, whereas a 100 W bulb draws **~435 mA** of AC current. Driving it directly would damage the SoC.
3. **Galvanic Isolation Required:** Connecting 230 V mains to the Pi's GPIO would create a direct conductive path, destroying the Pi and creating an electric-shock hazard. A **relay provides galvanic isolation** between the low-voltage control circuit and the high-voltage AC load.
*(Any two well-explained points — 3 marks)*

**A2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCIL607, Dec 2023]**
*What is the function of the flyback diode connected across a relay coil?*

**Model Answer:**
The flyback diode (typically **1N4007**) is connected in **reverse-bias** across the relay coil. When the GPIO switches LOW and the coil is de-energized, the collapsing magnetic field induces a high reverse-EMF spike ($V_L = -L \cdot dI/dt$), which can reach hundreds of volts and destroy the driving transistor. The diode becomes forward-biased by this spike, **clamping the voltage** to roughly 0.7 V and safely circulating the stored coil energy as heat. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how a Raspberry Pi can be used to switch a 230 V, 60 W incandescent bulb using a 5 V single-channel relay module. Mention all pin connections using BCM numbering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A relay coil has a resistance of 100 Ω and is to be switched by a 2N2222 transistor with β = 100. The Raspberry Pi GPIO supplies 3.3 V on HIGH. Calculate the required base resistor $R_B$ for hard saturation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Raspberry Pi side:** Configure GPIO 17 (BCM) as OUTPUT using the `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` and `GPIO.setup(17, GPIO.OUT)` calls. The user application writes `GPIO.output(17, GPIO.HIGH)` to switch ON and `GPIO.LOW` to switch OFF.

2. **Wiring connections (BCM numbering):**
   - **5 V (Pin 2)** → Relay module **Vcc** (red wire)
   - **GND (Pin 6)** → Relay module **GND** (black wire)
   - **GPIO 17 (Pin 11)** → Relay module **IN** (yellow signal wire)

3. **Relay module internals:** The IN pin drives the base of an on-board S8050 NPN transistor through a 1 kΩ resistor. When GPIO 17 goes HIGH, the transistor saturates and energizes the 5 V coil. A 1N4007 flyback diode protects the transistor.

4. **230 V AC load side:** The **Live (L)** wire of the 230 V AC mains is connected to the relay's **COM** terminal. The **NO (Normally Open)** terminal is connected to one terminal of the **bulb**, and the other terminal of the bulb returns to the **Neutral (N)** of the mains.

5. **Operation:** When GPIO 17 = HIGH → coil energised → armature pulls COM to NO → bulb receives 230 V → bulb **GLOWS**. When GPIO 17 = LOW → coil de-energised → spring returns COM to NC → bulb is **OFF**.

6. **Isolation diagram:** Show that no copper wire crosses between the 3.3 V DC control side and the 230 V AC load side; only magnetic flux inside the relay couples them.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_{coil} = 100 \text{ }\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{GPIO} = 3.3$ V, $V_{CC} = 5$ V, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2$ V.

**Step 1 — Compute coil current (collector current).**

$$I_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}} = \frac{5 - 0.2}{100} = \frac{4.8}{100} = 48 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute minimum base current for saturation.**

$$I_{B(min)} = \frac{I_C}{\beta} = \frac{48 \text{ mA}}{100} = 0.48 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Apply overdrive factor of 2 for hard saturation.**

$$I_{B(actual)} = 2 \times 0.48 \text{ mA} = 0.96 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Compute base resistor using KVL on input loop.**

$$R_B = \frac{V_{GPIO} - V_{BE}}{I_{B(actual)}} = \frac{3.3 - 0.7}{0.96 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.6}{0.00096}$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$R_B = 2708.33 \text{ }\Omega \approx 2.7 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use the nearest **E12 standard value: 2.7 kΩ**. **(7 marks complete)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **galvanic isolation** in relay-based switching. Differentiate between an **electromechanical relay (EMR)** and a **solid-state relay (SSR)** with respect to switching speed, life cycle, noise, and AC load compatibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Python control loop that toggles a 230 V bulb through a relay at 2-second intervals. Use BCM pin 17 and include proper `try/except/finally` cleanup. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never connect 5 V to a GPIO input pin** — this is a top-priority deduction (often 2 marks + remark). The Pi is 3.3 V logic only.
> 2. **Always include `GPIO.cleanup()`** in a `finally:` block. Missing this loses 1–2 marks and shows poor lab discipline.
> 3. **Use `GPIO.BCM` numbering** and mention it explicitly. Using BOARD numbering when the question specifies BCM is a common slip.
> 4. **Mention the flyback diode** when explaining the coil driver. A sketch of the diode's polarity (cathode to +Vcc) is frequently worth 1 mark.
> 5. **Never demonstrate the 230 V connection live** in the lab without faculty supervision and proper insulation. The KTU external examiner will deduct marks for unsafe wiring.

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Galvanic Isolation & EMR vs SSR (7 marks):**

**Galvanic Isolation (3 marks):**
Galvanic isolation is the principle of separating two electrical circuits so that **no direct conductive path (no copper wire) exists** between them, while still allowing signal or power transfer through a non-conductive medium. In a relay, the control circuit (Pi GPIO at 3.3 V) and the load circuit (230 V AC mains) are coupled only through the **magnetic field** of the coil, providing complete electrical isolation. This prevents fault voltages, surges, or short circuits on the AC side from reaching and destroying the low-voltage Pi.

**EMR vs SSR Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid-State Relay (SSR) |
|---|---|---|
| Switching Element | Moving armature + contacts | Triac / MOSFET / IGBT |
| Switching Speed | 5–15 ms (slow) | <1 µs (very fast) |
| Mechanical Life | ~10⁵ to 10⁶ operations | >10⁹ operations (no moving parts) |
| Contact Bounce | Yes (1–2 ms) | No (bounce-less) |
| EMI / Acoustic Noise | Yes (audible click + arc) | Silent, no arcing |
| On-state Voltage Drop | ~0 V (closed metal contact) | 1–2 V across triac (heats up) |
| Off-state Leakage | 0 (open air gap) | Few mA (snubber leakage) |
| AC Load Suitability | Excellent (any voltage within rating) | Excellent (zero-crossing variants) |
| DC Load Suitability | Good | Excellent (DC SSRs available) |
| Cost | Low (₹30–₹80) | Higher (₹150–₹500) |

**(b) Python Control Loop (7 marks):**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

RELAY_PIN = 17   # BCM numbering

# --- Initialisation ---
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
GPIO.setup(RELAY_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

try:
    while True:
        GPIO.output(RELAY_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)   # Relay ON, bulb glows
        print("Bulb ON")
        time.sleep(2)
        GPIO.output(RELAY_PIN, GPIO.LOW)    # Relay OFF, bulb dark
        print("Bulb OFF")
        time.sleep(2)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("User interrupted — stopping.")
finally:
    GPIO.output(RELAY_PIN, GPIO.LOW)        # Safety: bulb OFF
    GPIO.cleanup()                          # Release GPIO resources
    print("GPIO cleanup complete.")
```

**Explanation of the code (briefly):**
- `setmode(GPIO.BCM)` ensures we use Broadcom pin numbering.
- `setup(17, OUT)` configures the pin as an output.
- The `while True` loop alternates the relay every 2 s.
- The `finally` block guarantees that the relay is switched OFF and the GPIO pins are reset — critical for safety in a lab environment.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Galvanic isolation** is the *single most important safety property* of the relay; never bypass it.
- Raspberry Pi GPIO is **3.3 V logic** with a current limit of **~3–5 mA per pin** — never source/sink more.
- A typical **5 V relay module** contains a **transistor driver (S8050/BC547)**, a **1 kΩ base resistor**, and a **1N4007 flyback diode** — do not remove any of these.
- The **Base Resistor Formula** is $R_B = (V_{GPIO} - V_{BE}) / I_B$, with overdrive factor of 2 to ensure hard saturation.
- A **100 W / 230 V bulb** draws **~0.43 A RMS** and **~4.3 A inrush** at switch-on — well within the 10 A rating of a standard relay.
- The **NO (Normally Open)** terminal is used for ON/OFF switching; **NC (Normally Closed)** is used when the load must be ON by default and switched OFF by the relay.
- **Use `GPIO.BCM`** numbering unless the question explicitly asks for BOARD numbering.
- **Always include `GPIO.cleanup()`** in a `finally:` block.
- **Never connect 5 V to a GPIO pin** — only to the relay module's Vcc terminal.
- For **industrial isolation**, use an **optocoupler (PC817)** between the Pi and the relay coil.
- **Solid-State Relays (SSRs)** are preferred for high-cycle, silent, and bounce-free switching.
- **Always turn OFF the mains breaker** before wiring the 230 V side, and have it inspected by the lab faculty before powering up.
- **Examination tip:** When asked to "explain" the experiment, always draw a **3-zone block diagram** — Control / Isolation / Load — and label every terminal (BCM pin, Vcc, GND, COM, NO, NC, L, N).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control a 230V device using a threshold temperature using a temperature sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Control a 230V device using a threshold temperature using a temperature sensor # 🧪 Module Experiment 2 — Threshold-Based Temperature Control of a 230 V AC Load > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme | Lab Outcome Mapping** > This experiment maps to **CO2**: *Design and implement sensor interfacing circuits with microcontrollers for real-world control applications* and **CO4**: *Demonstrate signal conditioning, actuation, and isolation techniques for high-voltage loads.* It is a **Part A (Record + Viva)** lab evaluation item worth **20 marks** (Record: 10 + Viva: 5 + Execution: 5). --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > **Threshold-Based Temperature Control System** is a closed-loop embedded control application in which a temperature transducer (sensor) converts the physical thermal quantity into a measurable electrical signal, a microcontroller digitizes and processes this signal, compares it against a **predefined set-point (threshold)**, and actuates an electromechanical/electromagnetic switching device (relay) to energize or de-energize a **230 V, 50 Hz AC mains load** (lamp, fan, heater, solenoid valve, etc.). The system architecture is a classic **Sense → Process → Actuate** chain, also known as a **Sense-Compute-Actuate (SCA)** paradigm in IoT and embedded systems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English) Think of the system as a **smart bimetallic thermostat** — the kind in old refrigerators and geysers. However, instead of a mechanical strip bending to make/break a contact, we have: | Mechanical Thermostat (Old Way) | Electronic Threshold Controller (This Lab) | |---|---| | Bimetallic strip senses heat | **LM35** semiconductor sensor senses heat | | Metal bends and physically touches a contact | **Arduino** digitally compares the reading | | Contact closes → current flows → heater turns ON | **Relay coil energizes** → contacts close → **230 V device turns ON** | | Slow, drifts, inaccurate, no display | Fast, precise (0.5 °C), display possible, programmable | > [!NOTE] > **Why use a relay and not a transistor/MOSFET directly to switch 230 V?** > - A **230 V AC** signal cannot be switched by a low-voltage DC transistor safely (insulation breakdown risk). > - A relay provides **galvanic isolation** — the Arduino's 5 V DC side and the 230 V AC mains side are **electrically separated**, only coupled magnetically through the coil. > - A relay can also handle the **high inrush current** of inductive loads (fans, solenoids). ### 1.3 System Specifications (Per Lab Standard) | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---| | Mains Supply (Load Side) | **230 V AC, 50 Hz, Single Phase** | | Control Supply (Logic Side) | **+5 V DC** (from Arduino) | | Sensor Supply | **+5 V DC** | | Sensor Type | **LM35** (Precision Centigrade Temperature Sensor) | | Sensor Output | **10 mV / °C** (linear, analog) | | Temperature Range | **0 °C to 150 °C** (LM35) | | Microcontroller | **Arduino Uno (ATmega328P, 10-bit ADC)** | | ADC Reference Voltage | **+5.0 V** | | ADC Resolution | **5 V / 1024 = 4.88 mV per step** | | Temperature Resolution | **4.88 mV / 10 mV/°C ≈ 0.488 °C/step** | | Switching Element | **5 V Single-Channel Relay Module (with optocoupler & flyback diode)** | | Relay Contact Rating | **10 A @ 250 V AC** (typical) | > [!TIP] > **Why 0.488 °C resolution?** > The smallest voltage change the Arduino's 10-bit ADC can detect is **V_ref / 2¹⁰ = 5 / 1024 ≈ 4.88 mV**. Since the LM35 outputs **10 mV per °C**, the smallest temperature change detectable is **4.88 mV / 10 mV/°C = 0.488 °C**. This is the **quantization error** of the system. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Block-Level Operational Theory The complete signal flow is decomposed into **five cascaded blocks**: 1. **Sensing Stage (LM35)** - LM35 is a **three-terminal (Vcc, GND, Vout)** precision IC sensor manufactured by Texas Instruments. - Internally, it uses a **bandgap reference** and matched transistors whose V_BE varies at **-2 mV/°C**, which is then internally amplified and level-shifted to produce a positive **+10 mV/°C** linear output. - The output is **calibrated directly in Celsius** — no external calibration is needed (unlike thermistors which require Steinhart-Hart equation). - At **0 °C**, the output is **0 mV**; at **100 °C**, it is **1000 mV (1 V)**. 2. **Signal Conditioning Stage (Voltage Divider / Direct Input)** - Since LM35 output is **0–1 V** for **0–100 °C**, which is well within the Arduino's **0–5 V ADC range**, **no external conditioning** is required for most applications. - For temperatures above ~100 °C (e.g., industrial ovens using LM35 up to 150 °C), the output would be 1.5 V — still within range. - **For TMP36** (which has a 500 mV offset at 25 °C), the formula changes (see formula sheet). 3. **Digitization Stage (Arduino ADC)** - The ATmega328P has a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** ADC. - The analog voltage on pin **A0** is compared against a resistor-ladder-derived reference, and the closest of **1024 discrete levels (2¹⁰)** is selected. - The result is a **digital word from 0 to 1023**. 4. **Processing & Decision Stage (Arduino Sketch)** - The digital count is converted back to a voltage, then multiplied by **100** to get °C. - The computed temperature is **compared** with the user-defined threshold (e.g., **T_threshold = 30 °C**). - The output of the comparison drives a **digital I/O pin HIGH or LOW**. 5. **Actuation Stage (Relay + 230 V Load)** - The Arduino pin (max **40 mA source/sink**) **cannot directly drive the relay coil** (typically 70–100 mA), so the relay module has a **driver transistor (BC547 or similar) + flyback diode + optocoupler** built in. - When the Arduino pin goes **HIGH**, the transistor saturates, the coil energizes, the magnetic field pulls the common (COM) contact to the normally-open (NO) contact, and the 230 V circuit is completed. - When the pin is **LOW**, the spring-loaded armature returns the contact to **NC (Normally Closed)**, breaking the 230 V circuit. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these three equations — they account for 70 % of any derivation/proof question in the viva.** | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{out} = 10 \cdot T$ | LM35 transfer function (linear) | $V_{out}$ in V, $T$ in °C | | 2 | $\text{ADC\_count} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 1024 \right\rfloor$ | Arduino 10-bit ADC quantization | dimensionless integer (0–1023) | | 3 | $T \left(°C\right) = \dfrac{\text{ADC\_count} \cdot V_{ref}}{1024} \cdot \dfrac{1}{10\,\text{mV/°C}}$ | Recovering temperature from ADC | °C | | 4 | $T = \text{ADC\_count} \cdot 0.488$ | Simplified (with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V) | °C | | 5 | $\Delta T_{min} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{1024 \cdot S_{sensor}}$ | Smallest detectable temperature change | °C | | 6 | $\Delta T_{min} = \dfrac{5.0}{1024 \cdot 0.01} = 0.488\,°C$ | Plug-in for LM35 | °C | | 7 | $T_{TMP36} = \dfrac{\text{ADC} \cdot 5.0}{1024 \cdot 10\,\text{mV}} - 50$ | TMP36 sensor formula (500 mV offset) | °C | | 8 | $f_{ADC} \le \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot R_{source} \cdot C_{hold}}$ | ADC bandwidth limit (Nyquist) | Hz | ### 2.3 Why Hysteresis (Schmitt Trigger Behavior) is Critical > [!WARNING] > **This is the most common viva question and the #1 reason students lose marks.** **Problem — Relay Chattering:** If the actual temperature hovers around the threshold (say 30.0 °C), the relay will switch ON and OFF **dozens of times per second** due to electrical noise and ADC quantization (recall 0.488 °C step). This **chattering** causes: - Mechanical wear of the relay contacts → reduced relay life (from 100 000 to 5 000 operations). - EMI (electromagnetic interference) that can reset the Arduino. - Audible buzzing and visible flicker on the 230 V load. **Solution — Add Hysteresis Band:** $$\text{Upper Threshold (T_{H})} = T_{set} + \Delta T$$ $$\text{Lower Threshold (T_{L})} = T_{set} - \Delta T$$ $$\text{State} = \begin{cases} \text{ON, if } T \ge T_{H} \\ \text{OFF, if } T \le T_{L} \\ \text{No change, if } T_{L} < T < T_{H} \end{cases}$$ Typical $\Delta T$ = **2 °C to 5 °C**. This is essentially a **software Schmitt trigger**. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Domestic:** Geyser control, AC thermostat, refrigerator cooling, aquarium heater. - **Industrial:** Overtemperature protection in motor windings (PTC sensors), server-room cooling, plastic injection molding barrel temperature control, cold-chain monitoring in pharma cold storage. - **Automotive:** Engine coolant fan triggering, cabin climate control. - **Agriculture:** Greenhouse ventilation, soil heating mats in hydroponics. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: LM35 Voltage to Temperature Conversion in Arduino We are given: - $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V (Arduino's default analog reference) - ADC resolution = $2^{10} = 1024$ levels - LM35 sensitivity = $S = 10$ mV/°C = $0.010$ V/°C **Step 1 — ADC counts the analog input voltage.** The ATmega328P's SAR ADC divides $V_{in}$ into 1024 equal steps of size $\Delta V = V_{ref}/1024$. The output is the integer index of the step closest to $V_{in}$: $$\text{ADC} = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 1024\right)$$ **Step 2 — Invert the equation to find $V_{in}$ from the ADC reading.** $$V_{in} = \frac{\text{ADC} \cdot V_{ref}}{1024} = \frac{\text{ADC} \cdot 5.0}{1024}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the LM35 transfer function.** From the data sheet: $V_{in} = S \cdot T = 0.010 \cdot T$ **Step 4 — Solve for T by equating the two expressions for $V_{in}$.** $$\begin{aligned} 0.010 \cdot T &= \frac{\text{ADC} \cdot 5.0}{1024} \\ T &= \frac{\text{ADC} \cdot 5.0}{1024 \cdot 0.010} \\ T &= \frac{\text{ADC} \cdot 5.0}{10.24} \\ T &= \text{ADC} \cdot \frac{5.0}{10.24} \\ T &= \text{ADC} \cdot 0.48828125 \end{aligned}$$ > **Final Working Equation (for the Arduino code):** > $$\boxed{\,T(°C) = \text{analogRead(A0)} \times 0.488\,}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (For a Typical Viva) **Q:** The Arduino's A0 pin reads an ADC value of **307** from an LM35. Calculate (a) the sensor output voltage, (b) the measured temperature, and (c) the relay state if threshold = 30 °C. **Solution:** **(a) Sensor output voltage:** $$V_{in} = \frac{307 \times 5.0}{1024} = 1.4990 \text{ V} \approx 1.5 \text{ V}$$ **(b) Temperature:** $$T = \frac{1.4990}{0.010} = 149.9\,°C \;\;\text{or}\;\; T = 307 \times 0.488 = 149.8\,°C$$ **(c) Relay state:** $$T = 149.8\,°C > T_{threshold} = 30\,°C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Relay} = \textbf{HIGH (Load ON)}$$ > [!NOTE] > A reading of 149.8 °C is **valid** for the LM35 (max 150 °C) but **dangerous** in most lab settings. This is why the threshold should be set well below sensor limits. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Justification | Decision | Chosen Component | Rejected Alternative | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Temperature sensor | **LM35** | Thermistor (NTC) | LM35 is linear & calibrated; thermistor needs Steinhart-Hart polynomial and is non-linear | | Microcontroller | **Arduino Uno** | Raspberry Pi | Pi is overkill (needs OS boot), expensive; Uno is real-time, cheap, 10-bit ADC sufficient | | Switching element | **Electromechanical Relay** | SSR (Solid State Relay) | Relay is cheaper for lab; SSR has zero-crossing switching — no chattering issue but pricier | | Relay driver | **Transistor + Optocoupler (on module)** | Direct Arduino pin | Coil current (~80 mA) exceeds Arduino pin source (40 mA); optocoupler provides isolation | | Load | **Incandescent bulb (resistive)** | Inductive motor (for first trial) | Inductive loads have high inrush — good practice to test with resistive load first | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Thermal Energy
from Environment] --> B[LM35
Temperature Sensor] B -->|Analog Voltage
10 mV per Celsius| C[Arduino Uno
ATmega328P ADC] C -->|10-bit Digital
0 to 1023| D[Processing
Compute Temperature] D -->|Temperature in Celsius| E[Comparator
Threshold Check] E -->|T greater than Threshold| F[Digital Pin HIGH
5 Volts] E -->|T less than Threshold| G[Digital Pin LOW
0 Volts] F --> H[Relay Module
Driver Transistor] G --> H H -->|Electromagnetic
Coil Energized| I[Relay Contacts Close
COM to NO] I --> J[230 Volt AC
50 Hz Mains Load ON] H -->|Coil De energized| K[Relay Contacts Open
COM to NC] K --> L[230 Volt AC
Load OFF] D --> M[Serial Monitor
Display Temperature] D --> N[LCD Display
16 by 2 Optional] classDef sensor fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef mcu fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000 classDef actr fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef load fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000 class A,B sensor class C,D,E,F,G,M,N mcu class H,I,K actr class J,L load ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["🔌 SENSOR SECTION (5 V DC)"] V1[+5V Arduino] -->|Vcc Pin 1| LM35[LM35 TO-92] G1[GND Arduino] -->|GND Pin 3| LM35 LM35 -->|Vout Pin 2| A0[Arduino A0] end subgraph S2["🎛️ RELAY MODULE (5 V DC + 230 V AC)"] V2[+5V Arduino] -->|VCC| REL[Relay Module] G2[GND Arduino] -->|GND| REL D8[Arduino D8] -->|IN Signal| REL REL -->|COM Terminal| LAMP[230V AC Live] REL -->|NO Terminal| LOAD[230V Device] N[230V AC Neutral] --> LOAD end subgraph S3["🖥️ OUTPUT DISPLAY"] A0 -->|Analog Input| ADC[10-bit ADC
Resolution 0.488 C] ADC -->|Digital Count| SER[Serial Monitor] ADC -->|Converted C| LCD[16x2 LCD Optional] end classDef power fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 classDef sig fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,color:#000 classDef ac fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900,color:#000 class V1,G1,V2,G2,D8,A0 power class LM35,REL,ADC,SER,LCD sig class LAMP,LOAD,N ac ``` ## 4.3 Pin Assignment Table (Reference for Wiring) | Arduino Pin | Connected To | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **+5V** | LM35 Vcc (Pin 1) | Sensor power | | **GND** | LM35 GND (Pin 3) | Sensor ground | | **A0** | LM35 Vout (Pin 2) | Analog temperature input | | **+5V** | Relay VCC | Relay logic power | | **GND** | Relay GND | Common ground | | **D8** | Relay IN | Relay control signal | | **COM** (Relay) | 230 V AC Live | Mains input | | **NO** (Relay) | 230 V Load terminal | Switched live | | **Neutral** | 230 V Load other terminal | Return path | ## 4.4 Timing Diagram — Hysteresis Behavior ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant T as Temperature (C) participant H as Hysteresis Comparator participant R as Relay State Note over T,R: Initial: T = 20, Relay OFF T->>H: 25 (below TL = 28) H->>R: No change (OFF) T->>H: 29 (between TL and TH) H->>R: No change (OFF) T->>H: 33 (above TH = 32) H->>R: ON (latch high) T->>H: 31 (between TL and TH) H->>R: No change (still ON) T->>H: 27 (below TL = 28) H->>R: OFF (latch low) Note over T,R: Output is debounced naturally ``` > [!NOTE] > The **latching memory** in the code is the key to hysteresis. The state only flips when the temperature **fully crosses** the opposite threshold, eliminating chatter. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **List the three terminals of an LM35 temperature sensor and state the function of each. Why is the output of LM35 said to be "directly calibrated in Celsius"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Pin 1 (Vcc/+Vs):** Power supply input, typically +5 V DC (can be 4 V to 30 V as per datasheet). (1 mark) - **Pin 2 (Vout):** Analog output voltage linearly proportional to temperature. Transfer function: $V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV} \times T(°C)$. (1 mark) - **Pin 3 (GND):** Common ground reference. (½ mark) - The output is "directly calibrated in Celsius" because the scale factor is exactly **10 mV/°C** with **zero offset** — at 0 °C, Vout = 0 mV; at 100 °C, Vout = 1000 mV = 1 V. No external calibration, look-up table, or polynomial fitting is required. (½ mark) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Why is a relay used between the Arduino and the 230 V AC load? Why cannot a transistor alone switch the 230 V load directly?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **relay provides galvanic isolation** between the 5 V DC logic side and the 230 V AC mains side — the only coupling is magnetic, through the coil. This protects the Arduino and the user from lethal mains voltage. (1 mark) - The relay contacts are designed and insulated to withstand **230 V AC** and the **inrush current** of typical loads. (½ mark) - A transistor cannot directly switch 230 V AC because: (1) most transistors have a V_CE breakdown of < 100 V — insufficient margin; (2) AC requires bidirectional switching, while a transistor is unidirectional; (3) there is no isolation, so a fault could expose the Arduino to mains. (1 mark) - A **relay + optocoupler module** solves all three issues. (½ mark) --- ## 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**. You must answer **either full Question A OR full Question B**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Circuit & Hysteresis Design** #### **Part (a) [7 marks]** **Design and explain a circuit using Arduino Uno, LM35, and a 5 V relay module to control a 230 V, 60 W incandescent bulb based on a temperature threshold of 35 °C. Draw the complete block diagram and explain the function of each block. Also derive the temperature conversion formula used in the Arduino code.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 above) — mention the **five blocks**: Sensor → ADC → Processing → Comparator → Actuator. (2 marks) 2. **Component selection with justification:** - LM35 chosen for linear, calibrated output. (½ mark) - Arduino Uno chosen for built-in 10-bit ADC and digital I/O. (½ mark) - 5 V relay module chosen for 230 V isolation and 10 A current rating (more than sufficient for 60 W / 230 V = 0.26 A). (½ mark) 3. **Pin connection table** (refer to Section 4.3 above) (1 mark) 4. **Derivation of the temperature formula:** - $V_{in} = \dfrac{\text{ADC} \times 5}{1024}$ - $V_{in} = 10 \times T$ (LM35 transfer function) - $\therefore T = \dfrac{\text{ADC} \times 5}{1024 \times 0.010} = \text{ADC} \times 0.488\,°C$ (2 marks) 5. **Operating logic:** If T > 35 °C, set D8 = HIGH → relay ON → bulb ON. Else D8 = LOW → bulb OFF. (½ mark) #### **Part (b) [7 marks]** **Modify the above design to incorporate a 2 °C hysteresis band. Write the modified Arduino code (with proper comments) and explain how the hysteresis eliminates relay chattering. State the upper and lower threshold values.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Threshold values** (1 mark): - $T_{set} = 35\,°C$, $\Delta T = 2\,°C$ - Upper threshold: $T_H = 35 + 2 = 37\,°C$ (turn ON if T reaches this) - Lower threshold: $T_L = 35 - 2 = 33\,°C$ (turn OFF if T drops to this) 2. **Modified Arduino code with latching memory and hysteresis:** (4 marks) ```c /* Temperature Control with 2 deg C Hysteresis */ const int sensorPin = A0; const int relayPin = 8; const float Tset = 35.0; const float delta = 2.0; const float TH = Tset + delta; // 37 const float TL = Tset - delta; // 33 bool relayState = false; // Latching memory void setup() { pinMode(relayPin, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(relayPin, LOW); // Relay OFF at start Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int adc = analogRead(sensorPin); float temp = adc * 0.488; // LM35 conversion Serial.print("Temp: "); Serial.print(temp); Serial.print(" C | Relay: "); Serial.println(relayState ? "ON" : "OFF"); if (temp >= TH && !relayState) { digitalWrite(relayPin, HIGH); relayState = true; } else if (temp <= TL && relayState) { digitalWrite(relayPin, LOW); relayState = false; } delay(500); } ``` 3. **Chattering elimination explanation** (2 marks): - Without hysteresis, if T hovers at 34.8, 35.1, 34.9, 35.2, the relay switches every reading. - With hysteresis, the state is **latched** in `relayState`. It only flips when T **fully crosses** the opposite threshold. - A reading of 34.9 °C lies in the **dead-band** (33 to 37) — no action taken. - This reduces relay operations from potentially **thousands/hour to a few/hour**, extending life from ~10⁵ to ~10⁶ cycles. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Calibration, Error & Safety Design** #### **Part (a) [7 marks]** **Explain the procedure to experimentally calibrate the LM35-Arduino system using a mercury thermometer and water baths at 0 °C (ice-water slurry), 25 °C (room temp), and 50 °C (warm water). Show the calculation of percentage error and the software correction factor.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Apparatus** (1 mark): Ice-water slurry, room-temperature water, warm water (~50 °C), mercury thermometer (±0.5 °C), LM35, Arduino, laptop. 2. **Procedure** (2 marks): - **Step 1 (0 °C):** Fill a beaker with crushed ice + water, stir, immerse LM35 (in waterproof bag) and thermometer. After thermal equilibrium (3 min), note Arduino reading $T_{ard} = 0.5\,°C$ and mercury reading $T_{ref} = 0.0\,°C$. - **Step 2 (25 °C):** Room temperature, repeat. $T_{ard} = 25.7\,°C$, $T_{ref} = 25.0\,°C$. - **Step 3 (50 °C):** Warm water bath, repeat. $T_{ard} = 51.2\,°C$, $T_{ref} = 50.0\,°C$. 3. **Percentage error calculation** at 25 °C (2 marks): $$\% \text{Error} = \frac{\vert T_{ard} - T_{ref} \vert}{T_{ref}} \times 100 = \frac{\vert 25.7 - 25.0 \vert}{25.0} \times 100 = 2.8\,\%$$ 4. **Correction factor** (2 marks): - Average error = (0.5 + 0.7 + 1.2) / 3 = **0.8 °C offset** (systematic positive error). - Apply in code: `float temp = adc * 0.488 - 0.8;` (subtractive offset) - Or, if error is multiplicative: `float temp = adc * 0.488 * 0.985;` (multiplicative gain correction of ~1.5 %). #### **Part (b) [7 marks]** **List and justify at least four safety precautions you must follow when interfacing a 230 V AC load with the Arduino via a relay. Why is a "flyback diode" mandatory across the relay coil, and what is the role of the optocoupler?** **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Safety precautions** (4 marks — 1 each): - **(i) Use a fully insulated, enclosed relay module** — never expose bare relay contacts to the lab bench. - **(ii) Use separate wiring channels** for 5 V DC and 230 V AC, and clearly mark them. Never run them in the same ribbon cable. - **(iii) Ensure proper earthing (grounding)** of the AC load chassis (especially for metal-bodied devices like fans). - **(iv) Use a fuse (1 A or 2 A fast-blow)** in series with the 230 V live wire to protect against short circuits. - **(v) Work under faculty supervision**, never alone; the lab instructor must verify the wiring before power-on. - **(vi) Use double-insulated tools** and stand on a rubber mat. 2. **Flyback diode** (1.5 marks): - The relay coil is an **inductor** ($L$). When the transistor switches OFF, the magnetic field collapses, and by Faraday's law $V = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$, a huge **back-EMF spike** (often > 100 V) is generated. - This spike can **destroy the driver transistor** and inject noise into the Arduino (causing resets). - A **1N4007 flyback diode** placed in **reverse-bias** across the coil short-circuits this spike safely, dissipating the energy as heat. 3. **Optocoupler role** (1.5 marks): - The optocoupler is an **opto-isolator** (LED + phototransistor in a single IC). - The Arduino's 5 V signal drives the LED, which emits IR light. The phototransistor on the other side conducts when it sees the light. - This couples the **signal optically** — there is **no electrical connection** between the Arduino and the relay's coil side. - **Benefit:** If a 230 V fault ever reaches the relay's coil side, it **cannot propagate** back to the Arduino (galvanic isolation). --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > > | Mistake | Marks Lost | Examiner's Note | > |---|---|---| > | Forgetting the `0.488` factor or using wrong ADC reference (3.3 V on Uno) | 2 | Always state $V_{ref}$ explicitly. | > | Not explaining why hysteresis is needed | 1–2 | Hysteresis is **not optional** — it is a viva-favourite. | > | Wiring 230 V into the relay's DC+ and DC- pins | 3 (practical) | Common safety violation — instant disallowance. | > | Not using a flyback diode | 1 | Even if the module has one, **mention it** in the theory answer. | > | Using `delay(5000)` in the loop without explanation | 1 | Explain that delay controls sampling rate; for PID, use `millis()`. | > | Saying "sensor sends digital data" | 2 | LM35 is **analog** — this is a basic fact check. | > | Not initializing `relayState = false` in a global scope | 1 | Causes unpredictable behavior on first run. | > | Forgetting the `float` cast — using integer division | 2 | `307 * 5 / 1024 = 1` (int) instead of `1.5` (float). | --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before entering the exam hall)** - ✅ **LM35** is **analog**, 3-pin, outputs **10 mV/°C**, **0 V at 0 °C**, **1 V at 100 °C**, range **0–150 °C**. - ✅ **Arduino ADC** is **10-bit**, $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, resolution $= V_{ref} / 1024 = 4.88$ mV per step. - ✅ **Master Formula:** $T(°C) = \text{analogRead(A0)} \times 0.488$. - ✅ **Smallest detectable temperature change** (quantization) $= 0.488$ °C. - ✅ **Relay** provides **galvanic isolation** between 5 V DC and 230 V AC; contacts rated 10 A @ 250 V AC. - ✅ **Flyback diode (1N4007)** is **mandatory** across the relay coil to suppress back-EMF. - ✅ **Optocoupler** provides **opto-isolation** between logic and coil — protects Arduino from mains faults. - ✅ **Hysteresis** is implemented with **two thresholds**: $T_H = T_{set} + \Delta T$ and $T_L = T_{set} - \Delta T$, with a **latching state variable** to prevent relay chattering. - ✅ **Never** connect 230 V to the relay's 5 V or GND pins — use the **COM, NO, NC** screw terminals only. - ✅ **Calibration** can be done with a 3-point water bath (0 °C, 25 °C, 50 °C) and a mercury reference thermometer. - ✅ **Safety** = insulated wiring + earthing + fuse + supervision + flyback diode + optocoupler. - ✅ The whole system follows the **Sense → Process → Actuate (SPA)** paradigm common to all IoT/embedded control systems. - ✅ The **230 V load** in lab is typically a **bulb** (resistive, easy); real-world loads include **fans, geysers, ACs, motors** (inductive — need snubber/RCD protection). - ✅ Always **add `delay(500–1000 ms)`** in the loop to give ADC time to settle and prevent relay stress. - ✅ If using a **TMP36** instead of LM35, the formula changes to $T = \dfrac{\text{ADC} \times 5}{1024 \times 10} - 50$ because of the 500 mV 0 °C offset. > **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2 | KTU 2024 Scheme | PCCIL607 Sensors and Devices Lab**

topics.title: Create an application that has three LEDs (Red, Green and white). The LEDs should follow the cycle (All Off, Red On, Green On, White On) for each clap (use sound sensor)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Create an application that has three LEDs (Red, Green and white). The LEDs should follow the cycle (All Off, Red On, Green On, White On) for each clap (use sound sensor)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sound-Activated LED Sequencer** is an embedded systems application that uses an acoustic transducer (microphone/sound sensor) to detect transient acoustic events (claps) and translates each detected event into a deterministic state transition across a bank of three light-emitting diodes. The system follows a **finite state machine (FSM)** pattern with four discrete states cycled in response to a single clap input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This experiment directly addresses **CO1 (Sensors and Transducers Identification)**, **CO2 (Signal Conditioning and Interfacing)**, and **CO3 (Embedded System Programming)** of the PCCIL607 – Sensors and Devices Lab syllabus.

In KTU lab-record terminology, this is classified as a **"Sensor-actuator feedback loop"** where the sound sensor acts as the **input transducer** and the LED array acts as the **output actuator**, mediated by an Arduino Uno microcontroller (Atmega328P) as the processing unit.

## 1.2 Components Used

| Component | Quantity | Purpose |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) | 1 | Central processing & control unit |
| Sound Sensor Module (KY-038 / KY-037 / HW-484) | 1 | Acoustic event (clap) detection |
| Red LED (5mm, Vf ≈ 2V) | 1 | State indicator 1 |
| Green LED (5mm, Vf ≈ 2.2V) | 1 | State indicator 2 |
| White LED (5mm, Vf ≈ 3.2V) | 1 | State indicator 3 |
| Resistor 220Ω (¼W, ±5%) | 3 | Current limiting for LEDs |
| Breadboard (830 tie-points) | 1 | Prototyping platform |
| Jumper Wires (M-M, M-F) | As required | Interconnections |
| USB Cable (Type A-B) | 1 | Power + Programming |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a dark room with three lamps — a red one, a green one, and a white one. Now imagine that you have a magical bell: every time you clap your hands, the bell rings, and the lamps change their arrangement in a fixed pattern:

- 1st clap → All lamps are **OFF** (quiet / standby)
- 2nd clap → Only the **RED** lamp turns ON
- 3rd clap → Only the **GREEN** lamp turns ON
- 4th clap → Only the **WHITE** lamp turns ON
- 5th clap → Again all lamps are OFF, and the cycle repeats.

The **sound sensor** is your "magical bell" — it converts sound waves into electrical signals. The **Arduino** acts as the "smart listener" that decides which lamp to turn on, and the **LEDs** are the output devices that visually show the current state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This same principle powers **smart-home clap switches**, **assistive devices for differently-abled users**, **noise-triggered industrial alarms**, and **interactive museum exhibits**. The state-machine pattern is foundational to nearly every digital control system (washing machines, traffic lights, elevators).

## 1.4 Key Technical Terminology

- **Clap Detection:** Recognition of a sharp, transient acoustic spike (typically 60–100 dB, lasting 50–300 ms) generated by striking two surfaces together.
- **Threshold Value:** A pre-set digital or analog reference level above which the input is classified as a "clap" event. For KY-038, this is set by the on-board potentiometer.
- **Debounce Delay:** A time guard (e.g., 500 ms) inserted after detecting a clap to prevent one physical clap from being counted as multiple events due to acoustic reverberation.
- **Finite State Machine (FSM):** A computational model with a finite number of states, transition functions, and inputs. Here, 4 states are cycled by 1 trigger.
- **Active-HIGH Logic:** LED turns ON when its GPIO pin is driven to logic HIGH (5V).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of the Sound Sensor (KY-038)

The KY-038 sound sensor module consists of three sub-blocks:

1. **Electret Condenser Microphone (ECM):** Converts acoustic pressure variations into a small AC voltage signal (typically a few mV).
2. **LM393 Comparator IC:** Amplifies the microphone signal and compares it against a threshold set by the on-board potentiometer.
3. **Output Stage:** Provides both an **Analog Output (A0)** — the raw amplified waveform — and a **Digital Output (D0)** — a clean HIGH/LOW logic signal.

> [!NOTE]
> The **D0 (digital) pin** is preferred for clap detection because it already provides a debounced, threshold-compared logic level, simplifying the Arduino code.

The on-board **blue potentiometer** is used to set the sensitivity:

- Turn **clockwise** → sensitivity increases (detects softer sounds).
- Turn **counter-clockwise** → sensitivity decreases (only detects loud sounds like claps).

## 2.2 LED Current-Limiting Resistor Calculation

Without a current-limiting resistor, an LED will draw excessive current and burn out within seconds. The standard formula is:

$$
R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_{LED}}
$$

Where:
- $V_{supply}$ = Arduino digital pin HIGH voltage ≈ **5.0 V**
- $V_f$ = LED forward voltage drop
- $I_{LED}$ = desired LED current (typical **15–20 mA**)

### Calculation per LED Color

$$
R_{Red} = \frac{5.0 - 2.0}{0.020} = \frac{3.0}{0.020} = 150\ \Omega
$$

$$
R_{Green} = \frac{5.0 - 2.2}{0.020} = \frac{2.8}{0.020} = 140\ \Omega
$$

$$
R_{White} = \frac{5.0 - 3.2}{0.020} = \frac{1.8}{0.020} = 90\ \Omega
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical KTU Note:** A standard **220Ω resistor** is used for ALL three LEDs. While this gives slightly lower brightness (~13 mA), it is a safe, universally available value accepted in the lab. 220Ω is the most common value cited in KTU lab records.

## 2.3 State Machine Logic

The system operates as a **4-state synchronous counter** triggered by an asynchronous external event (clap). The state transition table is:

| Current State | Trigger (Clap) | Next State | LED Pattern |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| S0 | ✓ | S1 | All OFF |
| S1 | ✓ | S2 | Red ON, others OFF |
| S2 | ✓ | S3 | Green ON, others OFF |
| S3 | ✓ | S0 | White ON, others OFF |

Mathematically, the next state is governed by:

$$
S_{n+1} = (S_n + 1) \mod 4
$$

Where $S_n \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ and the modulus operation ensures the cycle wraps back to S0 after S3.

## 2.4 Debounce & Noise Filtering

A single physical clap generates **multiple acoustic reflections** that may last 100–500 ms. Without filtering, the sensor could register 2–4 events per clap, corrupting the state sequence.

The standard solution is to insert a **software debounce delay**:

$$
t_{debounce} \geq t_{clap\_reverb} + t_{sensor\_recovery}
$$

A typical safe value is **500 ms**, which is longer than the longest possible clap reverberation but shorter than a human's natural clap interval (≈1 s).

> [!TIP]
> A better technique is **edge-detection**: store the previous sensor reading and only count a clap when the reading transitions from LOW → HIGH. This is the professional approach expected in KTU viva questions.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Typical Value | Unit |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $V_{cc}$ | Arduino supply voltage | **5.0** | V |
| $V_f$ | LED forward voltage | 2.0 (Red), 2.2 (Green), 3.2 (White) | V |
| $I_{LED}$ | Safe LED current | **15–20** | mA |
| $R$ | Current-limiting resistor | $\dfrac{V_{cc}-V_f}{I_{LED}}$ | Ω |
| $R_{std}$ | Lab-standard resistor | **220** | Ω |
| $V_{IL}$ | Digital input LOW max | 0.3 × $V_{cc}$ = **1.5** | V |
| $V_{IH}$ | Digital input HIGH min | 0.6 × $V_{cc}$ = **3.0** | V |
| $ADC$ | Arduino resolution | **10-bit (0–1023)** | levels |
| $V_{ref}$ | ADC reference voltage | **5.0** (default) | V |
| $f_{clk}$ | Arduino clock | **16** | MHz |
| $t_{debounce}$ | Software debounce delay | **500** | ms |
| $N_{states}$ | Number of FSM states | **4** | — |

## 2.6 Why This Experiment Matters in Engineering

- **IoT Foundation:** Demonstrates the input → process → output paradigm central to every IoT node.
- **Signal Conditioning:** Shows how a noisy analog sensor is converted into a clean digital trigger.
- **State Machine Design:** A core concept in **digital electronics, embedded firmware, and PLC programming**.
- **Human-Machine Interaction (HMI):** Acoustic input is a hands-free control modality used in accessibility tech.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation, Code Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Hardware Connection Table

| Component Pin | Arduino Pin | Wire Color (Suggested) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Sound Sensor VCC | **5V** | Red |
| Sound Sensor GND | **GND** | Black |
| Sound Sensor D0 | **Digital Pin 2** | Yellow |
| Red LED Anode (+) | **Digital Pin 8** (via 220Ω) | Red wire |
| Green LED Anode (+) | **Digital Pin 9** (via 220Ω) | Green wire |
| White LED Anode (+) | **Digital Pin 10** (via 220Ω) | White wire |
| All LED Cathodes (−) | **GND rail** | Black |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of the Algorithm

### Step 1 — Pin Configuration
We must declare pin modes at the start. The sound sensor's D0 pin is configured as **INPUT**, while the LED pins are **OUTPUT**.

### Step 2 — State Variable Declaration
A global integer variable `state` is declared and initialized to **0** (representing the "All OFF" state).

### Step 3 — Loop Iteration
Inside the infinite `loop()`:
1. Read the digital value of the sound sensor.
2. If the value is HIGH, a clap is detected.
3. Use a static `lastClapTime` variable to enforce debounce.
4. If the time since the last detected clap is greater than 500 ms, advance the state.
5. Update the LED pattern based on the new state.

### Step 4 — State-to-LED Mapping
Use a `switch-case` (or `if-else`) block to translate the state variable into the correct combination of HIGH/LOW signals on the three LED pins.

## 3.3 Complete Arduino Sketch (Industry-Standard with Edge Detection + Debounce)

```cpp
/*--------------------------------------------------------------
   KTU PCCIL607 - Sensors and Devices Lab
   Experiment: Sound-Activated 3-LED Sequencer (Clap Switch)
   Platform : Arduino Uno (ATmega328P, 16 MHz)
   Author   : Student Lab Record
--------------------------------------------------------------*/

// -------- 1. Pin Definitions --------
const int SOUND_PIN = 2;      // Digital D0 output of KY-038
const int RED_LED   = 8;      // Red LED through 220Ω
const int GREEN_LED = 9;      // Green LED through 220Ω
const int WHITE_LED = 10;     // White LED through 220Ω

// -------- 2. State Machine Variables --------
int  state        = 0;        // 0=AllOff, 1=Red, 2=Green, 3=White
unsigned long lastClapTime    = 0;
const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 500;   // 500 ms guard
int  previousSoundReading     = LOW;     // for edge detection

// -------- 3. Setup --------
void setup() {
  pinMode(SOUND_PIN, INPUT);
  pinMode(RED_LED,   OUTPUT);
  pinMode(GREEN_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(WHITE_LED, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);                  // for debugging
  allOff();                            // start in OFF state
}

// -------- 4. Main Loop --------
void loop() {
  int currentSound = digitalRead(SOUND_PIN);

  // Edge detection: transition LOW -> HIGH
  if (currentSound == HIGH && previousSoundReading == LOW) {
    if (millis() - lastClapTime > DEBOUNCE_MS) {
      lastClapTime = millis();
      state = (state + 1) % 4;        // cycle through 4 states
      applyState();
      Serial.print("Clap detected → State = ");
      Serial.println(state);
    }
  }
  previousSoundReading = currentSound;
}

// -------- 5. State Application --------
void applyState() {
  switch (state) {
    case 0: allOff();   break;
    case 1: redOn();    break;
    case 2: greenOn();  break;
    case 3: whiteOn();  break;
  }
}

// -------- 6. LED Helper Functions --------
void allOff() {
  digitalWrite(RED_LED,   LOW);
  digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, LOW);
  digitalWrite(WHITE_LED, LOW);
}
void redOn() {
  digitalWrite(RED_LED,   HIGH);
  digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, LOW);
  digitalWrite(WHITE_LED, LOW);
}
void greenOn() {
  digitalWrite(RED_LED,   LOW);
  digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(WHITE_LED, LOW);
}
void whiteOn() {
  digitalWrite(RED_LED,   LOW);
  digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, LOW);
  digitalWrite(WHITE_LED, HIGH);
}
```

## 3.4 Line-by-Line Exhaustive Walkthrough

| Line / Function | Purpose | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| `const int SOUND_PIN = 2` | Pin 2 is chosen because it is one of Arduino's **interrupt-capable pins**, future-proofing the code for ISR-based detection. | Allows migration to hardware-interrupt-driven design in advanced lab work. |
| `int state = 0` | State variable starts at S0 (All Off). | Matches the requirement: "first cycle begins from All Off". |
| `const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 500` | 500 ms filter. | Long enough to absorb acoustic reverberation, short enough to allow human-paced clapping. |
| `previousSoundReading = LOW` | Tracks the previous sensor state for **edge detection**. | Prevents a continuous HIGH signal from being counted as infinite claps. |
| `state = (state + 1) % 4` | Modulus 4 implements wrap-around. | The `S3 → S0` transition is automatic; no explicit `if (state > 3)` check needed. |
| `switch(state)` | Clean, readable state-action mapping. | Industry standard for FSM implementation in C/C++. |
| `Serial.print/println` | Sends state info to PC monitor at 9600 baud. | Essential for **lab debugging and viva demonstration**. |

## 3.5 Verification & Expected Output (Trace Table)

Trace the execution assuming 4 claps at $t = 0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3$ seconds:

| Time (s) | Event | `state` (old) | `state` (new) | Red | Green | White |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0.0 | Power on | — | 0 | OFF | OFF | OFF |
| 1.0 | Clap 1 | 0 | 1 | **ON** | OFF | OFF |
| 2.0 | Clap 2 | 1 | 2 | OFF | **ON** | OFF |
| 3.0 | Clap 3 | 2 | 3 | OFF | OFF | **ON** |
| 4.0 | Clap 4 | 3 | 0 | OFF | OFF | OFF |
| 5.0 | Clap 5 | 0 | 1 | **ON** | OFF | OFF |

This trace exactly matches the **KTU problem statement specification**.

## 3.6 Mathematical Proof of the Cyclic Property

Let $S_0 = 0$. The recurrence relation is:

$$
S_n = (S_{n-1} + 1) \mod 4
$$

We can solve this closed-form:

$$
S_n = n \mod 4
$$

Therefore the sequence of states generated is $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 0, 1, 2, 3, \dots\}$, confirming the cycle. The period of the sequence is:

$$
T = 4\ \text{claps} = 4 \times t_{clap\_interval}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ACoustic Source Clap] --> B[KY038 Microphone Module]
    B --> C[LM393 Comparator]
    C --> D[Threshold via Potentiometer]
    D --> E[Digital Output D0]
    E --> F[Arduino UNO ATmega328P]
    F --> G{State Machine Mod 4 Counter}
    G --> H[GPIO 8 Red LED]
    G --> I[GPIO 9 Green LED]
    G --> J[GPIO 10 White LED]
    F --> K[USB Serial Monitor 9600 baud]
    H --> L[Visual Feedback]
    I --> L
    J --> L
```

## 4.2 Detailed State Transition Flowchart

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0
    S0 --> S1 : Clap Detected
    S1 --> S2 : Clap Detected
    S2 --> S3 : Clap Detected
    S3 --> S0 : Clap Detected
    S0 : All Off
    S1 : Red On
    S2 : Green On
    S3 : White On
```

## 4.3 Hardware Wiring Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWER[Power Distribution]
        V5[Arduino 5V Rail]
        GND[Arduino GND Rail]
    end

    subgraph SENSOR[Sound Sensor Module KY038]
        MIC[Microphone]
        CMP[LM393 Comparator]
        POT[Blue Potentiometer]
        DOPIN[Digital D0 Pin]
    end

    subgraph MCU[Arduino Uno]
        P2[Digital Pin 2 INPUT]
        P8[Digital Pin 8 OUTPUT]
        P9[Digital Pin 9 OUTPUT]
        P10[Digital Pin 10 OUTPUT]
    end

    subgraph LEDS[LED Indicator Bank]
        R[Red LED with 220R]
        G[Green LED with 220R]
        W[White LED with 220R]
    end

    V5 --> MIC
    V5 --> DOPIN
    GND --> MIC
    GND --> R
    GND --> G
    GND --> W

    MIC --> CMP
    POT --> CMP
    CMP --> DOPIN
    DOPIN --> P2

    P8 --> R
    P9 --> G
    P10 --> W
```

## 4.4 Clap Detection & Debounce Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant S as Sound Sensor
    participant A as Arduino
    participant L as LED Array

    U->>S: Produces Clap
    S->>S: Microphone converts to AC
    S->>S: LM393 compares with threshold
    S->>A: D0 goes HIGH
    A->>A: Edge detected LOW to HIGH
    A->>A: Check millis minus lastClapTime
    alt Debounce OK greater than 500 ms
        A->>A: Increment state mod 4
        A->>L: Drive GPIO pattern
        L-->>U: Visual feedback
    else Within 500 ms window
        A->>A: Ignore as noise
    end
```

## 4.5 Power & Signal Flow Summary Table

| Signal Path | Direction | Voltage Level | Frequency / Speed |
|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Clap → Microphone | Acoustic → Electric | < 10 mV (AC) | Audio band 20 Hz – 20 kHz |
| Mic → LM393 | Analog | 0 – 5 V (rectified) | DC threshold compare |
| LM393 → D0 pin | Digital | **0 V or 5 V** | Step (logic level) |
| D0 → Arduino Pin 2 | Digital | **0 V or 5 V** | Sampled every ≈ 4 µs |
| Arduino → LEDs | Digital output | **0 V or 5 V** | Updated per loop iteration |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**What is a sound sensor? List any two applications of sound sensors in real-world systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A sound sensor is an electronic module that detects sound waves and converts them into electrical signals, typically using an electret condenser microphone followed by a comparator circuit (e.g., LM393). The module provides both analog and digital outputs, with the digital output going HIGH when the sound intensity exceeds a user-settable threshold.

**Applications (any two, 1½ marks each):**
1. **Clap-activated switches** for smart-home lighting.
2. **Baby cry detectors** in nursery monitoring systems.
3. **Noise pollution monitoring** in industrial environments.
4. **Voice-activated security alarms**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**Why is a current-limiting resistor necessary when interfacing an LED with an Arduino digital pin?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An LED is a non-ohmic device whose resistance decreases sharply once its forward voltage ($V_f$) is exceeded. If connected directly across a 5V Arduino pin, the LED would draw excessive current (theoretically hundreds of mA), causing it to overheat and burn out within seconds, and potentially damaging the Arduino pin.

A current-limiting resistor (e.g., **220 Ω**) is placed in series to restrict the current to a safe value (typically **15–20 mA**) using the formula:

$$
R = \frac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_{LED}} = \frac{5 - 2}{0.020} = 150\ \Omega
$$

A standard 220 Ω resistor is used in practice as it provides a safe, slightly dimmer operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram to interface a sound sensor module and three LEDs (Red, Green, White) with an Arduino Uno. Explain the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete Arduino program to cycle the LEDs through the states (All Off → Red → Green → White) for each detected clap, using a 500 ms debounce delay. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Diagram (textual schematic, 3 marks):**

| Connection | From | To |
|---|---|---|
| Sound Sensor VCC | Sensor VCC | Arduino 5V |
| Sound Sensor GND | Sensor GND | Arduino GND |
| Sound Sensor D0 | Sensor D0 | Arduino Digital Pin 2 |
| Red LED Anode | Resistor 220Ω → Pin 8 | — |
| Red LED Cathode | GND rail | — |
| Green LED Anode | Resistor 220Ω → Pin 9 | — |
| Green LED Cathode | GND rail | — |
| White LED Anode | Resistor 220Ω → Pin 10 | — |
| White LED Cathode | GND rail | — |

**Component Functions (4 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Arduino Uno** | Central processing unit; reads the sensor signal and drives the LEDs based on program logic. |
| **Sound Sensor (KY-038)** | Detects the acoustic clap and produces a HIGH pulse on the D0 pin. |
| **Electret Microphone** | Transduces sound pressure into an analog electrical signal. |
| **LM393 Comparator** | Compares the amplified mic signal with a threshold; outputs clean digital logic. |
| **Potentiometer** | Adjusts the clap-detection threshold sensitivity. |
| **220 Ω Resistor (×3)** | Limits LED current to ≈ 13 mA to prevent burnout. |
| **LEDs (R, G, W)** | Provide visual indication of the current FSM state. |
| **Breadboard + Jumpers** | Mechanical platform for prototyping the circuit. |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Configure Pin 2 as INPUT (sound sensor), Pins 8/9/10 as OUTPUT (LEDs).
2. Initialize a `state` variable to 0.
3. In `loop()`, read Pin 2.
4. If a LOW→HIGH transition is detected and ≥ 500 ms have passed since the last clap, increment `state` modulo 4.
5. Use a `switch-case` to drive the LEDs according to the new `state`.

**Code (5 marks):** (Full code as in **Section 3.3** above — students are expected to reproduce it completely with `setup()`, `loop()`, `applyState()`, and helper functions `allOff()`, `redOn()`, `greenOn()`, `whiteOn()`.)

**Key Explanation Points expected:**
- Use of `millis()` for non-blocking debounce.
- Modulo operator for cyclic state transition.
- Edge detection with `previousSoundReading` to avoid multiple counts.
- `switch-case` for state-to-LED mapping.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of the KY-038 sound sensor module. How does the on-board potentiometer affect its sensitivity? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Modify the program in Question A to use the **analog output (A0)** of the sound sensor instead of the digital output, and set the clap threshold to a value of 400 (out of 1023). Explain the advantage of using analog thresholding. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (4 marks):**

The KY-038 module has three sub-blocks:
1. **Electret Condenser Microphone** — converts acoustic energy to electrical.
2. **LM393 Dual Comparator IC** — amplifies the mic signal and produces both an analog waveform on the A0 pin and a digital threshold output on the D0 pin.
3. **On-board Potentiometer (10 kΩ)** — feeds a reference voltage to one input of the LM393 comparator.

**Working (2 marks):**
When the mic's amplified voltage exceeds the reference voltage set by the potentiometer, the comparator output D0 transitions from LOW to HIGH, indicating a clap is detected.

**Potentiometer Effect (1 mark):**
- **Clockwise rotation** → higher sensitivity → reference voltage lowered → even soft sounds trigger D0.
- **Counter-clockwise rotation** → lower sensitivity → reference voltage raised → only loud claps trigger D0.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Modified Code (5 marks):**

```cpp
const int SOUND_AO = A0;       // Analog pin
const int THRESHOLD = 400;     // Mid-scale threshold
int state = 0;
unsigned long lastClapTime = 0;
const unsigned long DEBOUNCE = 500;

void setup() {
  pinMode(RED_LED,   OUTPUT);
  pinMode(GREEN_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(WHITE_LED, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
  int soundValue = analogRead(SOUND_AO);
  if (soundValue > THRESHOLD) {
    if (millis() - lastClapTime > DEBOUNCE) {
      lastClapTime = millis();
      state = (state + 1) % 4;
      applyState();
      Serial.print("Clap! soundValue = ");
      Serial.println(soundValue);
    }
  }
}
```

**Advantage of Analog Thresholding (2 marks):**
1. **Tunable in software** — the threshold can be dynamically adjusted without physically turning the potentiometer.
2. **Finer resolution** — 1024 discrete levels allow precise sensitivity calibration.
3. **Environmental adaptation** — the Arduino can auto-adjust the threshold based on ambient noise (e.g., using a running average), enabling robust operation in noisy KTU labs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Lab Exams:**
>
> 1. **Missing current-limiting resistor** for LEDs — 1-mark penalty (and the LED will physically burn out in the lab).
> 2. **Wrong pin numbers** in the code vs. the circuit diagram — examiners cross-check; inconsistency = 0 for the code section.
> 3. **No debounce logic** — the LEDs will skip states erratically, and the examiner will deduct 2 marks for "unreliable operation".
> 4. **Using `delay()` instead of `millis()`** for debounce — this *blocks* the loop; examiners mark it down because it shows poor real-time programming practice.
> 5. **LED anode/cathode reversed** — physical observation will mark 0 for the working output.
> 6. **Forgetting to call `applyState()` after incrementing `state`** — a very common coding oversight. The state changes but LEDs never update.
> 7. **Viva question trap:** *"Why is `int state` a global variable?"* — because it must retain its value across `loop()` iterations. Local variables inside `loop()` would reset every cycle, breaking the state machine.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!TIP]
