> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before your lab viva):**
>
> - ☐ The experiment is a **4-state FSM**: S0 (All Off) → S1 (Red) → S2 (Green) → S3 (White) → S0.
> - ☐ Trigger = **one clap** detected by the **KY-038 sound sensor's D0 pin** (or analog A0 with threshold).
> - ☐ Each LED requires a **220 Ω series resistor** for current limiting.
> - ☐ Use **edge detection (LOW→HIGH) + 500 ms debounce** to prevent multiple counts per clap.
> - ☐ Use **`state = (state + 1) % 4`** to achieve automatic cyclic wrap-around.
> - ☐ Use **`millis()` for non-blocking timing**; avoid `delay()` in real-time applications.
> - ☐ The **D0** output of KY-038 is pre-thresholded; the **A0** output requires software thresholding.
> - ☐ The **blue potentiometer** on the sensor sets the digital trigger sensitivity.
> - ☐ The Arduino's **ADC is 10-bit** (0–1023), referenced to **5.0 V** by default.
> - ☐ **State machine** is the underlying design pattern — a core KTU viva topic across multiple subjects.
> - ☐ Industrial relevance: **clap switches, noise alarms, accessibility devices, IoT HMI**.

**One-line takeaway for the record:**
> *The clap-controlled LED sequencer is a textbook example of an **interrupt-driven (or polled) sensor-actuator FSM** that fuses analog acoustic sensing, digital signal conditioning, and deterministic state programming on the Arduino platform — a foundational pattern for all modern IoT devices.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Create a web application for the above applications wherever possible with suitable modifications to get input and to send output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIL607 - SENSORS AND DEVICES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Create a web application for the above applications wherever possible with suitable modifications to get input and to send output ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Web Application for Sensor/Device Interfacing** is a software system, deployed on a local server or cloud platform, that uses standard web technologies (**HTML**, **CSS**, **JavaScript** for the front-end, and a server-side framework such as **Flask**, **Django**, or **Node.js** for the back-end) to establish a **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** for acquiring data from sensors and transmitting control signals to actuators. The web app communicates with physical hardware (e.g., Arduino, Raspberry Pi, ESP32) through protocols such as **HTTP/HTTPS (REST APIs)**, **WebSockets**, **MQTT**, or **Serial (UART)** communication, enabling remote monitoring and control of physical systems via any browser-enabled device. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab module emphasizes that wherever a sensor/actuator application is feasible, it should be exposed through a browser-based dashboard, making the experiment reproducible, demonstrable, and accessible without dedicated client software. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **universal remote control (the web app)** sitting in your browser. - The **sensor** is the *eye* of the system — it sees temperature, light, motion, or gas. - The **microcontroller** (e.g., Arduino) is the *brain and nerve* that reads the eye and decides what to do. - The **web server** is the *translator* who turns the brain's signals into a webpage your phone can display. - The **actuator** (fan, LED, motor) is the *muscle* that actually performs the action. > [!IMPORTANT] > Without the web layer, you must stand in front of the hardware and stare at an LCD. With the web layer, you can sit in Kerala and switch on a pump in your college lab in Chennai using your phone's browser. That is the **power of Web-of-Things (WoT)**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terms (Bolded Constants) - **HTTP Port:** **80** (HTTP) / **443** (HTTPS) - **Serial Baud Rate:** typically **9600 bps** for Arduino–PC communication - **WebSocket Port:** **8000** or **8080** (common defaults) - **MQTT Broker Port:** **1883** (unsecured) / **8883** (TLS) - **Update Interval:** typically **1–5 seconds** for real-time sensor dashboards - **JSON Payload Size:** typically **< 1 KB** per sensor reading --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Three-Tier Web Architecture for Sensor Apps A sensor web app is fundamentally built on a **three-tier architecture**: **Tier 1 — Presentation Layer (Front-End)** - HTML5 for page structure - CSS3 / Bootstrap for responsive layout - JavaScript (or frameworks like React/Vue) for dynamic content and AJAX polling - **Chart.js** or **Plotly** for plotting live sensor graphs **Tier 2 — Application/Logic Layer (Back-End)** - Python Flask / Django, or Node.js Express - Handles HTTP routes (`/read`, `/control`, `/dashboard`) - Performs data processing, threshold checks, alerts - Bridges HTTP requests to hardware via Serial (PySerial), GPIO libraries, or MQTT brokers **Tier 3 — Hardware Layer (Physical)** - Microcontroller (Arduino Uno/Mega, ESP8266, ESP32, Raspberry Pi) - Sensors (DHT11, LM35, LDR, PIR, MQ-2, Ultrasonic HC-SR04) - Actuators (Relay, DC motor, Servo, LED array, Buzzer) ### 2.2 Communication Flow (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **User opens browser** → Sends `GET /` request to web server. 2. **Server responds** → Sends back the HTML page with embedded JS. 3. **JavaScript triggers AJAX GET** on `/sensor` endpoint every N seconds. 4. **Backend reads sensor** (either from serial port, GPIO, or cache). 5. **Backend packages data** as JSON: `{"temperature": 28.5, "humidity": 65}`. 6. **JavaScript receives JSON** → Updates DOM elements (`document.getElementById("temp").innerText`). 7. **User clicks "Turn ON Fan"** → JavaScript sends `POST /control` with payload `{"device":"fan","state":"ON"}`. 8. **Backend writes to serial/relay** → Hardware actuator engages. 9. **Acknowledgment returned** → UI updates button state. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Component | Formula / Standard | Unit / Default Value | |---|---|---| | HTTP Request Size (approx) | $\text{Size} = \text{Headers} + \text{Payload}$ | Bytes | | Sampling Rate | $f_s = \frac{1}{T_s}$ | Hz | | Data Throughput | $R = N_{\text{samples}} \times f_s \times B_{\text{resolution}}$ | bits/s | | Baud Rate (Serial) | $\text{Bits/s} = \text{Baud} \times N_{\text{data bits}}$ | bps | | Polling Interval | $T_{\text{poll}} \geq \frac{L_{\text{frame}}}{B_{\text{baud}}}$ | seconds | | JSON Example | `{"sensor":"DHT11","temp":28.5,"unit":"C"}` | — | | REST Verbs | GET (read), POST (write), PUT (update), DELETE (remove) | — | | Arduino Analog Read | $V_{\text{in}} = \frac{A_{\text{ADC}} \times V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}-1}$ | Volts | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Rule:** Never use the pipe symbol `|` inside table cells — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX. (This is the exact rule you must follow in your own answer sheets when typesetting tables.) ### 2.4 Why This Architecture Matters in Engineering - **Industry 4.0 / IIoT:** Every factory floor now uses web dashboards to monitor thousands of sensors. - **Smart Agriculture:** Farmers monitor soil moisture and control irrigation from a phone. - **Healthcare:** Patient vitals streamed to a doctor's browser in real time. - **Home Automation:** Alexa, Google Home — all are web apps speaking to microcontrollers behind the scenes. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthrough ### 3.1 Derivation: Converting Arduino Analog Reading to Physical Voltage The Arduino Uno uses a **10-bit ADC** ($n = 10$) with a default reference voltage of $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\,\text{V}$. The relationship between the digital output and the analog input voltage is derived from the resolution of the ADC. The ADC divides the reference voltage into $2^n$ discrete steps: $$ \Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n} - 1} $$ For Arduino Uno: $$ \Delta V = \frac{5.0}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1023} \approx 4.888 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V/step} $$ For a digital reading $A_{\text{ADC}}$, the actual input voltage is: $$ V_{\text{in}} = A_{\text{ADC}} \times \Delta V = A_{\text{ADC}} \times \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n} - 1} $$ **Numerical Example:** If $A_{\text{ADC}} = 512$ (half scale), then: $$ V_{\text{in}} = 512 \times \frac{5.0}{1023} = 2.502\,\text{V} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: LM35 Temperature Sensor Conversion The **LM35** outputs **10 mV per °C**. Given the ADC-derived voltage $V_{\text{in}}$: $$ T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \frac{V_{\text{in}} \times 1000}{10} = V_{\text{in}} \times 100 $$ Substituting the ADC expression: $$ T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = A_{\text{ADC}} \times \frac{V_{\text{ref}} \times 100}{2^{n} - 1} = A_{\text{ADC}} \times \frac{5.0 \times 100}{1023} = A_{\text{ADC}} \times 0.4888 $$ **Numerical Example:** If $A_{\text{ADC}} = 100$, then $T = 100 \times 0.4888 = 48.88\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. ### 3.3 Derivation: Web Server Response Time Model For a sensor dashboard polling every $T_s$ seconds, with $N$ clients connected, the server's effective load $L$ is: $$ L = \frac{N}{T_s} \times C_{\text{request}} $$ where $C_{\text{request}}$ is the computational cost of a single sensor read cycle (in CPU-seconds). For a Flask app on a Raspberry Pi, typical $C_{\text{request}} \approx 0.005\,\text{s}$. If $N = 10$ clients and $T_s = 1\,\text{s}$: $$ L = \frac{10}{1} \times 0.005 = 0.05 \quad \text{(i.e., 5% CPU)} $$ If $T_s = 0.1\,\text{s}$ (10 Hz polling), then $L = 0.5$ (50% CPU) — saturating the Pi. This explains why **WebSockets** are preferred over AJAX polling for high-frequency apps. ### 3.4 Full End-to-End Example: Temperature Monitor Web App **Step 1 — Arduino Code (Reads LM35 on A0, prints to Serial)** ```cpp void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int adc = analogRead(A0); float voltage = adc * (5.0 / 1023.0); float tempC = voltage * 100.0; Serial.print("T:"); Serial.println(tempC); delay(1000); } ``` **Step 2 — Python Flask Backend (Reads Serial, Exposes Web Routes)** ```python from flask import Flask, jsonify, request, render_template import serial app = Flask(__name__) arduino = serial.Serial('COM3', 9600, timeout=1) # Linux: '/dev/ttyUSB0' def read_temperature(): line = arduino.readline().decode('utf-8', errors='ignore').strip() if line.startswith("T:"): return float(line[2:]) return None @app.route('/') def index(): return render_template('index.html') @app.route('/api/sensor') def sensor_data(): t = read_temperature() return jsonify({"temperature": t, "unit": "C"}) @app.route('/api/control', methods=['POST']) def control(): cmd = request.json.get("state") arduino.write(f"FAN:{cmd}\n".encode()) return jsonify({"status": "ok", "command": cmd}) if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000, debug=True) ``` **Step 3 — HTML Front-End (`templates/index.html`)** ```html Live Temperature Monitor

Sensor Dashboard

Temperature: --

``` **Step 4 — Communication Flow Trace** | Time (s) | Event | Direction | |---|---|---| | 0.0 | Browser requests `GET /` | Client → Server | | 0.05 | Server returns HTML page | Server → Client | | 2.0 | JS fires `GET /api/sensor` | Client → Server | | 2.01 | Backend reads Serial from Arduino | Server → Arduino | | 2.02 | Backend returns `{"temperature": 28.5}` | Server → Client | | 2.05 | JS updates `#temp` text | DOM update | | 5.0 | User clicks "ON" → `POST /api/control` | Client → Server | | 5.01 | Backend writes `FAN:ON` to Serial | Server → Arduino | | 5.02 | Relay triggers, fan starts | Hardware | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System Block Diagram — Sensor Web Application ```mermaid flowchart LR user1[User Browser] -->|HTTP GET| flask1[Flask Web Server] flask1 -->|HTML+JS| user1 user1 -->|HTTP POST Control| flask1 flask1 -->|Serial UART| arduino1[Arduino Uno] arduino1 -->|Analog Read| lm35[LM35 Temperature Sensor] lm35 -->|Voltage| arduino1 arduino1 -->|Digital Write| relay1[Relay Module] relay1 -->|Switched Mains| fan1[Cooling Fan] flask1 -->|JSON Response| user1 style flask1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style arduino1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style user1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Data Flow Diagram (DFD) — Level 1 ```mermaid flowchart TD browserA[Browser Client] -->|Sensor Request D1| serverA[Flask Backend] serverA -->|Serial Command D2| hwA[Arduino Hardware] hwA -->|Raw ADC D3| serverA serverA -->|Processed JSON D4| browserA browserA -->|User Click D5| serverA serverA -->|Actuator Trigger D6| hwA hwA -->|Physical Action D7| actuatorA[Relay / LED / Motor] actuatorA -->|Status Feedback D8| hwA style browserA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style serverA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style hwA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Request-Response Sequence (REST API) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Browser participant F as Flask Server participant A as Arduino participant S as LM35 Sensor participant R as Relay + Fan U->>F: GET / (load page) F-->>U: HTML + JS loop Every 2 seconds U->>F: GET /api/sensor F->>A: Serial: READ? A->>S: analogRead(A0) S-->>A: ADC value A-->>F: T:28.5 F-->>U: JSON {temp: 28.5} end U->>F: POST /api/control {state:ON} F->>A: Serial: FAN:ON A->>R: digitalWrite(HIGH) R-->>A: Fan running A-->>F: ACK F-->>U: JSON {status:ok} ``` ### 4.4 Three-Tier Architecture Overview ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TIER1["Presentation Layer (Front-End)"] HTML1[HTML5 Pages] CSS1[CSS3 / Bootstrap] JS1[JavaScript / AJAX / Chart.js] end subgraph TIER2["Application Layer (Back-End)"] ROUTES[Flask Routes] LOGIC[Data Processing Logic] COMM[Serial / MQTT / GPIO] end subgraph TIER3["Hardware Layer (Physical)"] MCU[Arduino / ESP32 / Raspberry Pi] SENSORS[Sensor Array] ACTUATORS[Relay / Motor / LED] end HTML1 --> ROUTES JS1 --> ROUTES ROUTES --> LOGIC LOGIC --> COMM COMM --> MCU MCU --> SENSORS MCU --> ACTUATORS style TIER1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style TIER2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style TIER3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** What is the role of AJAX polling in a web-based sensor monitoring application? **Model Answer:** AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML) polling is a technique where the client-side JavaScript periodically sends asynchronous HTTP requests to the server (e.g., every 1–2 seconds) to fetch updated sensor values without reloading the entire webpage. This enables **real-time-like** display of sensor data in the browser, reduces bandwidth by transferring only JSON payloads, and provides a smoother user experience. It is a lightweight alternative to WebSockets for low-frequency monitoring. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** List any two communication protocols commonly used to send sensor data from a microcontroller to a web server. **Model Answer:** 1. **HTTP/REST (POST/GET over TCP)** — Simplest, uses JSON payloads, works on Flask/Django backends. 2. **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** — Lightweight publish-subscribe protocol over TCP port 1883, ideal for low-bandwidth IoT. *(Other acceptable: WebSocket, CoAP, Serial UART)* --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier architecture** used to build a web application for monitoring temperature using an LM35 sensor and Arduino. (7 marks) **(b)** Write the **complete Python Flask backend code** and corresponding **HTML front-end** to read the temperature from Arduino via Serial and display it on a browser, refreshing every 2 seconds. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Three-Tier Architecture (7 marks):** The three-tier architecture consists of: - **Tier 1 – Presentation Layer:** Browser-based HTML/CSS/JS dashboard that displays real-time temperature and provides control buttons. - **Tier 2 – Application Layer:** Python Flask server running on a PC or Raspberry Pi. It hosts routes (`/`, `/api/sensor`, `/api/control`), reads the Serial port, packages data as JSON, and bridges HTTP requests to hardware commands. - **Tier 3 – Hardware Layer:** Arduino Uno with LM35 sensor on analog pin A0 and a relay-controlled fan on digital pin 8. A neat block diagram should be drawn showing the three layers with arrows indicating request flow (browser → server → Arduino) and response flow (sensor → Arduino → server → browser). Mention the data format (**JSON**) and communication medium (**Serial UART @ 9600 baud**). **(b) Complete Code (7 marks):** **Backend (`app.py`):** ```python from flask import Flask, jsonify, request, render_template import serial app = Flask(__name__) ser = serial.Serial('COM3', 9600, timeout=1) @app.route('/') def home(): return render_template('index.html') @app.route('/api/temp') def get_temp(): line = ser.readline().decode('utf-8', errors='ignore').strip() if line.startswith("T:"): return jsonify({"temp": float(line[2:]), "unit": "C"}) return jsonify({"temp": None}) if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000) ``` **Arduino Sketch:** ```cpp void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int adc = analogRead(A0); float t = adc * (5.0 / 1023.0) * 100.0; Serial.print("T:"); Serial.println(t); delay(1000); } ``` **Frontend (`templates/index.html`):** ```html

Live Temperature

--

``` **Explanation of Flow:** Browser loads HTML → JS polls `/api/temp` every 2s → Flask reads serial line → Returns JSON → DOM updates. The student must explain the data flow, error handling, and refresh mechanism to get full marks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] (Alternative Choice) **(a)** Explain the difference between **HTTP polling, AJAX polling, and WebSockets** for real-time sensor data delivery. Which is most suitable for a smart agriculture dashboard updating every 500 ms? Justify. (7 marks) **(b)** Design a web application architecture for a **gas leak detection system** using an **MQ-2 sensor**, a **buzzer actuator**, and a **Raspberry Pi**. Provide the JSON structure, route design, and a sequence diagram description. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison (7 marks):** | Method | Mechanism | Latency | Server Load | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | HTTP Polling | Full page reload on each request | Very High | Very High | Legacy systems | | AJAX Polling | JS fires async XMLHttpRequest / fetch periodically | Moderate | Moderate | Low-frequency dashboards (2–5 s) | | WebSockets | Persistent full-duplex TCP connection | Very Low | Low | High-frequency / real-time (sub-second) | **Best Choice for 500 ms Updates:** **WebSockets.** AJAX polling at 500 ms would create 2 requests/second per client, quickly saturating the server. WebSockets maintain a single persistent connection and push data only when the sensor value changes, offering sub-100 ms latency with minimal overhead. Libraries like **Flask-SocketIO** or **Node.js ws** are commonly used. **(b) Gas Leak Detection Architecture (7 marks):** - **Hardware:** Raspberry Pi 4, MQ-2 sensor on GPIO pin 17, Buzzer on GPIO pin 27, Status LED on GPIO 22. - **Backend Stack:** Flask + Flask-SocketIO + `RPi.GPIO` library. - **JSON Structure for Sensor Reading:** ```json { "sensor_id": "MQ2_01", "gas_ppm": 425, "threshold": 300, "status": "ALARM", "timestamp": "2024-12-15T14:32:11Z" } ``` - **Route Design:** - `GET /` → Main dashboard with live chart. - `GET /api/status` → Current gas reading. - `POST /api/buzzer` → `{state: "ON"/"OFF"}` to control buzzer. - `WS /socket.io` → Real-time push of sensor data. - **Sequence:** Sensor polled every 500 ms → Threshold check → If `gas_ppm > 300`, emit `alarm` event via WebSocket → Client plays beep sound and turns LED red → User clicks "Silence" → `POST /api/buzzer` → Backend drives GPIO LOW. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting CORS** when front-end and back-end run on different ports — browser blocks fetch calls silently. > 2. **Confusing GET vs POST** semantics — GET should never change server state (e.g., never use GET to "turn ON" a fan). > 3. **Hardcoding COM ports** without providing a configuration file — fails when port changes. > 4. **Missing exception handling** around `serial.readline()` — code crashes on disconnect. > 5. **Not mentioning baud rate matching** between Arduino (`Serial.begin`) and Python (`Serial('COMx', 9600)`) — common silent failure. > 6. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** — examiner deducts 1–2 marks for ambiguous flow. > 7. **Using `http` instead of `https`** in deployment — acceptable in lab, but loses marks in viva on security. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Web App for Sensors = Browser (HMI) + Server (Logic) + Hardware (Senses/Acts).** - **Flask is the most common KTU-recommended backend** — lightweight, Pythonic, easy to integrate with PySerial. - **AJAX polling** is acceptable for lab demos (2–5 s refresh); **WebSockets/MQTT** are advanced alternatives. - **JSON is the lingua franca** — always respond with `jsonify({...})` from Flask. - **Arduino-side protocol:** Print prefixed strings like `T:28.5`, `FAN:ON` — backend parses prefix to determine command/data type. - **ADC math:** $V_{\text{in}} = A_{\text{ADC}} \times \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n - 1}$ — for LM35, multiply $V_{\text{in}}$ by 100 to get °C. - **Common ports to remember:** Flask default = **5000**, HTTP = **80**, MQTT = **1883**, Serial baud = **9600**. - **Three-tier architecture** (Presentation / Application / Hardware) is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** — draw clearly with labeled arrows. - **REST verbs:** GET (read), POST (create/trigger), PUT (update), DELETE (remove). - **Security pitfall:** For lab demos, run on `0.0.0.0` (open) is fine; for production, add **authentication** (basic auth or token-based). - **Examiner loves:** Block diagrams, sequence diagrams, JSON examples, complete working code snippets, and clear data flow descriptions. ---
